html:
[1]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 拾遺記 - Title of the text, meaning 'Record of Lost and Forgotten Things.'
  • 撰 - Means 'to compile' or 'to write.'
  • 錄 - Means 'to record' or 'to compile.'
  • 序 - Means 'preface' or 'introduction.'
  • 卷 - Means 'scroll' or 'volume.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 春皇庖牺 - Fuxi, a legendary figure in Chinese mythology, credited with the invention of the trigrams used in the I Ching.
  • 炎帝神农 - Shennong, a legendary figure known for his contributions to agriculture and medicine.
  • 轩辕黄帝 - The Yellow Emperor, a central figure in Chinese mythology, considered the ancestor of the Chinese people.
  • 少昊 - Shao Hao, a legendary ruler associated with the development of early Chinese culture.
  • 颛顼 - Zhuan Xu, a legendary ruler known for his reforms and contributions to Chinese civilization.
  • 高辛 - Emperor Ku, a legendary ruler who is said to have established the Xia dynasty.
  • 唐尧 - Emperor Yao, a legendary ruler known for his wisdom and virtue.
  • 虞舜 - Emperor Shun, a legendary ruler known for his filial piety and benevolence.
  • 夏禹 - Yu the Great, a legendary ruler credited with controlling the great flood and founding the Xia dynasty.
  • 殷汤 - King Tang of Shang, a legendary ruler who overthrew the Xia dynasty and founded the Shang dynasty.
  • 周 - The Zhou dynasty, one of the longest-lasting dynasties in Chinese history, known for its cultural and philosophical contributions.
  • 周穆王 - King Mu of Zhou, a legendary ruler known for his travels and the compilation of the 'Classic of Mountains and Seas.'
  • 鲁僖公 - A ruler of the state of Lu during the Spring and Autumn period, known for his diplomatic skills.
  • 周灵王 - A ruler of the Zhou dynasty, known for his reign during a period of decline.
  • 燕昭王 - A ruler of the state of Yan during the Warring States period, known for his efforts to strengthen his state.
  • 秦始皇 - The first emperor of China, known for unifying the country and standardizing various systems.
  • 前汉上 - The early part of the Han dynasty, known for its prosperity and cultural achievements.
  • 前汉下 - The later part of the Han dynasty, marked by political and social changes.
  • 后汉 - The Eastern Han dynasty, known for its cultural and intellectual contributions.
  • 魏 - The state of Wei during the Three Kingdoms period, one of the three major states that emerged after the fall of the Han dynasty.
  • 吴 - The state of Wu during the Three Kingdoms period, known for its naval power and resistance against Wei.

Proper Nouns

  • 拾遺記 - Title of the text.
  • 王嘉 - The author of the text.
  • 萧绮 - The editor of the text.
  • 春皇庖牺 - Fuxi.
  • 炎帝神农 - Shennong.
  • 轩辕黄帝 - The Yellow Emperor.
  • 少昊 - Shao Hao.
  • 颛顼 - Zhuan Xu.
  • 高辛 - Emperor Ku.
  • 唐尧 - Emperor Yao.
  • 虞舜 - Emperor Shun.
  • 夏禹 - Yu the Great.
  • 殷汤 - King Tang of Shang.
  • 周 - The Zhou dynasty.
  • 周穆王 - King Mu of Zhou.
  • 鲁僖公 - A ruler of the state of Lu.
  • 周灵王 - A ruler of the Zhou dynasty.
  • 燕昭王 - A ruler of the state of Yan.
  • 秦始皇 - Qin Shi Huang.
  • 前汉上 - The early part of the Han dynasty.
  • 前汉下 - The later part of the Han dynasty.
  • 后汉 - The Eastern Han dynasty.
  • 魏 - The state of Wei.
  • 吴 - The state of Wu.

[2]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 蜀 - Ancient state of Shu, located in what is now Sichuan province.
  • 卷九 - Chapter Nine in a classical Chinese text.
  • 晋时事 - Matters of the Jin Dynasty, referring to historical events or records from the Jin period.
  • 诸名山 - Various famous mountains, indicating a list of notable mountains.
  • 昆仑山 - Mount Kunlun, one of the most famous mythical mountains in Chinese mythology.
  • 蓬莱山 - Penglai Mountain, another mythical mountain associated with immortality.
  • 方丈山 - Fangzhang Mountain, a mythical mountain mentioned in ancient texts.
  • 瀛洲 - Yingzhou, a mythical island in the Eastern Sea.
  • 员峤山 - Yuanqiao Mountain, a mythical mountain.
  • 岱舆山 - Daiyu Mountain, a mythical mountain.
  • 昆吾山 - Kunwu Mountain, a mythical mountain.
  • 洞庭山 - Dongting Mountain, a real mountain in Hunan province, but also associated with myth and legend.
  • 序 - Preface or Introduction to the text.
  • 《拾遗记》者，晋陇西安阳人王嘉字子年所撰 - The *Shiyi Ji* is a work by Wang Jia, courtesy name Zini, from Anyang, Longxi, during the Jin Dynasty.
  • 凡十九卷，二百二十篇，皆为残缺 - It consists of nineteen volumes and two hundred and twenty chapters, all of which are incomplete.
  • 当伪秦之季，王纲迁号，五都沦覆 - During the late period of the false Qin, the imperial authority was displaced, and the five capitals were destroyed.
  • 河洛之地，没为戎墟，宫室榛芜，书藏堙毁 - The land of the Yellow and Luo rivers became a barbarian wasteland, palaces and houses were overgrown with weeds, and the imperial library was destroyed.
  • 荆棘霜露，岂独悲于前王；鞠为禾黍，弥深嗟于兹代 - Thorns and frost, how can they only sadden the former kings; fields turned into grain, how much more sorrowful for this age.
  • 故使典章散灭，黉馆焚埃，皇图帝册，殆无一存 - Thus, the imperial decrees and records were scattered and destroyed, schools and academies were burned to ashes, and the imperial records and charts were almost entirely lost.
  • 故此书多有亡散 - Therefore, this book has many missing parts.
  • 文起羲、炎已来，事讫西晋之末 - The text begins from the era of Fuxi and Yan Emperor and ends with the late Western Jin Dynasty.
  • 五运因循，十有四代 - The five dynastic cycles continued, spanning fourteen generations.
  • 王子年乃搜撰异同，而殊怪必举 - Zini Wang compiled the differences and similarities, and all unusual and strange matters were included.
  • 纪事存朴，爱广尚奇 - The record of events is simple and straightforward, loving breadth and valuing the strange.
  • 宪章稽古之文，绮综编杂之部 - It follows the ancient rules of writing and combines various texts.
  • 《山海经》所不载，夏鼎未之或存 - What is not recorded in the *Shanhaijing* and what is not preserved in the Xia tripod.
  • 乃集而记矣 - Thus, it was collected and recorded.
  • 辞趣过诞，意旨迂阔 - The language is overly fantastical, and the meaning is remote and broad.
  • 推理陈迹，恨为繁冗 - Explaining the reasons and recounting the past, it is regrettable for its verbosity.
  • 多涉祯祥之书，博采神仙之事 - It often involves books of auspicious signs and widely collects stories of immortals.
  • 妙万物而为言，盖绝世而弘博矣 - It speaks of all things in a marvelous way, truly extraordinary and extensive.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 蜀 - An ancient state in Sichuan, known for its rich history and culture.
  • 晋时事 - Historical events or records from the Jin Dynasty, a significant period in Chinese history.
  • 诸名山 - Various famous mountains in Chinese mythology and geography.
  • 昆仑山 - A mythical mountain in Chinese mythology, often associated with the divine.
  • 蓬莱山 - A mythical mountain associated with immortality and the Eastern Sea.
  • 方丈山 - A mythical mountain mentioned in ancient texts, often associated with the sea.
  • 瀛洲 - A mythical island in the Eastern Sea, often associated with immortality.
  • 员峤山 - A mythical mountain, often associated with the sea.
  • 岱舆山 - A mythical mountain, often associated with the sea.
  • 昆吾山 - A mythical mountain, often associated with the sea.
  • 洞庭山 - A real mountain in Hunan province, but also associated with myth and legend.
  • 序 - A formal introduction to a text, often providing background and context.
  • 《拾遗记》 - A book compiled by Wang Jia, containing various myths, legends, and historical anecdotes.
  • 伪秦之季 - The late period of the false Qin, referring to a historical event where the imperial authority was displaced.
  • 五都沦覆 - The destruction of the five capitals, referring to a historical event.
  • 河洛之地 - The land of the Yellow and Luo rivers, a significant region in Chinese history.
  • 王纲迁号 - The displacement of the imperial authority, referring to a historical event.
  • 黉馆焚埃 - The burning of schools and academies, referring to a historical event.
  • 皇图帝册 - Imperial records and charts, referring to important historical documents.
  • 羲、炎 - Fuxi and Yan Emperor, legendary figures in Chinese mythology.
  • 五运因循 - The five dynastic cycles, referring to the cyclical nature of Chinese history.
  • 十有四代 - Fourteen generations, referring to the span of time covered by the book.
  • 王子年 - Wang Jia, the author of the *Shiyi Ji*.
  • 《山海经》 - The *Shanhaijing*, an ancient Chinese text containing myths, legends, and geographical descriptions.
  • 夏鼎 - The Xia tripod, an ancient Chinese artifact often associated with historical records.
  • 祯祥之书 - Books of auspicious signs, often containing predictions and omens.
  • 神仙之事 - Stories of immortals, often containing myths and legends.

[3]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 世德陵夷 - Classical phrase meaning 'the decline of family virtue' or 'the deterioration of ancestral virtue.'
  • 文颇缺略 - Means 'the text is somewhat lacking' or 'the text is incomplete.'
  • 绮更删其繁紊 - Means 'Qi further pruned the complexity and disorder.'
  • 纪其实美 - Means 'to record its true merits.'
  • 搜刊幽秘 - Means 'to search out and publish hidden secrets.'
  • 捃采残落 - Means 'to gather and collect scattered remnants.'
  • 言匪浮诡 - Means 'words are not frivolous or bizarre.'
  • 事弗空诬 - Means 'the matters are not false or slanderous.'
  • 推详往迹 - Means 'to carefully examine past events.'
  • 影彻经史 - Means 'to penetrate the classics and histories.'
  • 考验真怪 - Means 'to test the true and strange.'
  • 叶附图籍 - Means 'to align with the records and documents.'
  • 道业远者 - Means 'those whose way of life is distant.'
  • 辞省朴素 - Means 'words are concise and simple.'
  • 世德近者 - Means 'those whose family virtue is recent.'
  • 文存靡丽 - Means 'the text retains its ornate beauty.'
  • 编言贯物 - Means 'to compile words and connect them to objects.'
  • 宛然成章 - Means 'to form a coherent and elegant chapter.'
  • 数运则与世推移 - Means 'the fate is aligned with the times.'
  • 风政则因时回改 - Means 'the customs and policies change with the times.'
  • 金绳鸟篆之文 - Refers to ancient script resembling bird patterns.
  • 玉牒虫章之字 - Refers to ancient script resembling insect patterns.
  • 末代流传 - Means 'passed down through later generations.'
  • 多乖曩迹 - Means 'often deviating from the original traces.'
  • 虽探研镌写 - Means 'even though studied and engraved.'
  • 抑多疑误 - Means 'still often doubted and mistaken.'
  • 及言乎政化 - Means 'when it comes to politics and governance.'
  • 讹乎祯祥 - Means 'the signs of good fortune are often false.'
  • 随代而次之 - Means 'arranged according to the dynasties.'
  • 土地山川之域 - Refers to regions of land and mountains.
  • 或以名例相疑 - Means 'sometimes confused by names and examples.'
  • 草木鸟兽之类 - Refers to plants, animals, and birds.
  • 亦以声状相惑 - Means 'also confused by sounds and appearances.'
  • 随所载而区别 - Means 'distinguished according to what is recorded.'
  • 各因方而释之 - Means 'explained according to the local context.'
  • 宁可采于一说 - Means 'it is not possible to adopt a single opinion.'
  • 今搜检残遗 - Means 'now searching for and collecting the remnants.'
  • 合为一部 - Means 'combined into one volume.'
  • 凡一十卷 - Means 'a total of ten volumes.'
  • 序而录焉 - Means 'to preface and record it.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 绮 - Refers to Qi, likely a historical figure or scholar.
  • 金绳鸟篆之文，玉牒虫章之字 - Refers to ancient scripts and inscriptions, often associated with imperial or religious texts.
  • 末代流传 - Implies a continuity of tradition through time.
  • 多乖曩迹 - Suggests a divergence from earlier, more authentic forms.
  • 随代而次之 - Indicates a historical arrangement, likely organized by dynastic periods.
  • 土地山川之域 - Refers to geographical regions, often with cultural or political significance.
  • 草木鸟兽之类 - Refers to natural elements, often with symbolic or practical importance.
  • 随所载而区别 - Implies a method of classification based on recorded information.
  • 各因方而释之 - Suggests a localized or contextual interpretation.
  • 宁可采于一说 - Suggests a pluralistic approach to understanding.
  • 今搜检残遗 - Implies a process of historical or textual recovery.
  • 合为一部 - Suggests a compilation or synthesis of diverse materials.
  • 凡一十卷 - Indicates the scale of the work.
  • 序而录焉 - Suggests a formal presentation or publication.

Proper Nouns

  • 绮 - A historical figure or scholar, likely involved in the editing or compilation of the text.
  • 金绳鸟篆之文，玉牒虫章之字 - Ancient scripts and inscriptions, often associated with imperial or religious texts.
  • 末代流传 - Implies a continuity of tradition through time.
  • 多乖曩迹 - Suggests a divergence from earlier, more authentic forms.
  • 随代而次之 - Indicates a historical arrangement, likely organized by dynastic periods.
  • 土地山川之域 - Refers to geographical regions, often with cultural or political significance.
  • 草木鸟兽之类 - Refers to natural elements, often with symbolic or practical importance.
  • 随所载而区别 - Implies a method of classification based on recorded information.
  • 各因方而释之 - Suggests a localized or contextual interpretation.
  • 宁可采于一说 - Suggests a pluralistic approach to understanding.
  • 今搜检残遗 - Implies a process of historical or textual recovery.
  • 合为一部 - Suggests a compilation or synthesis of diverse materials.
  • 凡一十卷 - Indicates the scale of the work.
  • 序而录焉 - Suggests a formal presentation or publication.

[4]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 春皇 - Title or epithet for Fuxi (庖牺), often translated as 'Spring Emperor' or 'Emperor of the Spring.'
  • 庖牺之别号 - Means 'another name for Fuxi,' indicating that '春皇' is an alternative title.
  • 所都之国 - Refers to the country where Fuxi resided.
  • 华胥之洲 - A mythical place, often translated as 'Huaxu Island' or 'Land of Huaxu.'
  • 神母游其上 - Means 'the divine mother walked upon it,' referring to a supernatural event.
  • 青虹绕神母 - Describes a green rainbow encircling the divine mother, a sign of divine presence or blessing.
  • 觉有娠 - Means 'she became pregnant,' indicating the divine conception.
  • 历十二年而生庖牺 - Means 'after twelve years, she gave birth to Fuxi,' emphasizing the long gestation period.
  • 长头修目 - Describes Fuxi with a long head and long eyes, indicating his extraordinary appearance.
  • 龟齿龙唇 - Means 'tortoise teeth and dragon lips,' further emphasizing his unique and mythical features.
  • 眉有白毫 - Means 'white hair on his eyebrows,' another distinctive feature.
  • 须垂委地 - Means 'his beard hangs down to the ground,' indicating his long and flowing beard.
  • 岁星十二年一周天 - Refers to the twelve-year cycle of Jupiter (岁星) completing one orbit around the sky, aligning with the gestation period.
  • 圣人生皆有祥瑞 - Means 'when a sage is born, there are always auspicious signs,' highlighting the divine nature of Fuxi's birth.
  • 人皇蛇身九首 - Refers to the mythological figure Renhuang (人皇) with a snake body and nine heads, indicating a powerful and ancient ruler.
  • 肇自开辟 - Means 'since the beginning of creation,' emphasizing the primordial origin.
  • 日月重轮 - Means 'the sun and moon have double wheels,' a metaphor for a harmonious and prosperous era.
  • 山明海静 - Means 'mountains are bright and seas are calm,' indicating a peaceful and stable environment.
  • 为陵成谷 - Means 'mountains become valleys and valleys become mountains,' describing significant changes over time.
  • 世历推移 - Means 'the world undergoes changes over time,' indicating the passage of time and transformation.
  • 比于圣德 - Means 'compared to his sage virtue,' highlighting Fuxi's moral and intellectual superiority.
  • 有逾前皇 - Means 'he surpasses previous emperors,' indicating his exceptional status.
  • 礼义文物 - Refers to rites, morality, and cultural artifacts, indicating the establishment of civilization.
  • 于兹始作 - Means 'from this point, they were established,' emphasizing the beginning of these cultural elements.
  • 去巢穴之居 - Means 'to abandon cave-dwelling,' indicating the transition from primitive living to more advanced housing.
  • 变茹腥之食 - Means 'to change from eating raw meat,' indicating the development of cooking and food preparation.
  • 立礼教以导文 - Means 'to establish rites and teachings to guide culture,' emphasizing the role of education and social norms.
  • 造干戈以饰武 - Means 'to create weapons to adorn martial arts,' indicating the development of military technology and strategy.
  • 丝桑为瑟 - Means 'to make silk and mulberry trees into zithers,' indicating the creation of musical instruments.
  • 均土为埙 - Means 'to make clay into ocarinas,' indicating the creation of another musical instrument.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 春皇庖牺 - Fuxi, the legendary first ruler of China, known for his contributions to Chinese civilization.
  • 华胥之洲 - A mythical place associated with the birth of Fuxi, often linked to the concept of a utopian or divine land.
  • 神母 - The divine mother, often associated with the goddess Nüwa, who is said to have created humans and repaired the sky.
  • 青虹 - A green rainbow, a symbol of divine presence or blessing.
  • 岁星 - Jupiter, known in Chinese astronomy as the 'Year Star,' which completes one orbit around the sky every twelve years.
  • 人皇 - The mythological figure Renhuang, often depicted with a snake body and nine heads, representing a powerful and ancient ruler.
  • 日月重轮 - A metaphor for a harmonious and prosperous era, often used in classical Chinese literature to describe a golden age.
  • 山明海静 - A poetic description of a peaceful and stable environment, often used to evoke a sense of tranquility and harmony.
  • 为陵成谷 - A metaphor for significant changes over time, often used to describe the passage of time and the transformation of the natural world.
  • 世历推移 - A phrase indicating the passage of time and the continuous changes that occur in the world.
  • 礼义文物 - Rites, morality, and cultural artifacts, which are fundamental to the development of Chinese civilization.
  • 丝桑为瑟 - The creation of zithers from silk and mulberry trees, indicating the development of musical instruments.
  • 均土为埙 - The creation of ocarinas from clay, indicating the development of another musical instrument.

Proper Nouns

  • 春皇 - Spring Emperor or Emperor of the Spring.
  • 庖牺 - Fuxi, the legendary first ruler of China.
  • 华胥之洲 - Huaxu Island or Land of Huaxu.
  • 神母 - Divine mother, often associated with Nüwa.
  • 青虹 - Green rainbow.
  • 岁星 - Jupiter, the Year Star.
  • 人皇 - Renhuang, the mythological figure with a snake body and nine heads.
  • 日月重轮 - A metaphor for a harmonious and prosperous era.
  • 山明海静 - A poetic description of a peaceful and stable environment.
  • 为陵成谷 - A metaphor for significant changes over time.
  • 世历推移 - The passage of time and continuous changes.
  • 礼义文物 - Rites, morality, and cultural artifacts.
  • 丝桑为瑟 - Zithers made from silk and mulberry trees.
  • 均土为埙 - Ocarinas made from clay.

[5]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 禮樂於是興矣 - Classical expression meaning 'the rites and music were thus established,' marking the beginning of cultural and social order.
  • 調和八風 - Means 'to harmonize the eight winds,' a metaphor for aligning the natural elements or forces.
  • 以畫八卦 - Means 'to draw the eight trigrams,' referring to the creation of the *Bagua* (八卦) in Chinese cosmology.
  • 分六位以正六宗 - Means 'to divide into six positions to correct the six ancestral spirits,' indicating the establishment of ritual hierarchy.
  • 未有書契 - Means 'there were no written records,' referring to a time before the invention of writing.
  • 規天為圖，矩地取法 - Means 'to measure the heavens and draw diagrams, to square the earth and take its measure,' describing the act of observing and recording the cosmos.
  • 視五星之文，分晷景之度 - Means 'to observe the patterns of the five planets and divide the degrees of the sun's shadow,' referring to astronomical observation.
  • 使鬼神以致群祠 - Means 'to summon the spirits to all the shrines,' indicating the role of rituals in connecting the human and divine realms.
  • 審地勢以定川岳 - Means 'to examine the terrain to determine the rivers and mountains,' referring to the establishment of geographical and ritual boundaries.
  • 始嫁娶以修人道 - Means 'to begin marriage to cultivate human relationships,' marking the institution of social order.
  • 庖者，包也 - Play on words: '庖' (庖) means 'cooking' or 'butchering,' while '包' (包) means 'to contain' or 'to embrace,' suggesting that the figure's role encompasses all things.
  • 言包含万象 - Means 'to contain all things,' emphasizing the figure's comprehensive influence.
  • 以牺牲登荐于百神 - Means 'to offer sacrifices to all the gods,' referring to the practice of ritual offerings.
  • 民服其圣，故曰庖牺 - Means 'the people revered him as a sage, hence the name *Paotse* (庖牺),' explaining the origin of the figure's name.
  • 亦谓伏羲 - Means 'also called *Fuxi* (伏羲),' noting an alternative name for the figure.
  • 变混沌之质 - Means 'to transform the chaotic state,' referring to the figure's role in bringing order to the universe.
  • 文宓其教 - Means 'to civilize and educate,' indicating the figure's role in cultural development.
  • 故曰宓牺 - Means 'hence the name *Mifuxi* (宓牺),' explaining another name for the figure.
  • 布至德于天下 - Means 'to spread great virtue throughout the world,' emphasizing the figure's moral influence.
  • 元元之类，莫不尊焉 - Means 'all beings, without exception, revered him,' highlighting the figure's universal respect.
  • 以木德称王 - Means 'to rule with the virtue of wood,' referring to the figure's association with the element of wood in the Five Elements theory.
  • 故曰春皇 - Means 'hence the name *Spring Emperor* (春皇),' explaining another title for the figure.
  • 其明睿照于八区 - Means 'his wisdom and insight illuminated the eight regions,' emphasizing the figure's far-reaching influence.
  • 是谓太昊 - Means 'this is called *Taihao* (太昊),' explaining another title for the figure.
  • 昊者，明也 - Means 'Taihao means 'bright',' clarifying the meaning of the title.
  • 位居东方 - Means 'he occupied the eastern position,' referring to the figure's association with the east in cosmology.
  • 以含养蠢化 - Means 'to nurture and cultivate the crude and unformed,' indicating the figure's role in shaping the world.
  • 叶于木德 - Means 'in harmony with the virtue of wood,' reinforcing the figure's association with the element of wood.
  • 其音附角 - Means 'his music was associated with the *jiao* (角) pitch,' referring to the figure's connection to the musical scale.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 禮樂 - Refers to the rituals and music that form the foundation of Chinese civilization.
  • 八卦 - The eight trigrams of the *Yijing* (Book of Changes), a key text in Chinese cosmology.
  • 六宗 - Refers to the six ancestral spirits in Chinese ritual tradition.
  • 书契 - Refers to written records, marking the transition from oral to written culture.
  • 规天为图，矩地取法 - Refers to the ancient practice of observing and recording the heavens and earth, foundational to Chinese astronomy and geography.
  • 五星之文 - Refers to the five visible planets (Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn) in Chinese astronomy.
  • 晷景之度 - Refers to the measurement of the sun's shadow, used in ancient timekeeping and astronomy.
  • 鬼神 - Refers to spirits and deities in Chinese religious and ritual tradition.
  • 群祠 - Refers to multiple shrines or temples where rituals are performed.
  • 川岳 - Refers to rivers and mountains, key geographical features in Chinese cosmology.
  • 嫁娶 - Refers to the institution of marriage, a cornerstone of social order.
  • 庖牺 - Refers to *Paotse* (庖牺), an alternative name for the legendary figure *Fuxi* (伏羲).
  • 伏羲 - Refers to *Fuxi* (伏羲), a legendary figure in Chinese mythology, often associated with the creation of writing, music, and social order.
  • 混沌之质 - Refers to the primordial, formless state of the universe before creation.
  • 文宓其教 - Refers to the figure's role in civilizing and educating humanity.
  • 至德 - Refers to great virtue, a key concept in Confucian ethics.
  • 木德 - Refers to the virtue of wood in the Five Elements theory, associated with growth, flexibility, and renewal.
  • 春皇 - Refers to the 'Spring Emperor,' a title associated with *Fuxi* (伏羲) in Chinese mythology.
  • 太昊 - Refers to *Taihao* (太昊), another title for *Fuxi* (伏羲), meaning 'Great Brightness.'
  • 八区 - Refers to the eight regions or directions in Chinese cosmology.
  • 木皇 - Refers to the 'Wood Emperor,' another title for *Fuxi* (伏羲), emphasizing his association with the element of wood.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 炎帝神农 - Legendary Chinese ruler and founder of Chinese medicine and agriculture, known as the 'Divine Farmer.'
  • 始教民耒耜 - Began teaching the people to use plows, with '耒耜' (lěi sì) referring to ancient farming tools.
  • 躬勤畎亩之事 - Personally worked diligently in the fields, with '畎亩' (quǎn mǔ) referring to fields or farmland.
  • 百谷滋阜 - All grains flourished abundantly, with '滋阜' (zī fù) indicating growth and prosperity.
  • 圣德所感 - The influence of his virtuous nature, with '圣德' (shèng dé) referring to the virtue of a sage ruler.
  • 无不着焉 - Nothing was left unaffected, with '着' (zhuó) implying a lasting impact.
  • 神芝发其异色 - Divine mushrooms displayed their unusual colors, with '神芝' (shén zhī) referring to mystical mushrooms.
  • 灵苗擢其嘉颖 - Spiritual plants sprouted their fine grains, with '灵苗' (líng miáo) referring to sacred or spiritual plants.
  • 陆地丹蕖 - Red lotuses on land, with '丹蕖' (dān qú) referring to red lotus flowers.
  • 骈生如盖 - Grew in pairs like umbrellas, with '骈生' (pián shēng) indicating paired growth and '如盖' (rú gài) comparing them to umbrellas.
  • 香露滴沥 - Fragrant dew dripped, with '香露' (xiāng lù) referring to fragrant dew.
  • 下流成池 - Flowed down to form a pool, with '下流' (xià liú) indicating downward flow.
  • 因为豢龙之圃 - Therefore, it became a garden for raising dragons, with '豢龙' (huàn lóng) referring to the act of raising dragons.
  • 朱草蔓衍于街衢 - Red grass spread along the streets, with '蔓衍' (màn yǎn) indicating spreading and '街衢' (jiē qú) referring to streets.
  • 卿云蔚蔼于丛薄 - Purple clouds gathered in the thickets, with '卿云' (qīng yún) referring to purple clouds and '丛薄' (cóng bó) indicating dense vegetation.
  • 筑圆丘以祀朝日 - Built a circular altar to worship the morning sun, with '圆丘' (yuán qiū) referring to a round altar and '祀' (sì) meaning to worship.
  • 饰瑶阶以揖夜光 - Adorned the jade steps to welcome the night light, with '瑶阶' (yáo jiē) referring to jade steps and '揖夜光' (yī yè guāng) meaning to welcome the night light.
  • 奏九天之和乐 - Played harmonious music of the nine heavens, with '九天' (jiǔ tiān) referring to the heavens and '和乐' (hé lè) meaning harmonious music.
  • 百兽率舞 - All beasts danced in unison, with '率舞' (shuài wǔ) indicating dancing in unison.
  • 八音克谐 - The eight tones were perfectly harmonized, with '八音' (bā yīn) referring to the eight tones of traditional Chinese music.
  • 木石润泽 - Wood and stone became moist and lustrous, with '润泽' (rùn zé) indicating moisture and luster.
  • 时有流云洒液 - Sometimes clouds flowed and scattered dew, with '流云' (liú yún) referring to flowing clouds and '洒液' (sǎ yè) meaning to scatter dew.
  • 服之得道，后天而老 - Whoever consumes it attains the Dao and ages later than others, with '得道' (dé dào) meaning to attain the Dao and '后天而老' (hòu tiān ér lǎo) meaning to age later.
  • 以暗投水，浮而不灭 - When thrown into water in the dark, it floats without sinking, with '暗投' (àn tóu) meaning to throw in the dark and '浮而不灭' (fú ér bù miè) meaning to float without sinking.
  • 时有丹雀衔九穗禾 - Sometimes a red bird carried a stalk of nine-grain rice, with '丹雀' (dān què) referring to a red bird and '九穗禾' (jiǔ suì hé) meaning a stalk of nine-grain rice.
  • 其坠地者，帝乃拾之，以植于田，食者老而不死 - When it fell to the ground, the emperor picked it up and planted it in the fields; those who ate it did not age, with '拾之' (shí zhī) meaning to pick it up and '老而不死' (lǎo ér bù sǐ) meaning to not age.
  • 采峻锾之铜以为器 - Collected copper from the Juhnhan mountain to make utensils, with '峻锾' (jùn huán) referring to a mountain and '以为器' (yǐ wéi qì) meaning to make utensils.
  • 峻锾，山名也 - Juhnhan is a mountain, with '山名' (shān míng) meaning mountain name.
  • 下有金井，白气冠其上 - There was a golden well below, with white vapor rising above it, with '金井' (jīn jǐng) referring to a golden well and '白气' (bái qì) meaning white vapor.
  • 人升于其间，雷霆之声，在于地下 - When people ascended into it, the sound of thunder was heard underground, with '升于其间' (shēng yú qí jiān) meaning to ascend into it and '雷霆之声' (léi tíng zhī shēng) meaning the sound of thunder.
  • 井中之金柔弱，可以缄縢也 - The gold in the well was soft and could be sealed, with '缄縢' (jiān téng) meaning to seal.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 谨按 - Formal expression meaning 'carefully according to' or 'based on careful examination,' used to introduce a scholarly or authoritative statement.
  • 伏羲为上古 - Refers to Fu Xi as a legendary figure from ancient times, often considered one of the Three Sovereigns (三皇) in Chinese mythology.
  • 观文于天，察理于地 - A poetic way of saying that Fu Xi observed patterns in the heavens and understood principles on earth.
  • 俯仰二仪 - Refers to looking up at the heavens (天) and down at the earth (地), symbolizing the balance between heaven and earth.
  • 经纶万象 - Means 'to weave together all things,' suggesting that Fu Xi organized the cosmos or the world.
  • 至德备于冥昧 - A philosophical statement meaning that the highest virtue was fully realized in the mysterious and unseen aspects of the universe.
  • 神化通于精粹 - Suggests that the divine transformation was connected to the finest and most refined elements of existence.
  • 图书着其迹，河洛表其文 - Refers to the legendary *Book of Changes* (易经) and the *Lo Shu Square* (洛书), which were said to have appeared on the back of a tortoise in the Yellow River.
  • 变太素之质，改淳远之化 - Means that Fu Xi transformed the primitive and simple nature of the world and changed its distant and remote transformations.
  • 三才之位既立，四维之义乃张 - Refers to the establishment of the three realms (天, 地, 人) and the extension of the four cardinal directions (四维), symbolizing the structure of the universe.
  • 礼乐文物，自兹而始 - Indicates that rituals, music, and cultural artifacts originated from this time.
  • 降于下代，渐相移袭 - Means that these traditions were passed down through generations and gradually adapted over time.
  • 备昭籍菉，编列柱史 - Suggests that these records were meticulously documented and compiled by historians.
  • 考验先经，刊详往诰 - Means that these records were carefully examined and detailed in past edicts or writings.
  • 事列方典，取征群籍 - Indicates that these records were recorded in official documents and verified by various historical sources.
  • 博采百家，求详可证 - Means that scholars gathered information from many sources to find detailed and verifiable evidence.
  • 按《山海经》云 - A reference to the *Classic of Mountains and Seas* (山海经), an ancient Chinese text that describes mythical creatures, geography, and natural phenomena.
  • 棠帝之山，出浮水玉 - Refers to a mountain called 'Tangdi Mountain' that produces 'floating water jade,' a mythical material.
  • 巫闾之地，其木多文 - Refers to the 'Wujiu' region, where the trees are said to have many patterns or markings.
  • 道真俗朴，理会冥旨 - Means that the true path is simple and unadorned, and that it aligns with deep, hidden truths.
  • 与四时齐其契，精灵协其德 - Suggests that this path is in harmony with the four seasons and the spirits, and that it shares the same virtues.
  • 祯祥之异，胡可致哉 - A rhetorical question asking how such auspicious signs and wonders could come about.
  • 故使迹感诚着，幽只不藏其宝 - Means that because of sincere actions, even the hidden spirits reveal their treasures.
  • 只心剪害，殊性之类必驯也 - Suggests that the spirits cut away harm, and that even different kinds of beings will become tamed.
  • 以降露成池，蓄龙为圃 - Refers to the creation of a pool filled with dew and a garden that houses dragons, symbolizing the transformation of nature.
  • 及乎夏代，世载绵绝 - Means that by the time of the Xia Dynasty, the lineage had continued for many generations.
  • 时有豢龙之官 - Refers to officials who were responsible for taming or raising dragons, a mythical role that reflects the importance of dragons in Chinese culture.
  • 考诸遐籍，由斯立矣 - Means that after examining ancient records, this tradition was established.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 伏羲 - A legendary figure in Chinese mythology, often credited with creating the *Eight Diagrams* (八卦) and establishing the foundations of Chinese civilization.
  • 《周易》 - The *Book of Changes*, one of the oldest and most influential texts in Chinese philosophy, attributed to Fu Xi.
  • 《山海经》 - The *Classic of Mountains and Seas*, an ancient Chinese text that combines geography, mythology, and folklore.
  • 河洛 - Refers to the Yellow River and the Luo River, both of which are associated with important mythological events in Chinese history.
  • 《八索》 - The *Eight Diagrams*, a set of symbols used in divination and cosmology.
  • 《九丘》 - Refers to the *Nine Provinces*, a symbolic representation of the early Chinese world.
  • 夏代 - The Xia Dynasty, traditionally considered the first dynasty in Chinese history.
  • 豢龙之官 - Officials responsible for taming or raising dragons, a mythical role that reflects the importance of dragons in Chinese culture.

  • 服冕垂衣 - Describes the regal attire of the Yellow Emperor, emphasizing his status as a ruler.
  • 衮龙之颂 - A dragon robe is a symbol of imperial authority and power.

  • 水物为之祥踊 - Aquatic creatures leaping in an auspicious manner is a sign of good fortune.
  • 沧海为之恬波 - Calm waves in the vast sea are a sign of peace and stability.
  • 泛河沉璧 - Floating on the river and sinking a jade disc is likely a ritual or omen.

  • 熏风至 - A warm, fragrant wind is often associated with the arrival of spring and good fortune.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 以金德王 - Formal expression meaning 'reigned with the virtue of metal,' indicating the ruling element of the Five Phases.
  • 母曰皇娥 - Means 'her mother was called Huang'e,' introducing the subject's lineage.
  • 處璇宮而夜織 - Describes Huang'e living in a palace of jade and weaving at night, symbolizing her celestial status.
  • 或乘桴木而昼游 - Means 'sometimes she would ride a raft made of wood and travel during the day,' indicating her active and varied activities.
  • 與皇娥宴戲 - Means 'and play with Huang'e,' describing the interaction between the divine child and Huang'e.
  • 奏(女更)娟之乐 - Refers to playing a type of music, where (女更)娟 is likely a specific instrument or genre of music.
  • 游漾忘归 - Means 'they would wander and forget to return,' emphasizing the idyllic and carefree nature of their interactions.
  • 窮桑者，西海之滨 - Describes a place called Qiong Sang, located on the western sea, which is a mythical location.
  • 有孤桑之树 - Means 'there is a solitary mulberry tree,' describing a significant tree in the myth.
  • 直上千寻 - Means 'reaching thousands of feet high,' emphasizing the tree's grandeur.
  • 叶红椹紫 - Describes the leaves as red and the fruits as purple, indicating the tree's vibrant appearance.
  • 万岁一实 - Means 'it bears fruit once every ten thousand years,' highlighting the tree's rarity and longevity.
  • 食之后天而老 - Means 'eating its fruit allows one to live beyond the heavens and age,' emphasizing the tree's magical properties.
  • 帝子与皇娥泛于海上 - Means 'the divine child and Huang'e sailed on the sea,' describing their journey.
  • 以桂枝为表，结熏茅为旌 - Describes the construction of a flag using cinnamon branches and incense grass, indicating a ceremonial or symbolic purpose.
  • 刻玉为鸠，置于表端 - Means 'carving a jade magpie and placing it at the top of the flag,' symbolizing the magpie's role in divination.
  • 司至 - Refers to the magpie's ability to predict the arrival of seasons, a concept from the *Spring and Autumn Annals*.
  • 今之相风 - Means 'the modern wind vane,' indicating a connection to contemporary meteorological instruments.
  • 此之遗像也 - Means 'this is a remnant of that,' linking the ancient practice to modern usage.
  • 抚桐峰梓瑟 - Describes playing a zither made of paulownia and catalpa wood, indicating the quality and type of instrument.
  • 倚瑟而清歌 - Means 'leaning on the zither and singing a clear song,' describing the act of musical performance.
  • 天清地旷浩茫茫 - Means 'the sky is clear, the earth is vast and boundless,' setting a grand and expansive scene.
  • 万象回薄化无方 - Means 'all things change and transform without limit,' emphasizing the dynamic nature of the universe.
  • 涵天荡荡望沧沧 - Means 'the sky is vast and the ocean is boundless,' reinforcing the imagery of vastness.
  • 乘桴轻漾着日傍 - Means 'riding a raft gently floating beside the sun,' describing a serene and peaceful scene.
  • 当其何所至穷桑 - Means 'where did they reach, Qiong Sang,' asking about the destination of their journey.
  • 心知和乐悦未央 - Means 'knowing in their hearts that joy and harmony are endless,' expressing a sense of perpetual contentment.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 少昊 - Refers to the legendary Yellow Emperor of the Five Phases, specifically associated with the metal element.
  • 皇娥 - A celestial figure, often associated with the moon, who is the mother of the divine child.
  • 白帝之子 - Refers to the son of the White Emperor, a figure associated with the west and autumn in Chinese mythology.
  • 太白之精 - Refers to the essence of the planet Venus, which is associated with the White Emperor and the metal element.
  • 穷桑 - A mythical place on the western sea, often associated with the end of the world or a distant, mystical land.
  • 司至 - A concept from the *Spring and Autumn Annals*, referring to the magpie's ability to predict the arrival of seasons.
  • 相风 - Refers to the modern wind vane, a device used to indicate wind direction, which has roots in ancient Chinese meteorology.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 俗谓 - Common expression meaning 'people call' or 'it is commonly said,' used to introduce a colloquial or popular term.
  • 桑中 - A term with a double meaning: it refers to a place where mulberry trees grow, but it also has a sexual connotation in classical literature.
  • 《詩》中《衛風》云 - Refers to the *Shijing* (Book of Songs), specifically the *Wei Feng* section, and introduces a quotation from it.
  • 期我乎桑中 - A phrase from the *Wei Feng* section of the *Shijing*, meaning 'meet me at the mulberry grove,' which is often interpreted as a metaphor for a romantic rendezvous.
  • 白帝子答歌 - Refers to a song or poem attributed to the son of the White Emperor, a figure from Chinese mythology.
  • 四維八埏眇难极 - A poetic expression meaning 'the four corners and eight directions are hard to reach,' symbolizing the vastness of the world.
  • 驱光逐影穷水域 - A metaphorical phrase meaning 'chasing light and shadows to the ends of the water,' suggesting the pursuit of elusive or distant goals.
  • 璇宫夜静当轩织 - A poetic description of a night scene in a palace, where women are weaving under the moonlight.
  • 桐峰文梓千寻直 - Describes tall, straight trees, symbolizing strength and nobility.
  • 伐梓作器成琴瑟 - Refers to the process of cutting down trees to make musical instruments like zithers and harps.
  • 清歌流畅乐难极 - Describes the beauty and difficulty of achieving perfect harmony in music.
  • 沧湄海浦来栖息 - Refers to the coastal regions where birds and other creatures rest, symbolizing peace and tranquility.
  • 皇娥生少昊 - Refers to the birth of Shao Hao (also known as Xiao Hao) from the legendary figure Huang E.
  • 穷桑氏 - Another name for Shao Hao, emphasizing his connection to the mulberry grove.
  • 桑丘氏 - Another name for Shao Hao, emphasizing his connection to the mulberry grove.
  • 六国时 - Refers to the Warring States period, a time of political fragmentation and conflict in Chinese history.
  • 桑丘子着阴阳书 - Refers to a person named Sang Qiu who wrote a book on the *Yin-Yang* philosophy.
  • 余裔 - Means 'descendants' or 'lineage.'
  • 金天氏 - Another name for Shao Hao, emphasizing his association with the metal element.
  • 金穷氏 - Another name for Shao Hao, emphasizing his association with the metal element.
  • 五凤 - Refers to five phoenixes, symbols of good fortune and prosperity.
  • 随方之色 - Means 'colors according to their directions,' suggesting the diversity and harmony of the phoenixes.
  • 集于帝庭 - Means 'gather in the imperial court,' symbolizing the phoenixes' presence in the presence of the emperor.
  • 凤鸟氏 - Refers to the phoenixes as a symbol of the emperor.
  • 金鸣于山，银涌于地 - Describes the natural phenomena of gold ringing in the mountains and silver flowing from the ground, symbolizing wealth and abundance.
  • 龟蛇之类 - Refers to turtles and snakes, often used in Chinese mythology to symbolize longevity and wisdom.
  • 乍似人鬼之形 - Means 'sometimes resembling human or ghostly forms,' suggesting the supernatural or mystical nature of these creatures.
  • 水屈曲亦如龙凤之状 - Describes water that twists and turns, resembling the shapes of dragons and phoenixes.
  • 山盘纡亦如屈龙之势 - Describes mountains that wind and curve, resembling the form of a coiled dragon.
  • 龙山、龟山、凤水之目也 - Refers to specific geographical features named after dragons, turtles, and phoenixes.
  • 龙丘氏 - A surname derived from the place name Longqiu, mentioned in Ban Gu's *Yi Wen Zhi*.
  • 蛇丘氏 - A surname derived from the place name Sheqiu, mentioned in *The Western Queen Mother's Divine and Strange Transmission*.

  • 奇祥众祉 - Refers to unusual signs of good fortune and blessings.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 有丹丘之国 - Classical phrase meaning 'there is a country called Danqiu,' introducing the setting of the story.
  • 献码瑙瓮 - Means 'presented a rhinoceros horn jar,' where '码瑙' is a transliteration of 'rhinoceros horn' (犀角), not 'agate' or 'agate-like stone.'
  • 帝德所洽，被于殊方 - Classical expression meaning 'the emperor's virtue permeates distant lands,' emphasizing the spread of imperial influence.
  • 以露充于厨也 - Means 'to fill the kitchen with dew,' suggesting the use of the jar to store dew or other substances.
  • 码瑙，石类也 - Means 'rhinoceros horn is a type of stone,' clarifying the material.
  • 南方者为之胜 - Means 'those from the south are superior,' indicating a preference for southern rhinoceros horns.
  • 听马鸣则别其脑色 - Means 'by listening to the horse's neigh, one can discern the color of its brain,' referring to a method of identifying horse brains.
  • 脑色黄者，日行千里 - Means 'if the brain color is yellow, it can travel a thousand miles a day.'
  • 脑色青者，嘶闻数百里 - Means 'if the brain color is green, its neigh can be heard for hundreds of miles.'
  • 脑色黑者，入水毛鬣不濡，日行五百里 - Means 'if the brain color is black, it can enter water without getting wet and travel five hundred miles a day.'
  • 脑色白者，多力而怒 - Means 'if the brain color is white, it has great strength and is fierce.'
  • 今为器多用赤色 - Means 'now, most vessels are made in red,' indicating a preference for red-colored vessels.
  • 若是人工所制者，多不成器，亦殊朴拙 - Means 'if made by humans, they often fail to be functional and are quite crude,' criticizing human-made vessels.
  • 中国人有用者，则魑魅不能逢之 - Means 'if Chinese people use them, they cannot be harmed by demons,' suggesting a protective quality.
  • 一说云，马脑者，言是恶鬼之血，凝成此物 - Means 'another saying goes that rhinoceros horn is the blood of evil spirits, coagulated into this substance,' providing an alternative explanation for the origin of rhinoceros horn.
  • 昔黄帝除蚩尤及四方群凶，并诸妖魅，填川满谷，积血成渊，聚骨如岳 - Means 'in ancient times, Emperor Huangdi defeated Chiyou and the various evil spirits, filling rivers and valleys with blood and bones,' describing a mythological event.
  • 数年中，血凝如石，骨白如灰，膏流成泉 - Means 'after several years, the blood coagulated into stone, the bones turned white like ash, and the fat flowed into springs,' describing the transformation of the battlefield.
  • 故南方有肥泉之水，有白垩之山，望之峨峨，如霜雪矣 - Means 'thus, the south has the Fatty Spring and the White Chalk Mountain, which look majestic and white like frost and snow,' describing the geographical features.
  • 又有丹丘，千年一烧，黄河千年一清，至圣之君，以为大瑞 - Means 'there is also Danqiu, which burns once every thousand years, and the Yellow River, which clears once every thousand years, considered great omens by the most virtuous rulers,' describing the significance of these natural phenomena.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 不可斫削雕琢，乃可铸以为器也 - Metaphorical expression meaning that something cannot be shaped or refined by cutting or carving; it must be cast into a form.
  • 甘露在其中，盈而不竭 - Metaphorical expression describing a substance (here, 'sweet dew') that is abundant and inexhaustible.
  • 谓之宝露 - Term used to describe the sweet dew as a precious treasure.
  • 露已渐减 - Phrase indicating that the sweet dew is gradually diminishing.
  • 随帝世之污隆 - Metaphorical expression meaning that the state of the sweet dew corresponds to the moral and political condition of the emperor's reign.
  • 时淳则露满，时浇则露竭 - Metaphorical expression indicating that the sweetness of the dew corresponds to the purity of the times.
  • 及乎三代，减于陶唐之庭 - Phrase indicating that by the time of the Three Dynasties, the amount of sweet dew had decreased compared to the reign of Emperor Yao.
  • 舜迁宝瓮于衡山之上 - Phrase indicating that Emperor Shun moved the precious vessel to Mount Heng.
  • 故衡山之岳有宝露坛 - Phrase indicating that Mount Heng has a sacred altar for the sweet dew.
  • 舜于坛下起月馆，以望夕月 - Phrase indicating that Emperor Shun built a moon pavilion at the base of the altar to observe the evening moon.
  • 舜南巡至衡山，百辟群后皆得露泉之赐 - Phrase indicating that during his southern tour, Emperor Shun bestowed the sweet dew upon all the feudal lords.
  • 时有云气生于露坛 - Phrase indicating that clouds sometimes appeared over the altar of the sweet dew.
  • 又迁宝瓮于零陵之上 - Phrase indicating that the precious vessel was moved again to Mount Lingling.
  • 舜崩，瓮沦于地下 - Phrase indicating that after Emperor Shun's death, the vessel sank into the earth.
  • 至秦始皇通汨?之流为小溪，径从长沙至零陵，掘地得赤玉瓮，可容八斗，以应八方之数 - Phrase indicating that Emperor Qin Shi Huang constructed a small stream from Changsha to Mount Lingling, where a red jade vessel capable of holding eight dou was found, corresponding to the eight directions.
  • 至后汉东方朔识之 - Phrase indicating that during the Later Han Dynasty, Dongfang Shuo recognized the vessel.
  • 朔乃作《宝瓮铭》曰“宝云生于露坛，祥风起于月馆，望三壶如盈尺，视八鸿如萦带” - Phrase indicating that Dongfang Shuo wrote an inscription for the precious vessel, describing the clouds and winds associated with it and the distant appearance of the Three Mountains and the Eight Great Oceans.
  • 三壶，则海中三山也 - Phrase indicating that the Three Mountains are the three mythical islands in the sea.
  • 一曰方壶，则方丈也；二曰蓬壶，则蓬莱也；三曰瀛壶，则瀛洲也 - Phrase indicating the names of the Three Mountains.
  • 形如壶器 - Phrase indicating that the shape of the mountains resembles that of a vessel.
  • 此三山上广、中狭、下方，皆如工制，犹华山之似削成 - Phrase indicating that the Three Mountains are broad at the top, narrow in the middle, and flat at the bottom, like artificial constructions, similar to Mount Hua.
  • 八鸿者，八方之名；鸿，大也 - Phrase indicating that the Eight Great Oceans refer to the eight cardinal directions, with '鸿' meaning 'great.'
  • 登月馆以望四海三山，皆如聚米萦带者矣 - Phrase indicating that from the moon pavilion, one can see the Four Seas and the Three Mountains as if they were small clusters of rice surrounded by ribbons.

  • 浮玉山 - A mythical mountain, possibly named for its jade-like appearance or association with jade.
  • 阴火 - Literally 'yin fire,' a term for a mysterious or supernatural fire that burns without visible fuel.
  • 火德之运 - Refers to the 'fire virtue' or 'fire era' in the Five Elements theory, symbolizing a particular historical or cosmic phase.
  • 沉燃 - A term used by those who travel the sea to describe the 'sinking flame,' possibly referring to the '阴火' phenomenon.
  • 重明之鸟 - A mythical bird, also known as '双睛' (double-eyed), symbolizing purity and protection against evil.
  • 饴以琼膏 - Means 'fed with jade-like fat,' a metaphor for nourishment or reward.
  • 今人每岁元日 - Refers to people today on the first day of the year, indicating a tradition that continues into modern times.
  • 遗像 - Means 'leftover image' or 'legacy,' referring to the enduring influence of ancient practices.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 虞舜 - Refers to Shun, the legendary sage-king of the Xia dynasty, often depicted as a paragon of virtue and governance.

  • 言则及造化之始 - Means 'their words reached the beginning of creation,' implying deep wisdom and insight into the origins of the universe.
  • 虞舜在位十年 - Indicates that Shun ruled for ten years, a specific period of his reign.
  • 置五星之祠以祭之 - Means 'established a temple to the Five Planets to worship them,' where the Five Planets are traditionally Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn.
  • 有五长星出 - Refers to the appearance of five long stars in the sky, often interpreted as a celestial sign.
  • 熏风四起 - Describes a gentle, warm breeze, often associated with the arrival of summer.
  • 连珠合璧 - A metaphor for a series of auspicious signs or events, often used in ancient Chinese texts to describe harmonious and favorable conditions.
  • 万国重译而至 - Means 'from all over the world, people came through multiple translations,' indicating the widespread influence and recognition of Shun's rule.
  • 大频之国 - Refers to a distant or far-off country, possibly a metaphor for a remote or exotic land.
  • 怀山为害 - Means 'to cause harm by holding onto mountains,' suggesting a metaphorical threat or danger.
  • 萦天 - Describes a situation where something is entangled or wrapped around the sky, often used to describe a large, threatening presence.
  • 三河 - Refers to the three major rivers: the Milky Way (天河), the Yellow River (地河), and the Central River (中河).
  • 有时通壅 - Means 'sometimes flowing and sometimes blocked,' indicating the cyclical nature of water flow.
  • 至圣之治 - Refers to the governance of a sage-king, emphasizing the ideal state of order and harmony.
  • 水色俱澄 - Means 'the water is clear and pure,' describing a state of natural beauty and purity.
  • 无有流沫 - Means 'there is no foam or turbulence,' indicating calm and stability.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 五星之祠 - The Five Planets (Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn) were considered important celestial bodies in ancient Chinese astronomy and were often worshipped.
  • 熏风 - A gentle, warm breeze, often associated with the arrival of summer and the reign of a sage-king.
  • 连珠合璧 - A metaphor for a series of auspicious signs, often used in ancient Chinese texts to describe harmonious and favorable conditions.
  • 万国重译而至 - This phrase emphasizes the widespread influence and recognition of Shun's rule, suggesting that his virtue and governance were known and respected far and wide.
  • 大频之国 - A distant or far-off country, possibly a metaphor for a remote or exotic land.
  • 怀山为害 - A metaphorical threat or danger, often used to describe a situation where something is causing harm or disruption.
  • 三河 - The three major rivers in Chinese mythology: the Milky Way, the Yellow River, and the Central River.
  • 至圣之治 - The ideal state of governance under a sage-king, characterized by order, harmony, and natural beauty.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 巨鱼吸日，蛟绕于天 - Metaphor for celestial anomalies, symbolizing great disturbances or omens.
  • 此言吸日而星雨皆坠 - Statement describing the sun being swallowed and stars raining down, somewhat true but not entirely accurate.
  • 故使后来为之回惑 - Clause indicating the impact of such stories on future generations, causing confusion.
  • 托以无稽之言 - Phrase attributing the story to baseless rumors.
  • 特取其爱博德奇之间 - Phrase highlighting the reason why such stories are told despite their lack of credibility, especially for their broad appeal and strange nature.
  • 录其广异宏丽之靡矣 - Phrase emphasizing the literary value of such tales, recording their vast strangeness and magnificent beauty.
  • 能群飞衔土成丘坟 - Description of the bird's ability to fly in groups and carry soil to form mounds and tombs.
  • 吐五色之气，氤氲如云 - Description of the bird's magical appearance, emitting a five-colored mist, swirling like clouds.
  • 此鸟能反形变色，集于峻林之上 - Description of the bird's magical transformations, changing its shape and color, gathering on high trees.
  • 在木则为禽，行地则为兽，变化无常 - Description of the bird's versatility, on trees it is a bird; on the ground, it is an animal; its transformations are unpredictable.
  • 常游丹海之际，时来苍梧之野 - Description of the bird's habitat and movements, often roams the shores of Dan Sea and sometimes visits the wilds of Cangwu.
  • 衔青砂珠，积成垄阜 - Description of the bird's activity of creating structures, carrying blue sand pearls and accumulating them into hills and mounds.
  • 其珠轻细，风吹如尘起 - Description of the characteristics of the pearls, light and fine, blown by the wind like dust.
  • 今苍梧之外，山人采药，时有得青石，圆洁如珠，服之不死，带者身轻 - Description of the magical properties of the stones, found by mountain dwellers, round and clean like pearls, granting immortality if consumed, and those who wear them become lighter.
  • 故仙人方回《游南岳七言赞》曰 - Quotation from Fang Hui's poem, emphasizing the magical properties of the pearl dust, 'The pearl dust is round, clean, and light, and those who consume it gain eternal life.'

  • 丹海 - Refers to a mythical sea, possibly a reference to a body of water associated with the region.
  • 仙人方回 - Refers to Fang Hui, an immortal figure in Chinese mythology, known for his poetic works.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 錄曰 - A formal heading indicating that what follows is a record or note.
  • 按《春秋傳》云 - Means 'According to the *Spring and Autumn Annals* commentary,' introducing a citation from an authoritative source.
  • 星陨如雨，而夜犹明 - A vivid metaphor describing a celestial event where stars fall like rain, yet the night remains bright.
  • 麒麟斗而日月蚀，鲸鱼死而彗星见 - A poetic and symbolic description of natural phenomena linked to mythical creatures and celestial bodies.
  • 夫盈虚薄蚀，未详变于圣典 - A statement about the unknown changes in celestial phenomena as recorded in sacred texts.
  • 孛彗妖沴，着灾异于图册 - Refers to comets and other celestial anomalies being recorded as signs of calamity in historical records.
  • 麒麟斗，鲸鱼死，靡闻于前经 - Indicates that the events of the mythical creatures fighting and the whale dying are not mentioned in previous scriptures.
  • 求诸正诰，殆将昧焉 - Means 'Seeking in the true edicts, it seems obscure,' suggesting that these events are not clearly documented in authoritative sources.
  • 自稽考群籍 - Means 'After examining various books,' indicating a scholarly approach.
  • 伏羲至于轩辕、少昊、高辛、唐、虞之际 - Refers to the lineage of ancient Chinese rulers from Fuxi to Yao and Shun.
  • 禅业相袭，符表名类，未若尧之盛也 - Describes the succession of rulers and their symbols, noting that none were as great as Yao.
  • 按《易纬》云 - Means 'According to the *Yi Jing* commentary,' introducing another authoritative source.
  • 尧为阳精，叶德干道 - Describes Yao as the embodiment of yang energy and virtue, aligning with the cosmic order.
  • 粤若稽古，是谓上圣 - Means 'Examining ancient times, this is called the supreme sage,' emphasizing Yao's status.
  • 惟天为大，惟尧则之 - A statement about the greatness of heaven and Yao's role in emulating it.
  • 禅业有虞，所谓契叶符同 - Refers to the peaceful transfer of power from Yao to Shun, indicating a harmonious succession.
  • 明象日月 - Means 'Clearly representing the sun and moon,' suggesting a clear and stable governance.
  • 盖其载籍遐旷，算纪绵远 - Describes the vast and long history of these rulers.
  • 德业异纪，神迹各殊 - Indicates that the virtues and miracles of each ruler were distinct.
  • 考传闻于前古，求佥言于中世 - Means 'Examining ancient legends and seeking consensus in the middle ages,' indicating a comprehensive approach to historical research.
  • 而教道参差，祥德递起 - Describes the varying teachings and virtues that emerged over time.
  • 指明群说，能无彷佛 - Means 'Pointing out various explanations, it seems vague,' suggesting that the accounts are not entirely clear.
  • 精灵冥昧，至圣之所不语 - Refers to the mysterious and elusive nature of spiritual phenomena, even beyond the understanding of the wisest.
  • 安以浅末，贬其有无者哉 - Means 'How can we lightly dismiss their existence or non-existence?' questioning the validity of dismissing such phenomena.
  • 刘子政曰 - Means 'Liu Zizheng said,' introducing a quote from a historical figure.
  • 凡传闻不如亲闻，亲闻不如亲见 - A statement about the reliability of information, emphasizing that personal experience is more reliable than hearsay.

  • 淮南子 - A philosophical text attributed to Liu An, the Prince of Huainan.
  • 禅业 - The practice of abdicating the throne to a worthy successor.
  • 易纬 - The commentary on the *Yi Jing* (Book of Changes).
  • 刘子政 - Liu Zizheng, a historian and scholar from the Han dynasty.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 何則 - Rhetorical question meaning 'why is this so?' or 'what then?' used to introduce an explanation.
  • 神化欻忽 - Refers to divine transformations that are sudden and unpredictable.
  • 出隐难常 - Means 'appear and disappear without regularity,' describing the elusive nature of these phenomena.
  • 非肤受之所考算 - Means 'beyond the scope of superficial understanding or calculation,' emphasizing the depth and complexity of these events.
  • 恒情之所思测 - Means 'beyond the reach of ordinary human thought and measurement,' further emphasizing the extraordinary nature of these phenomena.
  • 龙火鸟水之异 - Refers to unusual occurrences involving dragons, fire, birds, and water.
  • 云凤麟虫之属 - Refers to various mythical creatures such as phoenixes, qilins, and insects.
  • 魍魉百怪之形 - Refers to a variety of ghostly and monstrous forms.
  • 欻忽之像 - Means 'sudden and fleeting appearances.'
  • 凭风云而自生 - Means 'arising spontaneously from wind and clouds,' describing the spontaneous nature of these phenomena.
  • 因金玉而相化 - Means 'transforming through gold and jade,' suggesting a transformation influenced by precious materials.
  • 未详备于夏鼎 - Means 'not fully recorded in the bronze vessels of the Xia dynasty,' indicating that these phenomena were not well-documented in ancient records.
  • 信莫记于山经 - Means 'not reliably recorded in the *Shanhaijing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas),' further emphasizing the lack of reliable documentation.
  • 贯月槎之诞 - Refers to the legend of the celestial boat that connects the heavens and earth.
  • 重明桂实之说 - Refers to the story of the double-brightened moon and the fruit of the osmanthus tree.
  • 阳燎出于冰木 - Means 'flames emerging from ice and wood,' describing a paradoxical phenomenon.
  • 阴虫生于炎山 - Means 'insects born in a fiery mountain,' another paradoxical phenomenon.
  • 易肠倒舌之民 - Refers to people with unusual physical characteristics, such as reversed intestines and tongues.
  • 蜕骨龙肉之景 - Refers to scenes of dragons shedding their bones and transforming into flesh.
  • 凭风云而托生 - Means 'arising and depending on wind and clouds,' describing the spontaneous and ethereal nature of these phenomena.
  • 含雨露而蠢育 - Means 'nurtured by rain and dew,' emphasizing the natural and elemental forces involved.
  • 已表怪于众图 - Means 'already depicted in many images,' indicating that these phenomena have been recorded in various forms.
  • 方见伟于群记 - Means 'now seen as remarkable in many records,' suggesting that these phenomena are gaining recognition.
  • 茫茫遐迩 - Means 'vast and far-reaching,' describing the widespread nature of these phenomena.
  • 眇眇流文 - Means 'dim and scattered writings,' referring to the sparse and often obscure records of these phenomena.
  • 百家迂阔 - Refers to the various schools of thought that are often abstract and impractical.
  • 各尚斯异 - Means 'each holds these differences in high regard,' indicating the diversity of opinions and beliefs.
  • 非守文于一说者矣 - Means 'not confined to a single interpretation,' emphasizing the multiplicity of perspectives.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 神化 - Refers to divine transformations, a common theme in Chinese mythology and religious texts.
  • 龙火鸟水之异 - These are specific examples of supernatural phenomena that are often found in Chinese folklore and legends.
  • 云凤麟虫之属 - These are mythical creatures that are often associated with auspicious signs and omens in Chinese culture.
  • 魍魉百怪之形 - These are ghostly and monstrous forms that are often found in Chinese ghost stories and legends.
  • 贯月槎之诞 - This refers to the legend of the celestial boat, which is a significant myth in Chinese astronomy and cosmology.
  • 重明桂实之说 - This refers to the story of the double-brightened moon and the fruit of the osmanthus tree, which is a popular legend in Chinese literature.
  • 阳燎出于冰木 - This describes a paradoxical phenomenon that is often found in Chinese alchemical and philosophical texts.
  • 阴虫生于炎山 - This describes another paradoxical phenomenon that is often found in Chinese alchemical and philosophical texts.
  • 易肠倒舌之民 - This refers to people with unusual physical characteristics, which are often found in Chinese medical and alchemical texts.
  • 蜕骨龙肉之景 - This refers to scenes of dragons shedding their bones and transforming into flesh, which is a common theme in Chinese mythology.
  • 夏鼎 - Refers to the bronze vessels of the Xia dynasty, which are important artifacts in Chinese archaeology and history.
  • 山经 - Refers to the *Shanhaijing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas), an ancient Chinese text that describes various geographical and mythological features.
  • 百家 - Refers to the various schools of thought during the Warring States period, including Confucianism, Daoism, and Legalism.
  • 斯异 - Refers to the diverse and often conflicting interpretations of these phenomena.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 拾遺記 - A historical text that records unusual or forgotten events and figures.
  • 夏禹 - The legendary founder of the Xia Dynasty and a key figure in Chinese mythology.
  • 堯命夏鯀治水 - A reference to the story of Emperor Yao appointing Yu's father, Gun, to control the floods.
  • 九載無績 - Nine years without success, indicating that Gun failed to achieve his mission.
  • 鯀自沉於羽淵 - Gun drowned himself in the Yu marsh, a dramatic event in the story.
  • 化為玄魚 - Transformed into a black fish, a supernatural transformation.
  • 時揚須振鱗 - Sometimes raising its whiskers and shaking its scales, a vivid description of the fish's movements.
  • 橫修波之上 - Across the long waves, describing the fish's position in the water.
  • 見者謂為“河精” - Those who saw it called it the 'river spirit,' a supernatural interpretation.
  • 羽淵與河海通源也 - The Yu marsh is connected to the rivers and seas, explaining the fish's connection to larger water systems.
  • 海民於羽山之中，修立鯀廟 - The people by the sea built a temple to Gun in the Yu mountains, a religious response to the event.
  • 四時以致祭祀 - Offering sacrifices throughout the four seasons, indicating ongoing veneration.
  • 觀者驚而畏矣 - Spectators were amazed and fearful, a reaction to the supernatural events.
  • 至舜命禹疏川奠岳 - When Emperor Shun appointed Yu to clear the rivers and stabilize the mountains, a transition to Yu's role.
  • 行遍日月之墟 - Traveling across the realms of the sun and moon, a grand description of Yu's journeys.
  • 惟不踐羽山之地 - Except for not treading on the land of Yu, a symbolic act of respect.
  • 皆聖德之感也 - All due to the influence of Yu's virtue, explaining the supernatural phenomena.
  • 鯀之靈化，其事互說 - The transformations of Gun are described differently in various accounts, acknowledging the variability in the stories.
  • 神變猶一，而色狀不同 - The transformations are similar, but the appearance differs, a nuanced description of the variations.
  • 玄魚黃能 - Black fish and yellow fish, a reference to the different forms of the transformed Gun.
  • 四音相亂 - The four sounds are confused, a play on the similarity in pronunciation of '玄' (xuan) and '鲧' (gun).
  • 傳寫流文 - In written transmission, the text has flowed, acknowledging the changes in the text over time.
  • “鯀”字或“魚”邊“玄”也 - The character for 'Gun' sometimes has a 'fish' radical and a 'xuan' radical, a note on the character's construction.
  • 群疑眾說 - Various doubts and interpretations, a summary of the different views on the story.
  • 並略記焉 - All are briefly recorded, indicating the comprehensive nature of the account.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 堯 - Emperor Yao, a legendary ruler of ancient China known for his virtue and governance.

  • 禹 - Yu, the legendary founder of the Xia Dynasty and a hero in Chinese mythology.
  • 羽淵 - The Yu marsh, a significant location in the story.
  • 蛟龍 - Dragons, often associated with water and supernatural power in Chinese mythology.
  • 舜 - Emperor Shun, another legendary ruler who succeeded Yao and appointed Yu to control the floods.
  • 翠岑 - Green mountains, symbolizing natural beauty and challenges.
  • 日月之墟 - The realms of the sun and moon, representing the vastness of the world.
  • 羽山 - The Yu mountains, a place of significance in the story.
  • 聖德 - Virtue, a central concept in Chinese moral and political thought.
  • 靈化 - Transformations, often associated with supernatural beings in Chinese mythology.
  • 四音相亂 - A reference to the phonetic similarities between characters, highlighting the complexity of the Chinese writing system.
  • 傳寫流文 - The process of written transmission, which can lead to variations in the text.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 肇迹轩史 - Means 'originating in the records of the Yellow Emperor,' indicating the earliest known records in Chinese history.
  • 道朴风淳 - Describes a time when the Dao (way) was simple and the customs were pure.
  • 文用尚质 - Indicates that writing was valued for its substance and simplicity.
  • 列圣通儒 - Refers to successive wise rulers and scholars.
  • 忧乎道缺 - Expresses concern over the decline of the Dao (way).
  • 玉牒金绳之书 - Refers to precious and sacred texts, often inscribed on jade tablets or gold ropes.
  • 虫章鸟篆之记 - Describes ancient scripts resembling insects and birds, symbolizing early forms of writing.
  • 秘诸岩薮 - Means 'hidden in remote mountains and forests.'

  • 逢丧乱 - Refers to encountering times of chaos and disorder.
  • 经籍事寝 - Means 'the affairs of books and learning were put aside.'
  • 前史旧章 - Refers to the records and traditions of the past.
  • 流散异域 - Means 'scattered to foreign lands.'
  • 字体与俗讹移 - Describes how the forms of characters changed with the times.

  • 历商、周之世 - Refers to the periods of the Shang and Zhou dynasties.

  • 简帛焚裂 - Means 'bamboo slips and silk books were burned and torn.'

  • 详其朽蠹之余 - Means 'examining the remains of decayed texts.'
  • 采捃传闻之说 - Refers to gathering hearsay and oral traditions.
  • 子年所述 - Refers to the account given in the *Zi Nian* (年表) or chronicles.
  • 与圣叶同 - Means 'in agreement with the sages.'
  • 擿文求理 - Refers to analyzing texts to understand their underlying principles.

  • 尧殛鲧于羽山 - Refers to the story of Emperor Yao exiling Gun to Yu Mountain.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 轩史 - Refers to the records of the Yellow Emperor, one of the legendary founders of Chinese civilization.
  • 尧殛鲧于羽山 - A historical event from the *Shangshu* (Book of Documents) where Emperor Yao exiles his son Gun for failing to control floods.
  • 黄能 - A mythical creature, often interpreted as a yellow dragon.

Proper Nouns

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 是在山變為能，入水化為魚也 - Classical structure of transformation, where '能' (neng) can refer to a mythical creature or a general ability, and '化為魚' (hua wei yu) means 'transform into a fish.'
  • 獸之依山，魚之附水 - Classical structure of metaphor, where '依山' (yi shan) means 'rely on the mountain' and '附水' (fu shui) means 'attach to the water,' symbolizing the natural habitat of animals and fish.
  • 各因其性而變化焉 - Classical structure of causality, meaning 'each changes according to its nature.'
  • 詳之正典，爰訪雜說 - Classical structure of sequential actions, meaning 'examine the canonical texts, and then consult various sayings.'
  • 若真若似，並略錄焉 - Classical structure of inclusiveness, meaning 'whether true or similar, all are briefly recorded.'
  • 禹铸九鼎，五者以應陽法，四者以象阴数 - Describes the casting of the Nine Tripods, where '應陽法' (ying yang fa) means 'correspond to the yang principle' and '象阴数' (xiang yin shu) means 'represent the yin number.'
  • 使工师以雌金为阴鼎，以雄金为阳鼎 - Classical structure of assignment, meaning 'let the master craftsman use female metal for the yin tripod and male metal for the yang tripod.'
  • 鼎中常满，以占气象之休否 - Classical structure of function, meaning 'the tripod always remains full, used to predict the auspiciousness or inauspiciousness of the weather.'
  • 当夏桀之世，鼎水忽沸 - Classical structure of historical context, meaning 'during the reign of King Jie of Xia, the water in the tripod suddenly boiled.'
  • 及周将末，九鼎咸震 - Classical structure of historical context, meaning 'by the end of the Zhou dynasty, all nine tripods trembled.'
  • 皆应灭亡之兆 - Classical structure of consequence, meaning 'all were signs of impending doom.'
  • 后世圣人，因禹之迹，代代铸鼎焉 - Classical structure of continuation, meaning 'later sages, following Yu's example, cast tripods generation after generation.'
  • 禹尽力沟洫，导川夷岳 - Classical structure of action, meaning 'Yu made great efforts to dig canals and divert rivers, leveling mountains.'
  • 黄龙曳尾于前，玄龟负青泥于后 - Classical structure of mythological imagery, meaning 'a yellow dragon trailed its tail in front, and a black turtle carried green mud behind.'
  • 玄龟，河精之使者也 - Classical structure of attribution, meaning 'the black turtle is the messenger of the river spirit.'
  • 龟颔下有印，文皆古篆，字作九州岛山川之字 - Classical structure of description, meaning 'the turtle's neck has a seal, the characters are all ancient seals, and the words are the names of the nine provinces and mountains and rivers.'
  • 禹所穿凿之处，皆以青泥封记其所，使玄龟印其上 - Classical structure of action, meaning 'wherever Yu excavated, he sealed it with green mud, and the black turtle stamped it.'
  • 今人聚土为界，此之遗像也 - Classical structure of legacy, meaning 'today, people gather earth to mark boundaries, which is a remnant of this practice.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 禹 - Refers to Yu the Great, a legendary figure in Chinese history known for controlling floods.
  • 九鼎 - Refers to the Nine Tripods, symbolic artifacts of power and authority in ancient China.
  • 夏桀 - Refers to King Jie of Xia, the last ruler of the Xia dynasty, known for his tyranny.
  • 周 - Refers to the Zhou dynasty, one of the most significant dynasties in Chinese history.
  • 黄龙 - Refers to a yellow dragon, a mythical creature often associated with the emperor and imperial power.
  • 玄龟 - Refers to a black turtle, a symbol of longevity and wisdom in Chinese culture.
  • 河精 - Refers to the river spirit, a deity associated with water and fertility.
  • 九州岛 - Refers to the nine provinces of ancient China, a concept used to describe the entire country.

Proper Nouns

  • 九鼎 - Nine Tripods
  • 夏桀 - King Jie of Xia
  • 周 - Zhou dynasty
  • 黄龙 - Yellow Dragon
  • 玄龟 - Black Turtle
  • 河精 - River Spirit

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 禹凿龙关之山，亦谓之龙门 - Historical reference to Yu carving the Dragon Gate mountain, also known as Longmen.
  • 至一空岩，深数十里，幽暗不可复行 - Describes a vast cave, deep for dozens of miles, dark and impossible to continue, emphasizing the difficulty of the journey.
  • 禹乃负火而进 - Shows Yu's determination and resourcefulness in carrying fire and proceeding.
  • 有兽状如豕，衔夜明之珠，其光如烛 - Mythological creature, symbolizing guidance and enlightenment, with a luminous pearl like a candle.
  • 又有青犬，行吠于前 - Suggests a protective or guiding presence with a blue dog barking ahead.
  • 禹计可十里，迷于昼夜 - Indicates the disorienting nature of the journey, with Yu calculating it was about ten miles but being confused by day and night.
  • 既觉渐明，见向来豕犬变为人形，皆着玄衣 - Transformation suggests a divine or supernatural encounter, with the pig and dog transforming into human form, all wearing black clothes.
  • 又见一神，蛇身人面 - Description of a deity, often associated with wisdom and transformation, with a snake body and a human face.
  • 禹因与语，神即示禹八卦之图，列于金版之上 - Interaction with the deity and the presentation of the *I Ching* diagram, showing the diagram of the Eight Trigrams arranged on a golden tablet.
  • 又有八神侍侧 - Signifies a grand and divine presence with eight gods attending.
  • 禹曰：“华胥生圣子，是汝耶？” - Reference to the mythological figure Huaxu, asking if the Holy Son born of Huaxu is the speaker.
  • 答曰：“华胥是九河神女，以生余也。” - Confirms the divine origin of the speaker, stating that Huaxu is the goddess of the Nine Rivers who gave birth to the speaker.
  • 乃探玉简授禹，长一尺二寸，以合十二时之数，使量度天地 - Symbolic gesture, with the jade tablet representing authority and knowledge, one foot two inches long to correspond to the twelve hours, to measure the heavens and earth.
  • 禹即执持此简，以平定水土 - Practical application of the divine gift, with Yu holding the tablet and using it to level the waters and earth.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 禹 - Legendary figure Yu, known for his efforts to control the floods and establish the Xia Dynasty.
  • 华胥 - Mythological figure Huaxu, often associated with the origin of Chinese civilization and the birth of Fuxi.
  • 八卦之图 - Diagram of the Eight Trigrams, a fundamental symbol in the *I Ching* (Book of Changes), representing the principles of the universe.
  • 玉简 - Jade tablet, often used in ancient Chinese culture as a symbol of authority and knowledge.

Proper Nouns

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 傅說赁为赭衣者，舂于深岩以自给 - Describes Fu Yue working as a laborer, grinding grain in a deep quarry to support himself.
  • 梦乘云绕日而行 - A dream where one rides on clouds circling the sun, symbolizing a vision of greatness or divine favor.
  • 筮得“利建侯”之卦 - Refers to divination yielding a favorable hexagram for establishing a feudal lord.
  • 岁余，汤以玉帛聘为阿衡也 - After a year, Tang of Shang invited Fu Yue with gifts of jade and silk to serve as his prime minister.
  • 纣之昏乱 - Describes King Zhou's moral corruption and misrule.
  • 欲讨诸侯 - Intends to attack or subdue the feudal lords.
  • 使飞廉、恶来诛戮贤良 - Orders Feilian and Erya to kill virtuous officials.
  • 取其宝器，埋于琼台之下 - Seizes their treasures and buries them under the Qiongtai Terrace.
  • 使飞廉等惑所近之国，侯服之内，使烽燧相续 - Orders Feilian to deceive nearby states within the feudal system, causing continuous alarms.
  • 纣登台以望火之所在，乃兴师往伐其国 - King Zhou ascends the terrace to see where the alarms are coming from and then sends troops to attack the country.
  • 杀其君，囚其民，收其女乐，肆其淫虐 - Kills the ruler, imprisons the people, takes their women as concubines, and indulges in excessive cruelty.
  • 神人愤怨 - Gods and people are filled with anger.
  • 时时有朱鸟衔火，如星之照耀，以乱烽燧之光 - Red birds carrying fire appear frequently, like stars illuminating the sky, confusing the alarm signals.
  • 纣乃回惑，使诸国灭其烽燧 - King Zhou becomes confused and orders the states to extinguish their alarms.
  • 于是亿兆夷民乃欢，万国已静 - The common people are relieved, and all the states become peaceful.
  • 及武伐纣，樵夫牧竖探高鸟之巢，得玉玺，文曰：“水德将灭，木祚方盛” - When Wu attacked King Zhou, a woodsman and a shepherd found a jade seal in a high bird's nest, inscribed with the text: 'The water element is about to decline, while the wood element is about to flourish.'
  • 文皆大篆，纪殷之世历已尽，而姬之圣德方隆 - The text is written in large seal script, indicating that the Shang dynasty's reign has ended, and the virtue of the Zhou dynasty is rising.
  • 是以三分天下而其二归周 - Therefore, two-thirds of the world's territories belong to the Zhou.
  • 故蚩蚩之类，嗟殷亡之晚，望周来之迟也 - Thus, the common people lament the late fall of the Shang and wish for the Zhou to come sooner.

  • 飛廉、惡來 - Advisors to King Zhou, known for their cruelty and treachery.
  • 瓊台 - A legendary palace or terrace in ancient China, often associated with luxury and decadence.
  • 烽燧 - Beacon fires used in ancient China to signal military alerts.
  • 武 - Refers to King Wu of Zhou, who overthrew the Shang dynasty.

  • 王东巡大骑之谷 - The king toured the eastern part of the Great Riding Valley. '東巡' means 'tour eastward,' and '大騎之谷' refers to a specific valley, possibly named for its association with equestrian activities.
  • 集诸方士仙术之要 - Gathered the essential techniques of various alchemists and immortals. '方士' refers to alchemists or practitioners of Taoist arts, and '仙術' means 'immortal techniques.'
  • 螭、鹄、龙、蛇之类，奇种凭空而出 - Chimerae, cranes, dragons, snakes, and other strange creatures emerged from nowhere. '奇种' means 'strange species' or 'unusual kinds.'
  • 王设长生之灯以自照，一名恒辉 - The king set up a lamp for eternal life to illuminate himself, also known as Everlasting Radiance. '长生之灯' refers to a lamp symbolizing immortality, and '恒辉' is its alternative name.
  • 璠膏之烛，遍于宫内 - Jade fat candles were lit throughout the palace. '璠膏' refers to a type of candle made from jade-like material.
  • 凤脑之灯 - Phoenix brain lamps. '凤脑' likely refers to a type of oil or wick that produces a flame resembling a phoenix.
  • 冰荷者，出冰壑之中 - Ice lotus flowers, which come from the ice valleys. '冰壑' refers to icy ravines or gorges.
  • 取此花以覆炊七八尺 - These flowers were used to cover the cooking area, about seven or eight feet high. '覆炊' means 'to cover the cooking area.'
  • 不欲使光明远也 - To prevent the light from spreading too far. '不欲' means 'do not want' or 'avoid.'
  • 西王母乘翠凤之辇而来 - The Queen Mother of the West arrived riding a green phoenix carriage. '翠凤之辇' refers to a carriage drawn by green phoenixes.
  • 前导以文虎、文豹，后列雕麟、紫麇 - She was preceded by tigers and leopards with patterns, and followed by carved unicorns and purple deer. '文虎' and '文豹' refer to patterned tigers and leopards, while '雕麟' and '紫麇' refer to carved unicorns and purple deer.
  • 曳丹玉之履，敷碧蒲之席，黄莞之荐 - She wore red jade shoes, sat on a green rush mat, and had a yellow rush cushion. '丹玉之履' refers to red jade shoes, '碧蒲之席' to a green rush mat, and '黄莞之荐' to a yellow rush cushion.
  • 荐清澄琬琰之膏以为酒 - She offered clear and pure jade-like oil as wine. '琬琰之膏' refers to a type of oil that is clear and pure, resembling jade.
  • 又进洞渊红花，嵚州甜雪，昆流素莲，阴岐黑枣，万岁冰桃，千常碧藕，青花白橘 - She also offered deep-sea red flowers, sweet snow from Qinqiu, white lotuses from Kunliu, black jujubes from Yinji, thousand-year-old ice peaches, ten-thousand-year-old green lotus roots, and white oranges with blue flowers. Each of these items is a rare or mythical food item.
  • 素莲者，一房百子，凌冬而茂 - White lotuses, which have a hundred seeds in one pod and thrive even in winter. '一房百子' means 'a hundred seeds in one pod,' and '凌冬而茂' means 'thrive in winter.'
  • 黑枣者，其树百寻，实长二尺，核细而柔，百年一熟 - Black jujubes, whose trees are a hundred fathoms tall, with fruits two feet long, small and soft seeds, and mature once every hundred years. '百寻' means 'a hundred fathoms,' and '百年一熟' means 'mature once every hundred years.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 周灵王立二十一年 - In the twenty-first year of King Ling of Zhou's reign; a standard way of dating events in Chinese history.
  • 孔子生于鲁襄公之世 - Confucius was born during the reign of Duke Xiang of Lu; a biographical detail used to situate Confucius in time.
  • 夜有二苍龙自天而下，来附征在之房 - At night, two blue dragons descended from the sky and came to the room of Zhengzai; a mythological description of an extraordinary event.
  • 因梦而生夫子 - And thus Confucius was born through a dream; suggests a supernatural origin for Confucius.
  • 有二神女，擎香露于空中而来，以沐浴征在 - Two divine maidens carried fragrant dew from the sky to bathe Zhengzai; another supernatural element.
  • 天帝下奏钧天之乐，列以颜氏之房 - The Heavenly Emperor played the music of the Central Heaven, arranged in the room of the Yan family; describes a celestial performance.
  • 空中有声，言天感生圣子，故降以和乐笙镛之音，异于俗世也 - There was a voice in the air saying that the heavens had given birth to a sage child, so they sent down the harmonious sounds of bells and drums, which are different from the music of the common world; explains the significance of the celestial music.
  • 又有五老列于征在之庭，则五星之精也 - There were also five old men standing in Zhengzai's courtyard, who were the spirits of the five planets; a reference to the Five Planets.
  • 夫子未生时，有麟吐玉书于阙里人家 - Before Confucius was born, a qilin (a mythical creature) spat out a jade book in a house in Qufu; a mythological event.
  • 文云：“水精之子，系衰周而素王。” - The text read: 'The son of the water spirit, bound to the declining Zhou and the simple king.'; the message on the jade book.
  • 至敬王之末，鲁定公二十四年，鲁人锄商田于大泽，得麟，以示夫子 - By the end of King Jing of Zhou's reign, in the twenty-fourth year of Duke Ding of Lu, a man from Lu named Chu found a qilin in a large marsh and showed it to Confucius; a historical event.
  • 系角之绂，尚犹在焉 - The silk ribbon tied to the qilin's horn was still there; a detail about the qilin.
  • 夫子知命之将终，乃抱麟解绂，涕泗滂沱 - Confucius knew that his life was coming to an end, so he embraced the qilin, untied the ribbon, and wept profusely; describes Confucius' emotional reaction.
  • 且麟出之时，及解绂之岁，垂百年矣 - Moreover, the time when the qilin appeared and the year when the ribbon was untied were nearly a hundred years apart; provides a temporal context.

  • 升之以望云色 - Literally 'to ascend it to look at the color of the clouds,' describing the purpose of the terrace.
