  • 王乃登台，望云气蓊郁 - Literally 'the king then ascended the terrace and looked at the dense cloud formation,' describing the king's action.
  • 忽见二人乘云而至，须发皆黄，非谣俗之类也 - Literally 'suddenly saw two people riding on the clouds, with yellow beards and hair, not of the common folk,' describing the appearance of the two figures.
  • 乘游龙飞凤之辇，驾以青螭 - Literally 'riding on a carriage shaped like a flying dragon and phoenix, driven by a green *chi* (a mythical beast),' describing the vehicle.
  • 其衣皆缝缉毛羽也 - Literally 'their clothes were all sewn from feathers,' describing the attire of the two figures.
  • 王即迎之上席 - Literally 'the king immediately welcomed them to the upper seat,' indicating the king's hospitality.
  • 时天下大旱，地裂木燃 - Literally 'at that time the whole country was suffering from a great drought, with the ground cracking and trees burning,' describing the natural disaster.
  • 一人先唱：“能为雪霜。” - Literally 'one of them first sang: 'Can you make snow and frost?'' indicating a request for supernatural intervention.

This phrase contrasts the loss of virtue due to desire with the change of will due to confusion.

  • 如手足卫头目，舟楫济巨川 - 如手足卫头目 means like hands and feet protecting the head and eyes. 舟楫济巨川 means like a boat crossing a great river. These are metaphors for the importance of loyalty.
  • 行求媚以取容 - 行求媚 means to act in a way to please. 以取容 means to gain favor.
  • 卒见于夷戮 - 卒见于 means finally faced. 夷戮 means execution by beheading.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 乃设麟文之席 - Indicates a sequence of events; '设' means 'to set up' or 'to arrange'; '麟文之席' refers to a luxurious or ornate seating arrangement decorated with patterns of qilin (a mythical creature).
  • 散荃芜之香 - '散' means 'to disperse' or 'to spread'; '荃芜之香' refers to a fragrant substance, possibly a perfume or incense.
  • 香出波弋国 - '波弋国' is likely a fictional or mythical place name, possibly referring to a distant land known for its unique perfumes.
  • 浸地则土石皆香 - '浸地' means 'to soak into the ground'; '土石皆香' means 'the soil and stones all become fragrant.'
  • 着朽木腐草，莫不郁茂 - '着' means 'to stick to' or 'to adhere to'; '朽木腐草' refers to decaying wood and grass; '莫不郁茂' means 'nothing fails to flourish.'
  • 以熏枯骨，则肌肉皆生 - '熏' means 'to fumigate' or 'to smoke'; '枯骨' means 'dry bones'; '肌肉皆生' means 'the flesh and muscles all grow back.'
  • 以屑喷地，厚四五寸 - '屑' means 'powder' or 'crushed material'; '喷地' means 'to sprinkle on the ground'; '厚四五寸' means 'about four to five inches thick.'
  • 使二女舞其上，弥日无迹，体轻故也 - '使' means 'to make' or 'to cause'; '二女' means 'two women'; '舞其上' means 'dance on it'; '弥日无迹' means 'for a whole day without leaving any trace'; '体轻故也' means 'because their bodies are light.'
  • 时有白鸾孤翔，衔千茎穟 - '时有' means 'sometimes there is'; '白鸾' means 'white phoenix'; '孤翔' means 'flying alone'; '衔千茎穟' means 'carrying thousands of ears of grain.'
  • 穟于空中自生，花实落地，则生根叶 - '穟' means 'ear of grain'; '自生' means 'to grow by itself'; '花实落地' means 'the flowers and fruits fall to the ground'; '生根叶' means 'sprout roots and leaves.'
  • 一岁百获，一茎满车，故曰“盈车嘉穟” - '一岁百获' means 'a hundred harvests in one year'; '一茎满车' means 'one stalk fills a cart'; '故曰”盈车嘉穟”' means 'therefore it is called 'abundant grains filling the cart.'
  • 麟文者，错杂宝以饰席也，皆为云霞麟凤之状 - '麟文者' means 'the qilin pattern'; '错杂宝' means 'a mixture of precious materials'; '饰席' means 'to decorate the seating'; '云霞麟凤之状' means 'in the shapes of clouds, rainbows, qilin, and phoenixes.'
  • 昭王复以衣袖麾之，舞者皆止 - '复' means 'again'; '以衣袖麾之' means 'with his sleeve he waved'; '舞者皆止' means 'the dancers all stopped.'
  • 昭王知其神异，处于崇霞之台，设枕席以寝宴，遣侍人以卫之 - '知其神异' means 'knew of their supernatural nature'; '处于崇霞之台' means 'resided on the high platform of the towering clouds'; '设枕席以寝宴' means 'arranged pillows and seating for sleeping and feasting'; '遣侍人以卫之' means 'sent attendants to guard them.'
  • 王好神仙之术，玄天之女，托形作此二人 - '好神仙之术' means 'enjoyed the practices of immortals'; '玄天之女' means 'the daughters of the Heavenly Realm'; '托形作此二人' means 'took the forms of these two women.'
  • 昭王之末，莫知所在 - '昭王之末' means 'at the end of King Zhao's reign'; '莫知所在' means 'no one knew where they were.'
  • 或云游于汉江，或伊洛之滨 - '或云' means 'it is said'; '游于汉江' means 'traveling along the Han River'; '伊洛之滨' means 'along the banks of the Yi and Luo rivers.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 寡人志于仙道 - I have set my mind on the Way of Immortality.

  • 可得遂乎？ - Can this be achieved?
  • 血清骨劲，肤实肠轻 - Clear blood, strong bones, solid skin, and light intestines. Physical descriptions of an immortal being, suggesting a purified body.
  • 历蓬、瀛而超碧海 - Traversed the mountains of Peng and Ying, surpassing the Azure Sea.
  • 洗神灭念 - Wash the spirit, extinguish thoughts. A Daoist practice of purifying the mind and eliminating desire.
  • 常游于太极之门 - Frequently roams the Gate of the Supreme Ultimate.
  • 妖容惑目 - Alluring appearances that dazzle the eyes. A metaphor for beauty that distracts from spiritual cultivation.
  • 列女成群 - A crowd of virtuous women. Possibly a euphemism for concubines or courtly entourage.
  • 迷心动虑 - Cloud the mind, disturb the thoughts. A common Daoist warning about the dangers of desire.
  • 操圭爵以量沧海 - To use a small jade cup to measure the sea. A metaphor for an impossible task, like trying to achieve immortality while indulging in earthly pleasures.
  • 执毫厘而回日月 - To grasp a hair and turn the sun and moon. Another metaphor for an impossible or futile endeavor.
  • 其可得乎！ - Can this be achieved? A rhetorical question emphasizing futility.
  • 彻色减味 - Abandon beauty and reduce indulgence in taste. A literal translation, but implies renouncing sensory pleasures.
  • 赐甘需羽衣一袭 - Bestowed upon Gan Xu a robe of feathers. A symbolic garment associated with immortals.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 昭王 - Likely refers to a historical king, possibly from the Warring States period or an earlier dynasty. The name ‘Zhao Wang’ is common in Chinese history (e.g., King Zhao of Yan, King Zhao of Chu). The text may be fictional or allegorical, but it uses the structure of historical narrative.
  • 甘需 - A minister or advisor to the king. The name is not historically prominent, but may be a fictional or symbolic figure.
  • 蓬、瀛 - Likely mythological or Daoist names for distant or mystical lands, possibly referring to the islands of Peng and Ying, which appear in Daoist texts.
  • 碧海 - A term often used in Daoist and mythological texts to refer to vast, sacred, or otherworldly seas.
  • 太极 - A central concept in Daoism, representing the source of all things.
  • 羽衣 - A garment associated with immortals in Daoist tradition, often worn by xian (immortals) or Daoist sages.
  • 明真理 - A title or designation given to a place, possibly a temple or a spiritual center.

Proper Nouns

  • 甘需 - A minister or advisor. Rendered as ‘Gan Xu’ or ‘Minister Gan Xu.’
  • 蓬、瀛 - Mythical or Daoist lands. Rendered as ‘Peng and Ying’ or ‘Mountains of Peng and Ying.’
  • 碧海 - A Daoist or mythological sea. Rendered as ‘Azure Sea.’
  • 太极 - A Daoist concept. Rendered as ‘Supreme Ultimate’ or ‘the Great Ultimate.’
  • 羽衣 - A Daoist garment. Rendered as ‘feathered robe’ or ‘robe of feathers.’
  • 明真理 - A title or designation. Rendered as ‘The Illumination of Truth’ or ‘The Enlightenment of Truth.’

  • 人皆壽三百歲 - Means 'people all live for three hundred years,' indicating a long lifespan.
  • 結草為衣，是謂卉服 - Means 'binding grass to make clothing, which is called 'grass clothes,' describing a simple and natural way of dressing.

  • 死葬于野外，以香木灵草瘗掩其尸 - Means 'they bury the dead in the wild, using fragrant wood and grass to cover the body,' describing a respectful and natural burial practice.
  • 闾里助送，号泣之音，动于林谷，河源为之流止，春木为之改色 - Describes the emotional impact of the funeral, where the community mourns, and the natural world responds.
  • 居丧水浆不入于口，至死者骨为尘埃，然后乃食 - Means 'during mourning, they do not eat or drink until the bones of the deceased turn to dust, then they resume eating,' describing a strict period of mourning.
  • 昔大禹随山导川，乃旌其地为无老纯孝之国 - Means 'In ancient times, Yu the Great followed the mountains and guided the rivers, and thus he honored this land as the country of the ageless and pure filial piety,' describing a legendary event that honors the country.

  • 受命因生，包乎五德 - Means 'receiving the mandate and thus living, encompassing the five virtues,' referring to the moral and ethical principles.
  • 守淳明以循身，资施以为本 - Means 'maintaining purity and clarity to guide one's actions, and using generosity as the foundation,' describing a virtuous way of life.
  • 义缘天属，生尽爱敬之容 - Means 'righteousness stemming from natural connections, showing love and respect throughout life,' describing the importance of familial and social bonds.
  • 体自心慈，死结追终之慕 - Means 'having a kind heart, even in death, there is a longing for the end,' describing the emotional aspect of death.
  • 盖处物之常情，有识之常道 - Means 'this is the constant nature of things and the common path of those who understand,' describing a universal truth.
  • 是以忠谏一至，则会理以通幽 - Means 'thus, when a loyal advisor speaks once, it brings understanding and illuminates the hidden,' describing the impact of wise counsel.
  • 神义由心，则祇灵为之昭感 - Means 'when righteousness comes from the heart, the spirits are moved and respond,' describing the spiritual aspect of righteousness.
  • 迹显神着，表降群祥 - Means 'the signs are clear and the spirits are evident, bringing down many good omens,' describing the positive outcomes of virtuous actions.
  • 行道不违，远迩旌德 - Means 'following the path without deviation, both near and far, they are praised for their virtue,' describing the widespread recognition of virtue.
  • 美乎异国之人，隔绝王化，阙闻大道 - Means 'how admirable are the people of a foreign land, isolated from the king's influence, lacking knowledge of the great way,' describing the admiration for a distant and isolated society.
  • 语其国法，华戎有殊 - Means 'describing their laws, there is a difference between the Chinese and the barbarians,' highlighting the cultural differences.
  • 观其政教，颇令殊俗 - Means 'observing their political and educational systems, they are quite different from the usual customs,' describing the unique governance and education.
  • 礼在四夷，事存诸诰 - Means 'rites exist among the four barbarian regions, and these matters are recorded in the edicts,' describing the presence of rituals in distant lands.
  • 孝让之风，莫不尚也 - Means 'the spirit of filial piety and yielding is highly valued,' emphasizing the importance of these virtues.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 大禹 - Refers to Yu the Great, a legendary Chinese ruler and founder of the Xia Dynasty, known for his efforts in flood control and the establishment of a centralized government.
  • 五德 - Refers to the five virtues, which are often associated with Confucian ethics: benevolence (仁), righteousness (义), propriety (礼), wisdom (智), and faithfulness (信).
  • 四夷 - Refers to the four barbarian regions, traditionally used to describe the non-Chinese peoples surrounding the Chinese civilization.
  • 王化 - Refers to the king's influence or the cultural and political influence of the central government.
  • 大道 - Refers to the great way or the path of righteousness and virtue.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 昭王思諸神異 - King Zhuang contemplates various divine wonders.
  • 學道之人也 - Refers to someone who studies the Dao (the Way), often translated as a 'Daoist scholar' or 'one who practices Daoism.'
  • 西王母將來游 - The Queen Mother of the West will come to visit.
  • 必語虛無之術 - Will surely speak about techniques or methods of achieving a state of emptiness or nothingness, often associated with Daoist practices.
  • 不逾一年 - Within a year.
  • 與昭王游於燧林之下 - Wanders with King Zhuang in a forest of firewood.
  • 說炎帝鑽火之術 - Discusses the technique of making fire by drilling, attributed to the Flame Emperor.
  • 取綠桂之膏，燃以照夜 - Burns the resin of the green osmanthus to light up the night.
  • 忽有飛蛾 - Suddenly there was a moth.
  • 狀如丹雀 - Appears like a red bird.
  • 來拂於桂膏之上 - Came to touch the osmanthus resin.
  • 此蛾出於員丘之穴 - The moth comes from a cave on Mount Yuanqiu.
  • 穴洞達九天 - The cave penetrates the highest heavens.
  • 中有細珠如流沙 - Inside the cave, there are tiny pearls like flowing sand.
  • 可穿而結 - Can be pierced and knotted.
  • 因用為佩 - Therefore used as an ornament.
  • 此是神蛾之矢也 - These are the arrows of the divine moth.
  • 蛾憑氣飲露 - The moth relies on qi (vital energy) and drinks dew.
  • 飛不集下 - Does not land.
  • 群仙殺此蛾合丹藥 - The group of immortals kills the moth to make elixirs.
  • 西王母與群仙游員丘之上 - The Queen Mother and immortals wander on Mount Yuanqiu.
  • 聚神蛾 - Gather divine moths.
  • 以琼筐盛之 - Place them in a jade basket.
  • 使玉童負筐 - Make a jade boy carry the basket.
  • 以游四极 - Wander to the four corners of the earth.
  • 來降燕庭 - Came down to the court of Yan.
  • 出此蛾以示昭王 - Show this moth to King Zhuang.
  • 王曰：“今乞此蛾以合九转神丹！” - King Zhuang says, 'I request this moth to make the Nine Transformation Elixir!'
  • 王母弗与 - The Queen Mother does not give it to him.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 昭王 - King Zhuang of Zhou, a ruler of the Zhou dynasty.
  • 西王母 - The Queen Mother of the West, a significant figure in Chinese mythology, often associated with longevity and immortality.
  • 炎帝 - The Flame Emperor, a legendary figure in Chinese mythology, known for his contributions to agriculture and fire-making.
  • 員丘 - A mythical mountain, often associated with the abode of immortals.
  • 九天 - The highest heavens, symbolizing the ultimate realm of spiritual attainment.
  • 九转神丹 - A legendary elixir of immortality, often mentioned in Daoist texts.
  • 燕庭 - The court of Yan, a state during the Warring States period.

Proper Nouns

  • 昭王 - King Zhuang of Zhou.
  • 西王母 - The Queen Mother of the West.
  • 炎帝 - The Flame Emperor.
  • 員丘 - Mount Yuanqiu.
  • 九天 - The highest heavens.
  • 九转神丹 - The Nine Transformation Elixir.
  • 燕庭 - The court of Yan.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 昭王坐握日之台参云 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'King Zhao sat on the *Worri Sun* platform, which reached into the clouds.'
  • 上可扪日 - Hyperbolic expression meaning 'one could touch the sun,' indicating the platform's great height.
  • 黑鸟白头 - Literal description of a bird with a black body and white head.
  • 洞光之珠 - Pearl with the property of illuminating darkness, suggesting it is highly valuable and magical.
  • 圆径一尺 - Specific measurement, meaning the pearl has a diameter of one foot.
  • 百神不能隐其精灵 - Superlative phrase meaning 'no gods or spirits could conceal its spirit,' emphasizing the pearl's supernatural power.
  • 阴泉之底 - Refers to the bottom of a dark spring, indicating a mysterious and hidden location.
  • 寒山之北 - Indicates a specific geographical location, the north side of Cold Mountain.
  • 员水之中 - Refers to a circular or round body of water, suggesting a unique and possibly mystical setting.
  • 黑蚌飞翔 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'black abalone flying,' indicating a rare and extraordinary phenomenon.
  • 五岳之上 - Refers to the highest points of the Five Great Mountains, emphasizing the bird's ability to fly to great heights.
  • 黄帝时 - Refers to the time of the legendary Yellow Emperor, a significant figure in Chinese mythology.
  • 雾成子 - Name that could be a historical or mythological figure, possibly a hermit or sage.
  • 高崖之上 - Refers to a high cliff, indicating a dramatic and elevated setting.
  • 悬日月 - Hyperbolic expression meaning 'since the time of the sun and moon,' indicating an extremely long period.
  • 销暑招凉之珠 - Descriptive name for the pearl, meaning 'pearl that dispels heat and attracts coolness.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 昭王 - Refers to King Zhao of Yan, a historical figure known for his efforts to strengthen the state of Yan.
  • 燕昭王 - Another reference to King Zhao of Yan, emphasizing his reign.
  • 瑶漳之水 - Refers to the Jade River, a mythical or significant body of water in the story.
  • 黄帝 - The legendary Yellow Emperor, a central figure in Chinese mythology and the founder of Chinese civilization.
  • 雾成子 - Name that could be a historical or mythological figure, possibly a hermit or sage.
  • 寒山 - A specific mountain, possibly a real or mythical location.
  • 五岳 - Refers to the Five Great Mountains, which are Mount Tai, Mount Hua, Mount Heng (Shanxi), Mount Heng (Hunan), and Mount Song, significant in Chinese culture and religion.

Proper Nouns

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 含丹青以漱地 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to rinse the ground with cinnabar and verdigris,' referring to the magical ability of the artist to create images on the ground.
  • 魑魅及诡怪群物之像 - Refers to images of mythical and monstrous creatures.
  • 刻玉为百兽之形 - Means 'carving jade into the shapes of a hundred beasts,' referring to the artist's skill in carving jade.
  • 毛发宛若真矣 - Means 'the fur is as real as if it were alive,' emphasizing the lifelike quality of the carvings.
  • 皆铭其臆前，记以日月 - Means 'all inscribed on their chests, marked with the date and month,' indicating the artist's method of recording the date of creation.
  • 工人以指画地，长百丈，直如绳墨 - Means 'the artist drew on the ground with his finger, a hundred paces long, straight as a string and plumb line,' emphasizing the artist's skill in drawing.
  • 方寸之内，画以四渎五岳列国之图 - Means 'within a square inch, he drew maps of the four rivers, five mountains, and various states,' indicating the artist's ability to create detailed maps.
  • 又画为龙凤，骞翥若飞 - Means 'he also drew dragons and phoenixes, soaring as if flying,' emphasizing the artist's skill in drawing mythical creatures.
  • 皆不可点睛，或点之，必飞走也 - Means 'none of them could have their eyes painted, or if they were, they would fly away,' indicating the magical nature of the drawings.
  • 始皇嗟曰 - Means 'the First Emperor sighed and said,' indicating his reaction to the artist's work.
  • 刻画之形，何得飞走 - Means 'how can the carved images fly away?' expressing his disbelief.
  • 使以淳漆各点两玉虎一眼眼 - Means 'he ordered them to paint the eyes of two jade tigers with pure lacquer,' indicating his attempt to test the magical nature of the drawings.
  • 旬日则失之，不知所在 - Means 'after ten days, they were lost, and their whereabouts were unknown,' indicating the disappearance of the tigers.
  • 山泽之人云 - Means 'the people of the mountains and marshes said,' indicating the report of common people.
  • 见二白虎，各无一目，相随而行，毛色相似，异于常见者 - Means 'they saw two white tigers, each missing one eye, walking together, with similar fur colors, different from what is commonly seen,' indicating the appearance of the tigers.
  • 至明年，西方献两白虎，各无一目 - Means 'the following year, two white tigers, each missing one eye, were offered from the west,' indicating the arrival of the tigers.
  • 始皇发槛视之，疑是先所失者，乃刺杀之 - Means 'the First Emperor opened the cage to look at them, suspecting they were the ones previously lost, and then had them killed,' indicating his reaction to the tigers.
  • 检其胸前，果是元年所刻玉虎 - Means 'he checked their chests, and indeed they were the jade tigers carved in the first year,' confirming the identity of the tigers.
  • 迄胡亥之灭，宝剑神物，随时散乱也 - Means 'until the death of Hu Hai, the precious swords and divine objects were scattered at the time,' indicating the fate of the objects.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 秦始皇 - The First Emperor of Qin, known for his unification of China and the construction of the Great Wall.
  • 始皇元年 - The first year of the First Emperor's reign, marking the beginning of the Qin dynasty.
  • 骞霄国 - A mythical or distant country, possibly referring to a foreign land.
  • 刻玉善画工名裔 - A skilled artist from the country of Qianxiao, known for his ability to carve jade and draw.
  • 丹青 - Refers to cinnabar and verdigris, traditional pigments used in painting.
  • 魑魅 - Mythical and monstrous creatures, often associated with evil or supernatural beings.
  • 诡怪群物之像 - Images of strange and mythical creatures.
  • 四渎 - The four major rivers of China: the Yellow River, the Yangtze River, the Huai River, and the Ji River.
  • 五岳 - The five sacred mountains of China: Mount Tai, Mount Hua, Mount Heng (in Hunan), Mount Heng (in Shanxi), and Mount Song.
  • 列国 - Various states or regions within China.
  • 龙凤 - Dragons and phoenixes, mythical creatures often associated with power and prosperity.
  • 胡亥 - The second emperor of the Qin dynasty, known for his short and tyrannical reign.
  • 宝剑神物 - Precious swords and divine objects, often associated with power and mysticism.

Proper Nouns

  • 秦始皇 - The First Emperor of Qin.
  • 始皇元年 - The first year of the First Emperor's reign.
  • 骞霄国 - A mythical or distant country.
  • 刻玉善画工名裔 - A skilled artist from the country of Qianxiao.
  • 胡亥 - The second emperor of the Qin dynasty.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 乘螺舟而至 - Metaphorical or mythical expression. '螺舟' (snail-boat) is a poetic or symbolic term for a mysterious or magical vessel. Render as 'a vessel shaped like a snail' or 'a snail-shaped boat' and add a note that it is a mythical or fantastical vessel.
  • 沉行海底，而水不浸入 - Describes a magical or supernatural vessel that can travel underwater without being submerged. Likely a metaphor for an advanced or otherworldly technology or mode of transport.
  • 瞭如亲睹 - Idiom expressing clarity of vision or knowledge, as though one had witnessed it firsthand.
  • 以万岁为一日 - Poetic expression used to describe a vastly different perception of time, common in mythological or fantastical texts.
  • 燃石以继日光 - Describes a mythical or magical substance that emits light, used to replace the sun at night. Likely a form of luminous mineral or firestone.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 始皇 - Refers to Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of China. Should be consistently rendered as 'the First Emperor' or 'Qin Shi Huang' depending on context.
  • 宛渠之民 - 'Wanchu' is a mythical or exotic land mentioned in ancient Chinese texts. It is not a real historical location but a legendary or foreign land.
  • 咸池 - Mythical or poetic name for a location, possibly related to the 'Sea of Salt' or a celestial location. Treat as a proper noun.
  • 炎帝 - One of the legendary 'Three Sovereigns' of ancient China, associated with the invention of fire and agriculture.
  • 轩辕之丘 - 'Xuanyuan's Hill' is a mythical location associated with the Yellow Emperor (Huangdi).
  • 少典 - Legendary figure, father of the Yellow Emperor.
  • 庆都 - Mother of the legendary sage-king Yao, one of the Five Emperors in Chinese tradition.
  • 冀州 - A region in ancient China.
  • 丹雀 - One of the Four Symbols in Chinese cosmology, associated with the south and the element of fire. Its appearance is often a sign of good fortune or the arrival of a sage.

Proper Nouns

  • 螺舟 - Mythical vessel. Alternative name: '沦波舟' ('Wave-Sinking Boat' or 'Submerged-Wave Boat').
  • 首山 - Location where copper was mined.
  • 金火气动 - Mystical or celestial sign.
  • 三鼎 - Three great tripods. Symbolic of imperial power and cosmic order.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 同志好学 - Having the same aspirations and loving learning, indicating a shared educational goal.
  • 迭剪发而鬻之，以相养 - Taking turns cutting their hair and selling it to support each other, a literal but idiomatic expression describing a mutual support system.
  • 或佣力写书 - Sometimes working as hired laborers to write books, indicating a combination of manual labor and scholarly activity.
  • 非圣人之言不读 - They would not read anything unless it was the words of sages, emphasizing their selective approach to reading.
  • 遇见《坟》《典》，行途无所题记，以墨书掌及股里，夜还而写之 - When they encountered the *Fen* and *Dian* (ancient texts), they would inscribe them on their palms and inner thighs with ink, then write them down at night, describing a method of memorization and recording.
  • 析竹为简 - Splitting bamboo to make writing tablets, a literal description of making writing materials.
  • 二人每假食于路，剥树皮编以为书帙 - Whenever they ate on the road, they would peel tree bark and weave it into book covers, describing a practical method of creating book covers.
  • 尝息大树之下，假息而寐 - They once rested under a large tree and slept while resting, indicating a moment of rest.
  • 二子何勤苦也 - Why are you so hardworking and diligent? a rhetorical question expressing admiration.
  • 请其术，教以干世出俗之辩 - They asked him for his skills, and he taught them how to debate and argue in the world, describing the teaching of rhetorical skills.
  • 探胸内，得二卷说书 - They found two scrolls of persuasive writing inside his chest, a metaphorical description of receiving knowledge.
  • 言辅时之事 - They contained advice for the times, indicating the content of the scrolls.
  • 秦王子婴立，凡百日，郎中赵高谋杀之 - Prince Ziying of Qin ascended the throne, but after one hundred days, the eunuch Zhao Gao plotted to kill him, describing a historical event.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 张仪、苏秦 - Historical figures known for their diplomatic and rhetorical skills during the Warring States period.
  • 鬼谷子 - A legendary figure associated with the Warring States period, known for his teachings in strategy and rhetoric.
  • 《坟》《典》 - Ancient texts, possibly referring to the *Shujing* (Book of Documents) and *Shijing* (Book of Poetry).
  • 秦王子婴 - The last ruler of the Qin dynasty, who briefly reigned after the death of Qin Er Shi.
  • 赵高 - A powerful eunuch who played a significant role in the fall of the Qin dynasty.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 子婴寝于望夷之宫 - Ziying slept in the Palace of Wangyi; indicates a state of rest or seclusion.
  • 纳玉舄而乘丹车 - Wearing jade shoes and riding in a red carriage; describes a grand and divine appearance.
  • 云欲见秦王子婴 - Declaring that he wanted to see the prince of Qin; indicates direct speech.
  • 阍者许进焉 - The gatekeeper allowed him to enter; indicates permission to enter.
  • 当有同姓名欲相诛暴 - There will be people with the same name who will kill each other violently; suggests a future conflict.
  • 翌日乃起 - The next day then got up; indicates the passage of time.
  • 疑赵高 - Suspected Zhao Gao; indicates growing distrust.
  • 囚高于咸阳狱 - Imprisoned Zhao Gao in the prison in Xianyang; indicates confinement.
  • 悬于井中 - Hung him in the well; describes the method of execution.
  • 七日不死 - Did not die after seven days; suggests supernatural resilience.
  • 更以镬汤煮 - Then boiled him in a large pot of water; describes another method of execution.
  • 七日不沸 - Did not boil after seven days; again suggests supernatural resilience.
  • 乃戮之 - Then killed him; indicates the final act of execution.
  • 高其神乎 - Is he a god?; expresses doubt about Zhao Gao's supernatural nature.
  • 初囚高之时 - At the beginning of imprisoning Zhao Gao; indicates the initial imprisonment.
  • 见高怀有一青丸 - Seeing Zhao Gao carrying a green pill; describes a mysterious object.
  • 赵高先世受韩终丹法 - Zhao Gao's ancestors received elixir techniques from Han Zhong; explains Zhao Gao's alleged connection to alchemy.
  • 冬月坐于坚冰 - Sitting on frozen ice in winter; describes a harsh winter condition.
  • 夏日卧于炉上 - Lying down on a stove in summer; describes a hot summer condition.
  • 不觉寒热 - Unaware of cold and heat; suggests that Zhao Gao was unaffected by extreme temperatures.

  • 泣送者千家 - Crying and sending off thousands of families; describes the mournful reaction of the people.
  • 或见一青雀从高尸中出 - Seeing a green bird emerging from Zhao Gao's body; describes a supernatural event.
  • 直入云 - Entering directly into the clouds; describes the bird flying into the sky.
  • 九转之验 - Nine-fold transformation; symbolizes the process of achieving immortality.
  • 信于是乎 - Believing in this case; expresses belief in the supernatural events.

  • 即始皇之灵 - Is the spirit of Qin Shi Huang; suggests that the figure in the dream was the spirit of Qin Shi Huang.
  • 所著玉舄 - Wearing jade shoes; describes the divine attire.
  • 则安期先生所遗也 - Is left behind by Master Anqi; suggests that the jade shoes were a gift from a legendary figure.
  • 鬼魅之理 - Principles of ghosts and spirits; refers to the principles governing ghosts and spirits.
  • 万世一时 - Rare and significant event; suggests that the event was rare and significant.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 盖犹褊惑尚多，滞情未尽 - Complex phrase meaning 'still, there are many narrow-minded and confused people, and their emotions are not fully resolved.' '褊惑' (biǎn huò) refers to narrow-mindedness and confusion, while '滞情' (zhì qíng) means emotional stagnation or unresolved feelings.
  • 神通玄化 - Compound term meaning 'supernatural transformations' or 'mystical changes.' '神通' (shén tōng) refers to supernatural abilities, and '玄化' (xuán huà) means profound or mysterious transformation.
  • 说变万端 - Means 'to speak of countless transformations' or 'to describe myriad changes.' '说变' (shuō biàn) means to speak of changes, and '万端' (wàn duān) means countless or myriad.
  • 徐行云垂之俦 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'companions who move slowly like clouds hanging in the sky.' '徐行' (xú xíng) means to move slowly, and '云垂' (yún chuí) means clouds hanging low in the sky.
  • 驾影乘霞之侣 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'companions who ride shadows and clouds.' '驾影' (jià yǐng) means to ride shadows, and '乘霞' (chéng xiá) means to ride clouds.
  • 穷神绝异 - Means 'to exhaust the supernatural and the extraordinary.' '穷神' (qióng shén) means to exhaust the supernatural, and '绝异' (jué yì) means extraordinary or unparalleled.
  • 随方而来 - Means 'to come from all directions' or 'to arrive from various places.' '随方' (suí fāng) means from all directions.
  • 衒绝殊形 - Means 'to display extraordinary and unique forms.' '衒绝' (xuàn jué) means to display extraordinarily, and '殊形' (shū xíng) means unique forms.
  • 越境而至 - Means 'to come from beyond the borders' or 'to arrive from distant places.' '越境' (yuè jìng) means beyond the borders, and '至' (zhì) means to arrive.
  • 托神以尽变 - Means 'to rely on the supernatural to achieve complete transformation.' '托神' (tuō shén) means to rely on the supernatural, and '尽变' (jìn biàn) means complete transformation.
  • 因变以穷神 - Means 'to use transformation to exhaust the supernatural.' '因变' (yīn biàn) means to use transformation, and '穷神' (qióng shén) means to exhaust the supernatural.
  • 触象难名 - Means 'to touch upon phenomena that are hard to name.' '触象' (chù xiàng) means to touch upon phenomena, and '难名' (nán míng) means hard to name.
  • 灵怪莫测 - Means 'mysterious and unpredictable.' '灵怪' (líng guài) means mysterious and strange, and '莫测' (mò cè) means unpredictable.
  • 含雷吐火之术 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'the art of containing thunder and spewing fire.' '含雷' (hán léi) means to contain thunder, and '吐火' (tǔ huǒ) means to spew fire.
  • 出于万毕之家 - Means 'originating from the family of Wan Bi.' '出于' (chū yú) means originating from, and '万毕之家' (wàn bì zhī jiā) refers to a specific family.
  • 毳羽于洪炉 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'to place downy feathers in a great furnace.' '毳羽' (cuì yǔ) means downy feathers, and '洪炉' (hóng lú) means a great furnace.
  • 炎烟火于冰水 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'to place fire and smoke in ice water.' '炎烟火' (yán yān huǒ) means fire and smoke, and '冰水' (bīng shuǐ) means ice water.
  • 漏海螺船之属 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'ships that leak into the sea.' '漏海' (lòu hǎi) means to leak into the sea, and '螺船之属' (luó chuán zhī shǔ) means ships.
  • 飞珠沉霞之类 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'pearls flying and clouds sinking.' '飞珠' (fēi zhū) means pearls flying, and '沉霞' (chén xiá) means clouds sinking.
  • 千途万品 - Means 'thousands of paths and myriad kinds.' '千途' (qiān tú) means thousands of paths, and '万品' (wàn pǐn) means myriad kinds.
  • 书籍之所未详 - Means 'details not fully explained in books.' '书籍' (shū jí) means books, and '未详' (wèi xiáng) means not fully explained.
  • 自神化以来 - Means 'since the era of deification.' '自神化' (zì shén huà) means since the era of deification, and '以来' (yǐ lái) means since.
  • 神奇莫与为例 - Means 'miraculous and unmatched.' '神奇' (shén qí) means miraculous, and '莫与为例' (mò yǔ wéi lì) means unmatched.
  • 夭龄促知之所效哉 - Means 'how could they be imitated by those with short lives and limited knowledge?' '夭龄' (yāo líng) means short lives, '促知' (cù zhī) means limited knowledge, and '之所效哉' (zhī suǒ xiào zāi) means how could they be imitated.
  • 今观子年之记 - Means 'now, observing the records of Zi Nian.' '今观' (jīn guān) means now observing, and '子年之记' (zǐ nián zhī jì) means the records of Zi Nian.

  • 或以字音相类 - Means 'sometimes due to similar sounds of characters.' '或以' (huò yǐ) means sometimes due to, '字音' (zì yīn) means sounds of characters, and '相类' (xiāng lèi) means similar.
  • 或以土俗为殊 - Means 'sometimes due to different local customs.' '或以' (huò yǐ) means sometimes due to, '土俗' (tǔ sú) means local customs, and '为殊' (wéi shū) means different.
  • 验诸坟史 - Means 'to verify in ancient texts.' '验诸' (yàn zhū) means to verify, and '坟史' (fén shǐ) means ancient texts.
  • 岂惟秦、仪之见异者哉 - Means 'how could it be only that Qin and Yi were seen as different?' '岂惟' (qǐ wéi) means how could it be only, '秦、仪' (qín, yí) means Qin and Yi, '之见异者' (zhī jiàn yì zhě) means those seen as different, and '哉' (zāi) is an exclamation particle.

  • 三尺之剑 - A metaphorical reference to a sword, often symbolizing authority or power, reflecting the symbolic use of objects in Chinese culture.
  • 应天地之数 - Refers to the cosmic or divine order, reflecting the influence of Chinese cosmology on literature.
  • 三为阳数 - Refers to the number three as a yang (positive) number in Chinese cosmology, reflecting the importance of numbers in Chinese philosophy.
  • 应天地之德 - Refers to the virtues of heaven and earth, reflecting the moral and ethical dimensions of Chinese thought.
  • 按《钩命诀》曰 - Refers to the *Gouming Jue* (a classic text), indicating the reliance on authoritative sources in Chinese literature.
  • 萧何为昴星精 - Refers to Xiao He, a historical figure, as being the essence of the star Altair (昴星), reflecting the integration of historical and mythological figures in Chinese literature.
  • 项羽、陈胜、胡亥为三猾 - Refers to Xiang Yu, Chen Sheng, and Hu Hai as the three cunning or deceitful individuals, reflecting the moral judgments in Chinese historical narratives.
  • 国为木德，汉叶火位 - Refers to the Five Elements theory, where the state is governed by the virtue of wood, and the Han dynasty by the virtue of fire, reflecting the influence of the Five Elements on Chinese political and philosophical thought.
  • 孝惠帝二年 - Refers to the second year of Emperor Hui of Han's reign, providing a historical context for the text.
  • 车书同文轨 - Symbolizes a harmonized society, reflecting the ideal of unity in Chinese political thought.
  • 干戈偃息 - Indicates peace, reflecting the ideal of peace and stability in Chinese political thought.
  • 重译来贡 - Refers to distant foreigners coming bearing tribute, reflecting the cosmopolitan nature of the Han dynasty.
  • 自乳以来 - Indicates from the time of birth, reflecting the emphasis on the natural and the divine in Chinese mythology.
  • 灵毛自蔽 - Refers to a divine hair that provides protection, reflecting the belief in divine protection in Chinese mythology.
  • 居于深穴 - Refers to living in a deep cave, reflecting the natural and mystical settings in Chinese mythology.
  • 其寿不可测也 - Means their lifespan cannot be measured, reflecting the mystery and unpredictability of life in Chinese mythology.
  • 方士韩稚解绝国人言 - Refers to the Daoist Han Zhi understanding the language of the distant foreigners, reflecting the role of Daoists in Chinese mythology and folklore.
  • 令问人寿几何 - Means to ask how long their life is, reflecting the curiosity about the lifespan of mythical beings.
  • 经见几代之事 - Means to have seen events of several generations, reflecting the long lifespan and wisdom of mythical beings.
  • 五运相承 - Refers to the Five Elements (Wu Xing) theory, where each element gives way to the next, reflecting the cyclical nature of life and the universe in Chinese philosophy.
  • 迭生迭死 - Means alternately born and dying, reflecting the cyclical nature of life and death in Chinese philosophy.
  • 如飞尘细雨 - Means like flying dust and fine rain, describing something as fleeting or imperceptible, reflecting the transient nature of life in Chinese philosophy.
  • 存殁不可论算 - Means existence and death cannot be calculated, reflecting the unpredictability of life and death in Chinese philosophy.

  • 屑屑焉以相诛灭 - Constant warfare.
  • 浮靡嚣动 - Decadent and restless society.
  • 淫于礼，乱于乐 - Excessive and disorderly rituals and music.
  • 世德浇讹，淳风坠矣 - Moral values have deteriorated and pure customs have declined.
  • 蛇身已上 - A state beyond the physical world.
  • 八风均 - The eight winds are balanced, symbolizing harmony.
  • 四时序 - The four seasons are in order, representing natural balance.
  • 不以威悦揽乎精运 - The essence of the universe operates without the need for force or pleasure.
  • 自钻火变腥以来 - The time when fire was first used to cook food.
  • 父老而慈，子寿而孝 - Describes a harmonious family life.
  • 自轩皇以来 - Marks the beginning of the Yellow Emperor's era.

  • 以送泥离之使 - To send a messenger to Mudili.
  • 大赦天下 - A general amnesty was declared.

  • 自造歌曲 - Means 'composed his own songs.'
  • 使女伶歌之 - Means 'had a female singer perform it.'
  • 日已西倾 - Literally 'the sun has already tilted westward,' meaning 'the sun has set' or 'it is late in the day.'
  • 凉风激水 - Means 'cool wind stirs the water.'
  • 女伶歌声甚遒 - Means 'the female singer's voice was very strong' or 'the female singer's voice was very powerful.'
  • 赋《落叶哀蝉》之曲 - Means 'composed the song 'Falling Leaves and Mourning Cicadas.'
  • 袂兮无声 - Literally 'sleeves, oh, without sound,' a poetic expression describing the absence of movement.
  • 玉墀兮尘生 - Literally 'jade steps, oh, dust rises,' a metaphor for the desolation of a place.
  • 虚房冷而寂寞 - Means 'the empty room is cold and lonely.'
  • 落叶依于重扃 - Means 'fallen leaves cling to the heavy doors.'
  • 望彼美之女兮安得 - Means 'how can I see that beautiful woman again?'
  • 感余心之未宁 - Means 'my heart is still unsettled.'
  • 闷闷不自支持 - Means 'feeling depressed and unable to bear it.'
  • 命龙膏之灯以照舟内 - Means 'ordered a lamp made of dragon fat to illuminate the boat.'
  • 悲不自止 - Means 'cannot stop being sad.'
  • 觉帝容色愁怨 - Means 'noticed the emperor's face was filled with sorrow and resentment.'
  • 进洪梁之酒 - Means 'presented wine from Hongliang.'
  • 酌以文螺之卮 - Means 'served in a cup made of seashells.'
  • 卮出波祇之国 - Means 'the cups come from the country of Boqi.'
  • 酒出洪梁之县 - Means 'the wine comes from the county of Hongliang.'
  • 此属右扶风 - Means 'this belongs to the Right Commandery.'
  • 至哀帝废此邑 - Means 'until Emperor Ai abolished this town.'
  • 南人受此酿法 - Means 'southern people learned this brewing method.'
  • 今言“云阳出美酒” - Means 'now people say 'Yunyang produces fine wine.'
  • 两声相乱矣 - Means 'the two sounds have become confused.'
  • 色悦心欢 - Means 'his face brightened and his heart was joyful.'
  • 诏女伶出侍 - Means 'ordered the female singer to attend.'
  • 息于延凉室 - Means 'rested in the Cool Extension Room.'
  • 卧梦李夫人授帝蘅芜之香 - Means 'dreamed that Lady Li gave him the fragrance of *hengwu* (a type of herb).'
  • 涕泣洽席 - Means 'tears wet the mat.'
  • 改延凉室为遗芳梦室 - Means 'changed the Cool Extension Room to the Fragrance of Remembrance Room.'
  • 深嬖 - Means 'deeply favored' or 'deeply loved.'
  • 死后常思梦之 - Means 'often thought of and dreamed of her after her death.'
  • 或欲见夫人 - Means 'sometimes wanted to see her.'
  • 貌憔悴 - Means 'looked emaciated.'
  • 嫔御不宁 - Means 'the consorts were uneasy.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 诏李少君 - Formal command to summon Li Shaojun.
  • 与之语曰 - To speak with him and say...
  • 朕思李夫人，其可得见乎 - I miss Lady Li; can I see her again?
  • 可遥见，不可同于帷幄 - She can be seen from afar but not in the same room.
  • 暗海有潜英之石 - In the Dark Sea, there is a stone called the Hidden Pearl.
  • 其色青，轻如毛羽 - It is blue in color and as light as feathers.
  • 寒盛则石温，暑盛则石冷 - When it is cold, the stone is warm; when it is hot, the stone is cool.
  • 刻之为人像，神悟不异真人 - Carving it into a human likeness, it has a divine understanding no different from a real person.
  • 使此石像往，则夫人至矣 - If this stone figure goes, then Lady Li will come.
  • 此石人能传译人言语，有声无气，故知神异也 - This stone figure can translate human speech, having sound but no breath, thus proving its supernatural nature.
  • 愿得楼船百艘，巨力千人，能浮水登木者，皆使明于道术，赍不死之药 - I wish to have a hundred ships, a thousand strong men, and those who can swim and climb trees, all skilled in Daoist arts, bringing the elixir of immortality.
  • 乃至暗海，经十年而还 - They went to the Dark Sea and returned after ten years.
  • 昔之去人，或升云不归，或托形假死，获反者四五人 - Those who went before either ascended to the clouds and did not return, or took on false forms and pretended to die; only four or five returned.
  • 得此石，即命工人依先图刻作夫人形 - When they obtained the stone, they ordered the craftsmen to carve it according to the original design into the likeness of Lady Li.
  • 刻成，置于轻纱幕里，宛若生时 - Once carved, it was placed behind a sheer curtain, looking just like her when alive.
  • 譬如中宵忽梦，而昼可得近观乎 - It's like having a dream in the middle of the night; can you see it clearly during the day?
  • 此石毒，宜远望，不可逼也 - This stone is toxic; it's best to look at it from a distance and not get too close.
  • 勿轻万乘之尊，惑此精魅之物 - Do not underestimate the majesty of the emperor; be wary of this supernatural entity.
  • 帝乃从其谏 - The emperor then followed his advice.
  • 见夫人毕，少君乃使舂此石人为丸，服之，不复思梦 - After seeing Lady Li, Li Shaojun had the stone figure ground into pills, which were taken, and the emperor no longer dreamed of her.
  • 乃筑灵梦台，岁时祀之 - They then built a Spirit Dream Terrace and worshipped it regularly.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 元封元年 - The first year of the Yuánfēng era (110 BCE) during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han.
  • 浮忻国 - A fictional or lesser-known country, contributing to the exotic and mysterious nature of the narrative.
  • 兰金之泥 - A type of gold ore, possibly a metaphor for something valuable and rare.
  • 盛夏之时 - The hottest part of summer, setting the scene for the boiling springs.
  • 飞鸟不能过 - The intense heat, a common description in classical literature to emphasize extreme conditions.
  • 国人常见水边有人冶此金为器 - A common sight among the locals, reflecting the daily life and activities of the people.
  • 金状混混若泥 - The gold ore appears as if it were mud, emphasizing its form before processing.
  • 百铸，其色变白，有光如银 - The transformation process, highlighting the alchemical nature of the gold.
  • 银烛 - A term for the silver-like object, possibly a candle or a similar item made from the processed gold.
  • 封诸函匣及诸宫门 - Sealing various chests and palace doors, suggesting a protective or ceremonial use.
  • 鬼魅不敢干 - Ghosts and evil spirits dare not approach, implying a supernatural protection.
  • 上将出征及使绝国 - High-ranking officials going to war or on missions to distant lands, indicating significant events.
  • 多以此泥为玺封 - Often using this gold mud to seal imperial seals, highlighting its importance.
  • 卫青、张骞、苏武、傅介子之使 - Famous historical figures who were sent on missions, adding historical context.
  • 日南之南 - The southernmost part of the territory, indicating a distant and remote location.
  • 淫泉之浦 - A place where the water flows out of the ground, forming a pond or stream, with a suggestive name.
  • 浸淫从地而出成渊 - Water seeping out of the ground and forming a deep pool, describing the formation of the spring.
  • 小处可滥觞褰涉 - Small areas can be easily crossed, suggesting the shallow parts of the water.
  • 大处可方舟沿泝 - Larger areas can accommodate boats, indicating the navigability of the water.
  • 随流屈直 - Following the course of the water, which can be straight or winding.
  • 激石之声，似人之歌笑 - The sound of water hitting rocks is like human singing and laughing, creating a vivid auditory image.
  • 闻者令人淫动 - Those who hear it feel a sense of desire, adding a moral or cautionary note.
  • 秦破骊山之坟 - The time when the Qin dynasty destroyed the mausoleum of Emperor Qin Shi Huang, a significant historical event.
  • 行野者见金凫向南而飞 - A traveler saw a golden duck flying south, leading to a discovery.
  • 宝鼎元年 - The first year of the Bǎodǐng era (148 CE) during the reign of Emperor Huan of Han.
  • 张善为日南太守 - Zhang Shan served as the governor of Rìnán, providing a specific historical context.
  • 郡民有得金凫以献 - A local resident found and presented the golden duck, continuing the narrative.
  • 张善该博德通 - Zhang Shan was knowledgeable and virtuous, adding character depth.
  • 考其年月，即秦始皇墓之金凫也 - Determining the date and confirming that it was the golden duck from the tomb of Emperor Qin Shi Huang, providing a historical link.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 昔始皇为冢 - Refers to the past, specifically to the time of Qin Shi Huang, and describes the construction of his tomb.
  • 敛天下瑰异 - Means to gather rare and precious things from all over the world.
  • 生殉工人 - Refers to the practice of burying living workers alive with the deceased.
  • 倾远方奇宝于冢中 - Means to fill the tomb with rare and exotic treasures from distant lands.
  • 为江海川渎及列山岳之形 - Describes the tomb's interior as being modeled after rivers, seas, lakes, and mountains.
  • 以沙棠沉檀为舟楫 - Means to use sandalwood and fragrant wood to make boats and paddles.
  • 金银为凫雁 - Means to use gold and silver to make ducks and geese.
  • 以琉璃杂宝为龟鱼 - Means to use glass and various precious materials to make turtles and fish.
  • 又于海中作玉象鲸鱼，衔火珠为星，以代膏烛，光出墓中 - Describes the creation of jade elephants and whales in the sea, holding fire pearls as stars to replace oil lamps, with light shining out of the tomb.
  • 精灵之伟也 - Means the greatness of the spirit or the grandeur of the spiritual aspect.
  • 昔生埋工人于冢内，至被开时，皆不死 - Refers to the workers who were buried alive but survived when the tomb was opened.
  • 工人于冢内琢石为龙凤仙人之像，及作碑文辞赞 - Means the workers carved stone images of dragons, phoenixes, immortals, and wrote inscriptions and praises.
  • 汉初发此冢，验诸史传，皆无列仙龙凤之制 - Means that when the tomb was opened at the beginning of the Han dynasty, it was checked against historical records, and there were no records of such divine figures.
  • 后人更写此碑文，而辞多怨酷之言，乃谓为“怨碑” - Means that later people rewrote the inscription, which contained many words of resentment and cruelty, and it was called the 'Resentment Stele.'
  • 《史记》略而不录 - Means that the *Records of the Grand Historian* (Shiji) briefly mentioned it but did not record it in detail.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 始皇 - Refers to Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of China.
  • 汉初 - Refers to the early years of the Han dynasty.
  • 《史记》 - Refers to the *Records of the Grand Historian*, a historical text compiled by Sima Qian.
  • 列仙龙凤 - Refers to divine figures such as immortals, dragons, and phoenixes, which are common in Chinese mythology.
  • 怨碑 - Refers to the 'Resentment Stele,' a term used to describe an inscription that expresses resentment or criticism.

Proper Nouns

  • 始皇 - Qin Shi Huang.
  • 汉初 - Early Han dynasty.
  • 《史记》 - *Records of the Grand Historian*.
  • 怨碑 - Resentment Stele.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 董偃常卧延清之室 - Idiomatic expression indicating a habitual action in a specific location.
  • 以画石为床，文如锦也 - Descriptive phrase emphasizing the luxurious nature of the bed.
  • 石体甚轻，出郅支国 - Factual statement about the origin and properties of the stone.
  • 上设紫琉璃帐，火齐屏风 - Descriptive phrase highlighting the luxurious setting.
  • 列灵麻之烛，以紫玉为盘，如屈龙，皆用杂宝饰之 - Detailed description of the luxurious furnishings.
  • 侍者于户外扇偃 - Descriptive phrase indicating the attentive service provided.
  • 玉石岂须扇而后凉耶？ - Rhetorical question emphasizing the natural coolness of the materials.
  • 侍者乃却扇，以手摸，方知有屏风 - Sequence of actions revealing the presence of a screen.
  • 又以玉精为盘，贮冰于膝前 - Descriptive phrase highlighting the cooling effect of the ice.
  • 玉精与冰同其洁澈 - Comparative statement emphasizing the clarity of the materials.
  • 侍者谓冰之无盘，必融湿席，乃合玉盘拂之，落阶下，冰玉俱碎，偃以为乐 - Sequence of actions leading to an amusing outcome.
  • 此玉精，千涂国所贡也 - Factual statement about the origin of the jade plate.
  • 武帝以此赐偃 - Factual statement about the gift.
  • 哀、平之世，民家犹有此器，而多残破 - Historical statement about the condition of the item.
  • 及王莽之世，不复知其所在 - Historical statement about the loss of the item.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 董偃 - Historical figure, likely a favorite of Emperor Wu of Han.
  • 延清 - Likely a person or a place, possibly a palace or a specific room.
  • 郅支国 - Ancient kingdom, possibly referring to the kingdom of Zhizhi.
  • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han, a powerful and influential ruler.
  • 哀、平之世 - Reigns of Emperors Ai and Ping of Han, marking the end of the Western Han dynasty.
  • 王莽之世 - Reign of Wang Mang, who established the Xin dynasty and briefly overthrew the Han dynasty.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 四足一尾，鸣则俱鸣 - Describes the unique physical characteristics of the chicken: four feet and one tail, and it would crow together when both heads did.
  • 《诗》云：‘牝鸡无晨。’一云：‘牝鸡之晨，惟家之索。’ - Quotes from the *Book of Songs*: 'A hen does not crow at dawn.' Another version says, 'If a hen crows at dawn, it is a sign of household decline.'
  • 三七末世，鸡不鸣，犬不吠，宫中荆刺乱相系，当有九虎争为帝 - Predicts a chaotic end times scenario where chickens do not crow, dogs do not bark, thorns and brambles grow in the palace, and nine tigers will vie for the throne.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 太初二年 - Refers to the second year of the Tai' An era during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han (141 BCE).
  • 大月氏国 - Refers to the Great Yuezhi, an ancient nomadic people who later established the Kushan Empire.
  • 武帝 - Refers to Emperor Wu of Han, a powerful and influential ruler known for his military campaigns and cultural achievements.
  • 甘泉故馆 - Refers to the Old Pavilion of Ganquan, a place in the Han capital where the emperor might have kept exotic animals.
  • 王莽篡位 - Refers to Wang Mang's usurpation of the Han throne, marking the end of the Western Han dynasty and the beginning of the Xin dynasty.
  • 九虎 - Refers to the title 'Nine Tigers' used by Wang Mang's generals, symbolizing their power and authority.
  • 天汉 - Refers to the Milky Way, often used in Chinese literature as a symbol of the heavens or the celestial realm.
  • 鹍鸡 - A type of bird, possibly a mythical or rare bird, known for its distinctive call.
  • 暄鸡 - Another name for the chicken, possibly derived from its call or appearance.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 继六国之遗弊 - Following the legacy of the six states' misfortunes; refers to the decline of the Warring States period.
  • 黔黎嗟秦亡之晚 - The common people lamented the late fall of Qin; '黔黎' refers to the common people.
  • 恨汉来之迟 - Regretted the tardiness of the Han dynasty's arrival.
  • 肇基帝业 - Laid the foundation for imperial rule; indicates the establishment of the Han dynasty.
  • 恢张区宇 - Expanded the territory; refers to the expansion of the empire.
  • 务宽刑辟 - Focused on lenient laws; describes the policy of leniency in governance.
  • 无为之治 - Rule of non-action; a reference to the Daoist philosophy of minimal intervention.
  • 德侔三王 - Moral virtue equal to the three kings; refers to the moral achievements of the early Chinese rulers.
  • 教通四海 - Teachings spread throughout the world; indicates the widespread influence of the Han dynasty.
  • 世载愈光 - The legacy became even more illustrious; describes the increasing glory of the Han dynasty.
  • 省方巡岳 - Inspecting the provinces and visiting the mountains; refers to imperial tours.
  • 标元崇号 - Establishing the supreme title; indicates the elevation of the emperor's status.
  • 闻礼乐以恢风 - Promoting rites and music to revitalize the customs; emphasizes the role of culture in governance.
  • 广文义以饰俗 - Expanding the meaning of literature to adorn the customs; highlights the importance of literature in shaping society.
  • 改律历而建封禅 - Reforming the calendar and laws and establishing the Fengshan sacrifices; refers to significant religious and political rituals.
  • 祀百神以招群瑞 - Worshipping all the gods to attract various blessings; indicates the practice of religious ceremonies to bring good fortune.
  • 周、孔之教 - The teachings of Zhou and Confucius; refers to the moral and ethical teachings of these figures.
  • 不贵虚无之学 - Do not value the study of the void; indicates a rejection of Daoist philosophy.
  • 修黄老 - Study the teachings of Huang-Lao; refers to the combination of Daoist and Legalist philosophies.
  • 治却老之方 - Seek methods to avoid aging; indicates the pursuit of longevity practices.
  • 求报无福之祀 - Seek blessings from rituals that lack merit; suggests the futility of certain religious practices.
  • 张敞切言 - Zhang Chang spoke frankly; refers to a historical figure known for his direct advice.
  • 使远斥仙术 - To distance oneself from the art of immortality; indicates a rejection of Daoist practices.
  • 指以苌弘、楚襄怀、秦皇、徐福之事 - Pointing to the examples of Chang Hong, Chu Xianghui, Qin Shi Huang, and Xu Fu; refers to historical figures associated with Daoist practices.
  • 辛垣之徒 - The followers of Xin Yuan; refers to those who supported Daoist practices.
  • 卒见夷戮 - Ultimately, they were executed; indicates the punishment of those who supported Daoist practices.
  • 仙者 - Immortals; refers to those who seek immortality.
  • 尚冲静以忘形体 - Value tranquility and forget the body; indicates the Daoist ideal of detachment from the physical world.
  • 守寂寞而袪嚣务 - Maintain solitude and eliminate worldly affairs; emphasizes the importance of inner peace and detachment from material concerns.
  • 好微行而尚克伐 - Favored secret travels and valued conquests; indicates the emperor's preference for covert activities and military achievements.
  • 恢宫宇而广苑囿 - Expanded the palace and gardens; refers to the construction and expansion of imperial buildings.
  • 永乖长生久视之法 - Forever deviated from the method of long life and eternal vision; indicates the failure to achieve immortality.
  • 失玄一守道之要 - Lost the essence of adhering to the Dao; suggests a departure from the fundamental principles of Daoism.
  • 悔少翁之先诛 - Regretted the early execution of Shao Weng; refers to a historical figure who was executed for his Daoist practices.
  • 惑栾大之诡说 - Deceived by the deceptive words of Luan Da; indicates the emperor's susceptibility to false promises of immortality.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 汉兴 - The rise of the Han dynasty; marks the beginning of a new era in Chinese history.
  • 六国之遗弊 - The legacy of the Warring States period; characterized by political fragmentation and social unrest.
  • 黔黎 - The common people; often used to describe the general population.
  • 高祖 - Emperor Gaozu of Han; the founder of the Han dynasty.
  • 孝惠 - Emperor Hui of Han; the second emperor of the Han dynasty, known for his lenient policies.
  • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han; the seventh emperor of the Han dynasty, known for his military campaigns and cultural achievements.
  • 周、孔之教 - The teachings of Confucius and other sages; emphasizing moral and ethical conduct.
  • 黄老 - The combined teachings of Huangdi (Yellow Emperor) and Laozi; representing Daoist philosophy.
  • 苌弘、楚襄怀、秦皇、徐福 - Historical figures associated with Daoist practices and the pursuit of immortality.
  • 辛垣 - A historical figure known for supporting Daoist practices.
  • 少翁 - A historical figure who was executed for his Daoist practices.
  • 栾大 - A historical figure who deceived Emperor Wu of Han with false promises of immortality.

Proper Nouns

  • 汉 - The Han dynasty.
  • 高祖 - Emperor Gaozu of Han.

  • 虽未及玄真 - Means 'although not reaching the level of supreme truth.'
  • 颇参神邃 - Means 'to some extent, it participates in the profound mysteries.'

  • 验以众说 - Means 'to verify with various sayings.'
  • 未有异于斯乎 - Means 'there is nothing different from this.'
  • 夫五运递兴 - Means 'the five elements take turns in rise.'
  • 数之常理 - Means 'the constant principle of numbers.'
  • 金、土之兆 - Refers to the signs of the Metal and Earth elements.
  • 魏、晋当焉 - Means 'Wei and Jin dynasties should be here.'
  • 董偃起自贩珠之徒 - Means 'Dong Yan rose from a pearl-selling background.'
  • 因庖宰而升宠 - Means 'he gained favor through the kitchen and dining hall.'
  • 窃幸一时 - Means 'to secretly enjoy a momentary favor.'
  • 富倾海宇 - Means 'wealth that could overturn the world.'
  • 内蓄神异之珍 - Means 'to possess within rare and mystical treasures.'
  • 衒非世之宝 - Means 'to display treasures that are not of this world.'
  • 一朝绝爱 - Means 'one day, the favor was cut off.'
  • 信盛衰之有兆乎 - Means 'is it a sign of the rise and fall?'
  • 夫为棺椁者 - Means 'the purpose of making coffins.'
  • 以防蝼蚁之患 - Means 'to prevent the harm of ants and termites.'
  • 权敛骨之离 - Means 'to temporarily gather the bones.'
  • 圣人使合其正礼 - Means 'the sage commands the proper rites.'
  • 恶其逾费 - Means 'dislikes excessive spending.'
  • 疾其过薄 - Means 'dislikes being too thin.'
  • 至如澹台灭明之俭 - Means 'as for Dan Tai Mie Ming's frugality.'
  • 盛姬、秦皇之奢 - Refers to the extravagance of King Sheng Ji and Emperor Qin Shi Huang.
  • 皆失于节用 - Means 'all miss the point of moderation.'
  • 嗟乎 - Interjection expressing sorrow or regret.
  • 形销神灭 - Means 'the body decays and the spirit perishes.'
  • 欻为一棺之土 - Means 'suddenly becomes the soil of a coffin.'
  • 为陵成谷 - Means 'becomes a hill and then a valley.'
  • 琼珣美宝 - Refers to precious jade and beautiful treasures.
  • 奄为烬尘 - Means 'suddenly becomes ashes and dust.'
  • 斯则费生加死 - Means 'this is to waste life and add to death.'
  • 无益身名也 - Means 'it is of no benefit to one's reputation.'
  • 冥然长往 - Means 'to go away forever in darkness.'
  • 何忆曩时之盛 - Means 'how can one remember the past glory?'
  • 仲尼云 - Means 'Confucius said.'
  • 不如速朽 - Means 'it is better to decay quickly.'
  • 敛手足形 - Means 'to gather the hands and feet.'
  • 圣人以斯昭诫 - Means 'the sage uses this as a warning.'
  • 岂不尚哉 - Means 'isn't it commendable?'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 李夫人 - Refers to Lady Li, a concubine of Emperor Wu of Han, known for her beauty and the emperor's deep affection for her.
  • 董偃 - A historical figure who rose from a lowly background to gain favor at the court.
  • 澹台灭明 - A historical figure known for his frugality.
  • 盛姬 - Refers to King Sheng Ji, a historical figure known for his extravagance.
  • 秦皇 - Refers to Emperor Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of China, known for his extravagance.
  • 五运 - Refers to the Five Elements (Wu Xing) in Chinese cosmology, which are Metal, Wood, Water, Fire, and Earth.
  • 魏、晋 - Refers to the Wei and Jin dynasties, which followed the Han dynasty.
  • 仲尼 - Refers to Confucius, the great philosopher and teacher of ancient China.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 尚淫奢 - Compound verb-noun construction meaning 'to indulge in luxury and sensuality.' Reflects moral judgment of the emperor's behavior.
  • 多进谄佞 - '进' means 'to recommend or bring forward,' while '谄佞' refers to flatterers or sycophants. The phrase means 'he frequently brought in flatterers.'
  • 竞以妆饰妖丽，巧言取容 - '竞' means 'compete in,' and '取容' is a classical expression meaning 'to win favor.' The whole phrase can be rendered as 'competed in adorning themselves with妖丽 (妖艳美丽) and used flattering words to gain favor.'
  • 飘若蝉翼 - Poetic simile meaning 'floating like a cicada’s wing.' Classical metaphor for something very light and delicate.
  • 示用奢带修裙 - '奢带' and '修裙' are archaic terms for luxurious sashes and long skirts. The phrase implies showing off luxurious clothing.
  • 宫人皆效其断袖 - '效' means 'imitate,' and '断袖' is a term from the famous story of Emperor Ai and Dong Xian, where a sleeve was cut to avoid disturbing the emperor during sleep. This is a reference to a homosexual relationship in ancient China.
  • 割袖恐惊其眠 - Direct explanation of the '断袖' incident. It means 'cutting the sleeve to avoid waking him during sleep.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 哀帝 - Emperor Ai of the Western Han Dynasty (r. 7 BC – 1 AD). Known for his relationship with Dong Xian, a famous anecdote in Chinese history.
  • 董贤 - A high-ranking official and favorite of Emperor Ai. His story is well-known in Chinese history and literature, and his '断袖' incident is a classic symbol of imperial favor and homosexual relationships.
  • 明帝 - Emperor Ming of the Eastern Han Dynasty (r. 57–75 AD). Known for his virtuous rule and the story of the 'Qionglong' (穹隆) fruit.
  • 穹隆 - A mythical fruit said to be from the mountain of Kunlun, associated with immortality and the Queen Mother of Heaven (西王母). The name itself is poetic and symbolic.
  • 西王母 - A central figure in Daoist mythology, associated with longevity and immortality.
  • 霜林园 - A garden where cold-resistant plants were cultivated. The term '霜林' (frost forest) suggests a place where frost and cold are present, possibly a garden designed for special plants.
  • 相陵 - A term that seems to be a phonetic variant of '霜林' (shuāng lín), possibly due to the pronunciation of the character '相' (xiāng) being similar to '霜' (shuāng). This is a possible homophonic error or a dialectal variation in the original text.
  • The term '四劫一实' refers to a concept from Buddhist cosmology, where '劫' (jié) refers to an eon or a cosmic cycle. So 'four eons, one fruit' suggests the fruit is extremely rare and valuable.

Proper Nouns

  • 哀帝 - Should be translated consistently as 'Emperor Ai of Han.'
  • 董贤 - A proper name, should be transliterated as 'Dong Xian.'
  • 明帝 - Should be translated as 'Emperor Ming of Han.'
  • 阴贵人 - A title meaning 'Lady Yin,' referring to a concubine or noblewoman. Should be translated as 'Lady Yin.'
  • 炖煌 - A place name, transliterated as 'Dunhuang.'
  • 恒山 - A mountain and place name, transliterated as 'Hengshan.'
  • 西王母 - A title, should be translated as 'Queen Mother of Heaven.'
  • 霜林园 - A proper noun, should be transliterated as 'Shuanglin Garden.'

  • 有獻驯鹊 - There was a tame magpie offered.
  • 若有喜樂事，則鼓翼翔鳴 - If there is a joyful event, it flaps its wings and flies and cries.
  • 按庄周云“雕陵之鹊” - According to Zhuangzi, the 'Qiao Ling magpie.'
  • 盖其类也 - It is of the same kind.
  • 《淮南子》云：“鹊知人喜。” - The *Huainanzi* says, 'The magpie knows human joy.'
  • 今之所记，大小虽殊，远近为异 - What is recorded now, though different in size and distance, is the same in essence.
  • 故略举焉 - Therefore, I will briefly mention it.
  • 安帝好微行 - Emperor An enjoyed secret travels.
  • 于郊垧或露宿 - In the outskirts or slept in the open air.
  • 起帷宫，皆用锦罽文绣 - Set up tents made of brocade, felt, and embroidered fabrics.
  • 至永初三年，国用不足 - By the third year of Yongchu, the state treasury was insufficient.
  • 令吏民入钱者得为官 - Ordered officials and commoners to contribute money to become officials.
  • 有琅琊王溥，即王吉之后 - There was Wang Pu of Langya, the descendant of Wang Ji.
  • 吉先为昌邑中尉 - Wang Ji was first the Zhongwei of Changyi.
  • 溥奕世衰凌 - Wang Pu's family declined for generations.
  • 及安帝时，家贫不得仕 - By the time of Emperor An, he was poor and could not become an official.
  • 乃挟竹简插笔，于洛阳市佣书 - He then carried bamboo slips and a brush, working as a copyist in the market of Luoyang.
  • 美于形貌，又多文辞 - He was handsome in appearance and had many literary skills.
  • 僦其书者，丈夫赠其衣冠，妇人遗其珠玉 - Those who hired him to copy books, men gave him clothes, and women gave him jewels.
  • 一日之中，衣宝盈车而归 - In one day, he returned with a carriage full of clothes and treasures.
  • 积粟于廪，九族宗亲，莫不仰其衣食 - He stored grain in granaries, and his nine clans and relatives all depended on his food and clothing.
  • 洛阳称为善笔而得富 - In Luoyang, he was called a good copyist and became wealthy.
  • 溥先时家贫，穿井得铁印 - Wang Pu, when he was poor, dug a well and found an iron seal.
  • 铭曰：“佣力得富，钱至亿庾。一土三田，军门主簿。” - The inscription said, 'Through labor, one gains wealth, with money reaching the billions. One earth, three fields, military gate clerk.'
  • 后以一亿钱输官，得中垒校尉 - Later, he paid one billion coins to the government and became the Commander of the Central Camp.
  • 三田一土，“垒”字也 - Three fields and one earth, the character for 'camp'.
  • 中垒校尉掌北军垒门，故曰军门主簿 - The Commander of the Central Camp oversees the gates of the northern army camp, hence the title 'military gate clerk'.
  • 积善降福，神明报焉 - Accumulated virtue brings blessings, and the spirits repay him.

  • 凉风起兮日照渠 - Cool winds rise, and the sun shines on the canal, describing the scene.

  • 清丝流管歌玉凫 - Clear strings and flowing pipes sing the jade duck, describing the musical instruments and the song.
  • 千年万岁喜难逾 - For a thousand years and ten thousand ages, joy is hard to surpass, expressing the enduring nature of joy.

  • 亦云月出则舒也 - Also said to open when the moon rises, providing another name for the lotus.
  • 故曰“望舒荷” - Thus called 'Moon-Opening Lotus,' giving the lotus another name.

  • 三六以下 - Means 'aged eighteen or younger,' again using a traditional method of age calculation.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 有闻于内则 - Refers to being well-known for internal virtues or moral conduct, especially in the context of a queen or empress. '内则' (nèi zé) is a classical term from the *Book of Rites* (*Liji*), referring to the moral code for women within the household.
  • 惟觉脚下如有乘践 - Poetic expression suggesting that the empress felt as though she were being carried or supported by something unseen, likely interpreted as divine or supernatural assistance.
  • 刮玉钗以覆于疮 - '刮玉钗' (guā yù chāi) means to scrape a jade hairpin, which was a common accessory for women. Here, the empress uses it to clean or cover a wound, indicating her resourcefulness and care.
  • 以泪湔帝衣及面，洁静如浣 - '湔' (jiān) means to wash or rinse. The phrase suggests that her tears washed the emperor's clothes and face, making them clean as if they had been washed. This is a poetic and metaphorical expression, likely emphasizing her emotional purity and devotion.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 神物之助焉 - The phrase suggests divine or supernatural aid, which is common in classical Chinese narratives to highlight the heroism or virtue of a character.
  • 幽祇之所感矣 - '幽祇' (yōu qí) refers to the spirits of the unseen or the heavens. This is a classical term used to denote the spiritual or supernatural realm. The phrase means that the empress's sincerity moved the spirits or heaven itself.

Proper Nouns

  • 伏皇后 - A member of the Fu clan, who were prominent in the Han court. She is portrayed here as a virtuous and capable woman, in contrast to the chaos of the times.
  • 李?霍 - The character '?' is likely a corruption or missing character. Based on historical context, the most likely name is Li Jue (李傕), a warlord who opposed the Han court and participated in the siege of Luoyang.
  • 玉钗 - A specific object used in the narrative; should be translated consistently as 'jade hairpin.'

  • 夫丹石可磨，而不可奪其堅色；蘭桂可折，而不可掩其貞芳 - Parallel structure comparing the unchangeable nature of jade and stones to the unyielding fragrance of orchids and cassia, emphasizing the inherent qualities of a person.
  • 伏後履純明之姿，懷忠亮之質，臨危授命，壯夫未能加焉 - Sequence describing Fu Hou's qualities and her courageous actions, with '臨危授命' being a key phrase that emphasizes her bravery.
  • 知死不吝，馮媛之儔也 - Phrase comparing Fu Hou's bravery to that of Feng Yuan, using '儔' to indicate similarity.
  • 求之千古，亦所罕聞 - Phrase emphasizing the rarity of such examples throughout history.
  • 漢興，至於哀、平、元、成，尚以宮室，崇苑囿 - Sequence describing the early Han Dynasty and the construction of palaces and gardens.
  • 而西京始有弘侈，東都繼其繁奢 - Phrase contrasting the grandeur of the Western Capital with the continued luxury of the Eastern Capital.

  • 孝哀廣四時之房，靈帝修裸遊之館 - Phrase describing the specific constructions of Emperor Ai and Emperor Ling.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 累金數億 - Literally 'accumulated gold in the tens of millions,' used to describe immense wealth.
  • 家僮四百余人 - Literally 'over four hundred domestic servants,' indicating a large household staff.
  • 以黃金為器 - Literally 'using gold as vessels,' meaning that gold was used to make household items.
  • 工冶之聲，震于都鄙 - Literally 'the sound of smelting and forging, shaking the capital and its suburbs,' describing the loud noise of metalworking.
  • 郭氏之室，不雨而雷 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'De's house, where thunder roars without rain,' symbolizing the intense sound of metalworking.
  • 庭中起高閣長庑 - Literally 'a high pavilion and long corridor were built in the courtyard,' describing the grand architecture of the house.
  • 置衡石于其上，以稱量珠玉也 - Literally 'a balance and weights were placed on it to weigh pearls and jade,' indicating the use of the pavilion for weighing precious items.
  • 閣下有藏金窟 - Literally 'under the pavilion there is a hidden treasure vault,' referring to a secret storage place for gold.
  • 列武士以衛之 - Literally 'arranging warriors to guard it,' indicating the security measures in place.
  • 錯雜寶以飾台榭 - Literally 'adorn the pavilions and terraces with a mix of treasures,' describing the lavish decoration of the house.
  • 懸明珠于四垂 - Literally 'hanging pearls at the four corners,' indicating the luxurious decoration of the house.
  • 昼視之如星，夜望之如月 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'in the day it looks like stars, at night it looks like the moon,' describing the brilliance of the pearls.
  • 里語曰：“洛阳多钱郭氏室，夜日昼星富无匹。” - A folk saying meaning 'In Luoyang, the De household has more money than anyone else; at night it's like the moon, in the day it's like stars, unmatched in wealth.'
  • 其宠者皆以玉器盛食 - Literally 'those favored ones all use jade vessels to serve food,' indicating the luxurious lifestyle of the household.
  • 琼厨金穴 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'jade kitchen and gold cave,' referring to the luxurious and wealthy lifestyle of the De household.
  • 小心畏慎 - Literally 'careful and cautious,' describing De Kuang's character.
  • 闭门优游 - Literally 'shutting the door and living leisurely,' indicating a life of seclusion and ease.
  • 未曾干世事 - Literally 'never involved in worldly affairs,' indicating De Kuang's disinterest in politics.
  • 为一时之智也 - Literally 'to be wise for his time,' indicating that De Kuang's behavior was considered wise by contemporary standards.
