Idiomatic Expressions

  • 夫后族之盛 - Formal expression meaning 'the prosperity of the maternal family,' introducing the topic.
  • 专挟内主之威 - Means 'exclusively wielding the power of the inner ruler,' referring to the influence of the emperor's mother or consort.
  • 党嬖强盛 - Means 'favored and powerful,' describing the rise of a family through favoritism.
  • 肆嚣于天下 - Means 'to spread arrogance throughout the world,' indicating the family's overbearing behavior.
  • 妖幸侵政 - Means 'evil luck or fortune intrudes into politics,' referring to the interference of a favored family in state affairs.
  • 擅椒房之亲 - Means 'to monopolize the closeness of the imperial harem,' highlighting the family's privileged position.
  • 在昔魏冉，富倾嬴国 - Refers to the historical example of Wei Ran, who was so wealthy that his riches could tip the balance of the Qin state.
  • 汉世王凤，同拜五侯 - Refers to Wang Feng of the Han dynasty, who was granted the title of Duke and had five brothers who were also ennobled.
  • 馆第僭于京都 - Means 'mansions僭越 in the capital,' indicating that the family's residences were excessive and inappropriate.
  • 嫱姬丽于宫掖 - Means 'beautiful concubines in the palace,' referring to the family's influence over the imperial harem.
  • 瑰赂南金，弥玩于王府 - Means 'precious gifts and southern gold, endlessly enjoyed in the royal treasury,' describing the family's lavish spending.
  • 缇绣雕文，被饰于土木 - Means 'embroidered brocades and carved decorations, adorning buildings,' referring to the family's opulent lifestyle.
  • 高廓洞门，极夏屋之盛 - Means 'high and spacious halls, reaching the height of summer houses' splendor,' describing the grandeur of the family's residences.
  • 文马朱轩，穷车服之靡 - Means 'ornate horses and red carriages, exhausting the luxury of vehicles and attire,' referring to the family's extravagant transportation and clothing.
  • 自古擅骄，未有如斯之例 - Means 'since ancient times, no one has been so arrogant,' emphasizing the uniqueness of the family's behavior.
  • 虽三归移于管室，八佾陈于季庭 - Refers to historical examples of excessive behavior, such as Duke Zhuang of Zheng having three wives and Duke Zhao of Lu arranging an eight-row dance in his courtyard.
  • 方之为劣矣 - Means 'compared to these, they are inferior,' indicating that even these historical examples are less severe.
  • 郭况内凭姻宠，外专声厉 - Means 'Guo Kuang relied on his marital status for favor and was known for his strictness,' describing the family's influence and reputation.
  • 远采山丹之穴，积陶朱、程郑之产 - Means 'he gathered wealth from distant places and accumulated the riches of Tao Zhu and Cheng Zheng,' referring to the family's extensive wealth.
  • 未足称其盛欤 - Means 'is this enough to describe their prosperity?' questioning the adequacy of the description.
  • 曾不恃其戚里，矜其财势 - Means 'he did not rely on his family connections or boast about his wealth and power,' indicating humility.
  • 秉温恭之正，守道持盈 - Means 'he upheld the virtue of warmth and respect, and maintained the path while holding onto abundance,' describing the family's moral stance.
  • 而自竞慎 - Means 'and was cautious in self-competing,' indicating self-restraint.
  • 是可谓知几其神乎 - Means 'this can be called a divine understanding of the signs,' praising the family's insight.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 魏冉 - Refers to Wei Ran, a powerful minister of the Qin state during the Warring States period.
  • 嬴国 - Refers to the Qin state, which eventually unified China.
  • 王凤 - Refers to Wang Feng, a powerful minister of the Western Han dynasty.
  • 五侯 - Refers to the five brothers of Wang Feng who were ennobled.
  • 椒房 - Refers to the imperial harem, named after the spiced rooms where concubines lived.
  • 三归 - Refers to Duke Zhuang of Zheng having three wives, an example of excessive behavior.
  • 八佾 - Refers to an eight-row dance, which was only allowed for the Son of Heaven in ancient Chinese ritual.
  • 季庭 - Refers to Duke Zhao of Lu, who arranged an eight-row dance in his courtyard, another example of excessive behavior.
  • 郭况 - Refers to Guo Kuang, a member of the Guo family, a prominent maternal family during the Eastern Han dynasty.
  • 山丹之穴 - Refers to distant and valuable resources.
  • 陶朱、程郑 - Refers to Tao Zhu and Cheng Zheng, famous merchants in ancient China.

Proper Nouns

  • 魏冉 - Wei Ran
  • 嬴国 - Qin state
  • 王凤 - Wang Feng
  • 五侯 - Five Dukes
  • 椒房 - Imperial harem
  • 三归 - Three wives of Duke Zhuang of Zheng
  • 八佾 - Eight-row dance
  • 季庭 - Duke Zhao of Lu
  • 郭况 - Guo Kuang
  • 山丹之穴 - Distant and valuable resources
  • 陶朱、程郑 - Tao Zhu and Cheng Zheng

[101]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 校書天禄阁 - Act of cataloging or editing books in the Tielu Pavilion, a library during the Han Dynasty.
  • 专精覃思 - Focus one's mind deeply and think profoundly.
  • 着黄衣，植青藜杖 - An elderly man wearing a yellow robe and holding a staff made of green calamus.
  • 吹杖端，烟燃 - A magical or supernatural act where the elder blows on the end of his staff, causing it to emit smoke and light.
  • 开辟已前 - Time before the creation of the world, often used in Chinese mythology.
  • 裂裳及绅 - Tear one's clothes and sash, a gesture of urgency or importance.
  • 金卯之子 - A person born in the year of the Metal Rabbit, according to the Chinese zodiac.
  • 太一之精 - The essence of Taiyi, a supreme deity in Chinese mythology.
  • 怀中竹牒 - A bamboo slip or tablet, often used for writing or carrying documents.
  • 天文地图之书 - Books containing knowledge of astronomy and geography.
  • 略授子焉 - Briefly impart knowledge to someone.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 刘向 - Liu Xiang, a prominent scholar and historian during the Han Dynasty, known for his work in cataloging and editing classical texts.
  • 成帝 - Emperor Cheng of Han, the ruler during whose reign Liu Xiang worked.
  • 天禄阁 - Tielu Pavilion, a library or study hall during the Han Dynasty, where scholars worked on cataloging and editing texts.
  • 太一 - Taiyi, a supreme deity in Chinese mythology, often associated with the cosmos and the origin of the universe.
  • 洪范五行 - The 'Great Pattern of the Five Elements,' a concept in Chinese cosmology and philosophy.
  • 竹牒 - Bamboo slips or tablets, commonly used for writing in ancient China.
  • 天文地图 - Ancient Chinese texts and maps that contained knowledge of the stars and geography.

Proper Nouns

  • 刘向 - Liu Xiang, a scholar and historian during the Han Dynasty.
  • 成帝 - Emperor Cheng of Han, the ruler during whose reign Liu Xiang worked.
  • 天禄阁 - Tielu Pavilion, a library or study hall during the Han Dynasty.
  • 太一 - Taiyi, a supreme deity in Chinese mythology.
  • 洪范五行 - The 'Great Pattern of the Five Elements,' a concept in Chinese cosmology and philosophy.
  • 竹牒 - Bamboo slips or tablets, commonly used for writing in ancient China.
  • 天文地图 - Ancient Chinese texts and maps that contained knowledge of the stars and geography.

[102]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 明惠过人 - Means 'exceedingly bright and wise,' a classical expression for exceptional intelligence.
  • 未尝有教者入门 - Means 'never had a teacher come into our home,' referring to the lack of formal education.
  • 剥庭中桑皮以为牒 - Means 'peeled the bark from the mulberry tree in the courtyard to make writing tablets,' describing a resourceful method of creating writing materials.
  • 舌耕 - A metaphor for earning a living through teaching or lecturing, implying that knowledge is like a crop cultivated by the tongue.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 贾逵 - A historical figure from the Eastern Han dynasty, known for his intelligence and scholarly achievements.
  • 韩瑶之妇 - Refers to the wife of Han Yao, a historical figure, and her role in nurturing young Jia Kui.
  • 六经 - Refers to the Six Classics of Confucianism: the *Shijing* (Book of Poetry), *Shujing* (Book of Documents), *Yijing* (Book of Changes), *Liji* (Book of Rites), *Chunqiu* (Spring and Autumn Annals), and *Lunyu* (Analects of Confucius).
  • 舌耕 - A metaphorical expression for earning a living through teaching, emphasizing the value of knowledge and learning.

Proper Nouns

  • 贾逵 - A historical figure from the Eastern Han dynasty.
  • 韩瑶 - A historical figure, the husband of the sister who nurtured Jia Kui.
  • 六经 - The Six Classics of Confucianism.
  • 舌耕 - A metaphorical term for earning a living through teaching.

[103]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 木讷 - Compound adjective meaning 'reticent' or 'uncommunicative,' often used to describe someone who is quiet or slow to speak.
  • 阴阳算术 - Refers to the study of yin-yang and numerology, which were important in ancient Chinese philosophy and divination.
  • 河洛谶纬 - Refers to the 'River and Luo River' (河图洛书), which are legendary maps or diagrams associated with the origins of Chinese civilization, and '谶纬' (prophecies and divination texts) that were often used to interpret and predict events.
  • 远年古谚 - Refers to ancient proverbs or sayings from distant times.
  • 历代图籍 - Refers to historical records and books from past dynasties.
  • 莫不咸诵也 - A formal expression meaning 'all of them were thoroughly memorized.'
  • 注记 - Refers to notes or annotations.
  • 口不能说 - Means 'unable to speak' or 'not good at verbal explanation.'
  • 三阙 - Refers to three critiques or criticisms of different schools of thought. '阙' (què) can mean 'deficiency' or 'critique.'
  • 理幽微 - Means 'profound and subtle principles.'
  • 知机藏往 - A compound phrase meaning 'to understand the timing and to hide the past,' referring to the ability to grasp the deeper meanings of things.
  • 锋起而攻之 - Means 'to rise up and attack it,' indicating a strong opposition or challenge.
  • 嬴粮而至 - Means 'to bring provisions' or 'to travel with food supplies,' implying a long journey.
  • 细流之赴巨海 - A metaphor comparing the small streams flowing into the ocean, symbolizing the convergence of many students.
  • 经神 - Refers to a person who is considered the 'spirit' or 'essence' of a particular field of study.
  • 学海 - A metaphor for a vast sea of knowledge, often used to describe someone who is deeply learned.
  • 负笈 - Refers to carrying a bag of books, a metaphor for studying or traveling to study.
  • 远险阻 - Means 'to venture far into dangerous places,' indicating a willingness to overcome obstacles.
  • 人而不学，则何以成 - A rhetorical question meaning 'If a person does not study, how can they succeed?'
  • 依林木之下 - Means 'to take shelter under trees,' indicating a humble or simple living arrangement.
  • 编茅为庵 - Refers to making a simple hut by weaving reeds.
  • 削荆为笔 - Means 'to carve a brush from thorny branches,' indicating resourcefulness.
  • 克树汁为墨 - Means 'to use tree sap as ink,' indicating ingenuity in using available materials.
  • 映星望月 - Refers to studying by the light of stars and the moon, indicating dedication to learning.
  • 暗则?娄麻蒿以自照 - Means 'in the dark, they would light fireflies or wild herbs to illuminate themselves,' indicating resourcefulness in studying under difficult conditions.
  • 观书有合意者，题其衣裳，以记其事 - Means 'when reading something that resonated with them, they would write it on their clothes to remember it,' indicating a deep engagement with the material.
  • 门徒悦其勤学，更以静衣易之 - Means 'his disciples admired his diligence and gave him clean clothes to replace the ones he had written on,' indicating the respect and support he received.
  • 非圣人之言不视 - Means 'he would not read anything that was not from a sage,' indicating a strict adherence to classical and authoritative sources.
  • 临终诫曰 - Means 'on his deathbed, he said,' indicating a final piece of advice or warning.
  • 夫人好学，虽死若存 - Means 'A person who loves learning, even if they die, still lives on,' indicating the enduring value of knowledge.
  • 不学者虽存，谓之行尸走肉耳 - Means 'A person who does not study, even if they live, is like a walking corpse,' indicating the emptiness of a life without learning.
  • 河洛秘奥 - Refers to the profound mysteries of the 'River and Luo River' (河图洛书), which were considered sacred and secret.
  • 非正典籍所载 - Means 'not recorded in official texts,' indicating that these mysteries were beyond the scope of conventional knowledge.
  • 皆注记于柱壁及园林树木 - Means 'he wrote all of these on pillars, walls, and trees in gardens,' indicating a methodical approach to recording knowledge.
  • 慕好学者，来辄写之 - Means 'those who were eager to learn would come and copy them,' indicating the spread of knowledge.
  • 经苑 - Refers to a 'garden of classics,' a metaphor for a place where classical knowledge is cultivated and preserved.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 何休 - A prominent scholar and commentator on the *Gongyang Commentary* (公羊传) during the Eastern Han dynasty. He is known for his work on the *Gongyang Commentary* and his critiques of other schools of thought.
  • 阴阳算术 - The study of yin-yang and numerology was an important aspect of Chinese philosophy and divination, often used to understand the natural world and predict future events.
  • 河洛谶纬 - The 'River and Luo River' (河图洛书) are legendary diagrams that are said to have been revealed to ancient sages, and '谶纬' (prophecies and divination texts) were often used to interpret and predict events in Chinese history.
  • 远年古谚 - Ancient proverbs and sayings were an important part of Chinese culture, often used to convey wisdom and moral lessons.
  • 历代图籍 - Historical records and books from past dynasties were essential for understanding Chinese history and culture.
  • 郑康成 - A prominent scholar and commentator on the *Zhouli* (周礼) and other classical texts during the Eastern Han dynasty. He is known for his extensive commentary on the *Zhouli* and his work on the *Yijing* (I Ching).
  • 任末 - A scholar and teacher during the Eastern Han dynasty, known for his dedication to learning and his resourcefulness in studying under difficult conditions.

[104]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 本名平，慕曾参之行，改名为曾 - Originally named Ping, he admired the conduct of Zeng Shen and changed his name to Zeng.
  • 事亲尽礼，日用三牲之养，一味不亏 - He served his parents with utmost propriety, daily offering three sacrifices, never lacking in any way.
  • 不先亲而不食新味也 - He would not eat new flavors before his parents.
  • 得新味则含怀而归 - When he encountered new flavors, he would carry them back to share with his parents.
  • 不畜鸡犬，言喧嚣惊动于亲老 - He did not keep chickens or dogs, saying that their noise would disturb his elderly parents.
  • 时亢旱，井池皆竭。母思甘清之水，曾跪而操瓶，则甘泉自涌，清美于常 - During a severe drought, wells and ponds all dried up. His mother longed for sweet, clear water. Zeng knelt and held a jar, and sweet, clear water gushed forth, more refreshing than usual.
  • 学徒有贫者，皆给食 - He provided food for all poor students.
  • 天下名书，上古以来，文篆讹落者，曾皆刊正，垂万余卷 - He corrected all the famous books from ancient times, where texts had been lost or corrupted, amassing over ten thousand volumes.
  • 及国难既夷，收天下遗书于曾家，连车继轨，输于王府 - After the national crisis was resolved, books from all over the country were collected at Zeng's home, transported in a continuous line of vehicles to the royal palace.
  • 诸弟子于门外立祠，谓曰“曹师祠” - His disciples built a shrine outside his home, calling it the 'Cao Shifu Shrine.'
  • 及世乱，家家焚庐，曾虑先文湮没，乃积石为仓以藏书，故谓曹氏为“书仓” - During times of chaos, when every household burned down, Zeng feared that ancient texts would be lost. He built a stone warehouse to store books, hence the Cao family was called the 'Book Warehouse.'
  • 录曰：观乎刘向显学于汉成时，才包三古，艺该九圣，悬日月以来，其类少矣 - The commentator says: Observing Liu Xiang's fame during the reign of Emperor Cheng of Han, his talent encompassed three ancient periods, his arts included nine sages, since the time of the sun and moon, few have been like him.
  • 逮乎后汉，贾、何、任、曹之学，并为圣神，通生民到今，盖斯而已 - By the Later Han period, the studies of Jia, He, Ren, and Cao were all considered divine, spanning from ancient times to the present, and there have been none greater.
  • 若颜渊之殆庶几；关美、张霸，何足显大儒哉！ - If they were like Yan Hui, nearly perfect; Guan Mei and Zhang Ba, how could they compare to great scholars!
  • 至如五君之徒，孔门之外未有也，方之入室，彼有惭焉 - As for the five gentlemen, there has never been anyone like them outside Confucius' school; compared to them, others would feel ashamed.
  • 贾氏之姊，所谓知识妇人鉴乎圣也 - Jia's sister, known as a wise woman, could discern the sacred.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 曹曾，鲁人也 - Cao Zeng, a native of Lu.
  • 曾参 - Zeng Shen, a disciple of Confucius and a model of filial piety.
  • 三牲 - Three sacrificial animals, traditionally ox, sheep, and pig, used in rituals.
  • 亢旱 - Severe drought, indicating a period of extreme dryness.
  • 甘清之水 - Sweet, clear water, symbolizing purity and refreshment.
  • 学徒 - Students, referring to those who study under a teacher.
  • 天下名书 - Famous books from all over the world, indicating a vast collection of literature.
  • 国难 - National crisis, referring to a period of political or social turmoil.
  • 曹师祠 - Cao Shifu Shrine, a place of veneration for Cao Zeng.
  • 书仓 - Book Warehouse, a repository for books.
  • 刘向 - Liu Xiang, a famous scholar and bibliographer of the Han dynasty.
  • 汉成时 - During the reign of Emperor Cheng of Han.
  • 三古 - Three ancient periods, likely referring to the Xia, Shang, and Zhou dynasties.
  • 九圣 - Nine sages, possibly referring to nine prominent figures in Chinese history.
  • 颜渊 - Yan Hui, a disciple of Confucius known for his wisdom and virtue.
  • 关美、张霸 - Guan Mei and Zhang Ba, historical figures or scholars.
  • 五君 - The five gentlemen, likely referring to a group of respected scholars or officials.

Proper Nouns

  • 曹曾 - Cao Zeng

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 咸熙元夫 - Likely a typo or corruption, possibly meant to be '咸熙元年' (Shanxi Yuan Year), referring to the first year of the Shanxi era, a period in the Three Kingdoms era.
  • 文帝所爱美人 - Refers to a beloved beauty of the emperor, a common theme in classical literature.
  • 容貌绝世 - Means 'beauty unparalleled in the world,' emphasizing extraordinary physical beauty.
  • 歔欷累日 - Means 'to sob for several days,' describing intense emotional distress.
  • 泪下沾衣 - Means 'tears wet her clothes,' depicting deep sorrow.
  • 以玉唾壶承泪 - Means 'using a jade spitting pot to catch tears,' a symbolic act of preserving the depth of emotion.
  • 泪凝如血 - Means 'tears congealed like blood,' a vivid description of the intensity of her tears.
  • 文车十乘 - Refers to ten carriages, likely elaborately decorated, used for a grand procession.
  • 镂金为轮辋 - Means 'inlaid with gold on the rim of the wheels,' describing the luxurious decoration of the carriages.
  • 丹画其毂 - Means 'painted red on the hubs,' further detailing the ornate design.
  • 轭前有杂宝为龙凤 - Means 'with various treasures forming dragons and phoenixes on the yoke,' indicating the use of precious materials and symbolic creatures.
  • 衔百子铃 - Means 'with a hundred children's bells,' suggesting a decorative element that produces a pleasant sound.
  • 锵锵和鸣 - Means 'ringing in harmony,' describing the pleasant sound of the bells.
  • 响于林野 - Means 'echoing through the forest and fields,' emphasizing the resonance of the sound.
  • 青色之牛 - Refers to blue-colored oxen, likely a specific breed or a symbolic color.
  • 尸屠国所献 - Means 'offered by the Shitu Kingdom,' indicating the origin of the oxen.
  • 足如马蹄 - Means 'feet like horse hooves,' describing the unusual feature of the oxen.
  • 道侧烧石叶之香 - Means 'burning the fragrant stone leaves on the side of the road,' referring to a type of incense.
  • 此石重迭，状如云母 - Means 'this stone is layered, resembling mica,' describing the appearance of the incense.
  • 其光气辟恶厉之疾 - Means 'its light and fragrance ward off evil and harmful diseases,' highlighting the protective properties of the incense.
  • 此香腹题国所进也 - Means 'this incense was presented by the Futi Kingdom,' indicating the origin of the incense.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 魏 - Refers to the state of Wei during the Three Kingdoms period, one of the three major states that emerged after the fall of the Han dynasty.
  • 文帝 - Refers to Cao Pi, the first emperor of the Wei dynasty, known for his literary talents.
  • 常山 - Refers to Changshan, a region in present-day Hebei Province, known for its historical significance.
  • 亭长 - A local official responsible for maintaining order and collecting taxes in a small area.
  • 酇乡亭长 - Refers to a specific position in a village, indicating a low-ranking official.
  • 千金宝赂 - Means 'a thousand gold pieces as a bribe,' describing a substantial gift.
  • 六宫 - Refers to the six palaces in the imperial harem, indicating the highest rank of concubines.
  • 文车十乘 - Refers to a grand procession, a common practice in imperial ceremonies.
  • 青色之牛 - Refers to a specific breed of oxen, likely a symbol of wealth and status.
  • 尸屠国 - Refers to the Shitu Kingdom, a historical state known for its contributions to the Wei court.
  • 腹题国 - Refers to the Futi Kingdom, another historical state known for its contributions to the Wei court.

Proper Nouns

  • 魏 - The state of Wei during the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 文帝 - Cao Pi, the first emperor of the Wei dynasty.
  • 常山 - A region in present-day Hebei Province.
  • 亭长 - A local official.
  • 酇乡亭长 - A specific position in a village.
  • 千金宝赂 - A thousand gold pieces as a bribe.
  • 六宫 - The six palaces in the imperial harem.
  • 文车十乘 - Ten carriages used for a grand procession.
  • 青色之牛 - Blue-colored oxen.
  • 尸屠国 - The Shitu Kingdom.
  • 腹题国 - The Futi Kingdom.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 膏烛之光，相续不灭 - Describes the continuous glow of candles, likely a metaphor for the enduring nature of the event or journey.
  • 车徒咽路，尘起蔽于星月 - A vivid description of the road being blocked by vehicles and dust rising to obscure the stars and moon, symbolizing the grandeur and scale of the procession.
  • 尘宵 - A compound term that combines 'dust' and 'night,' possibly referring to the night of the dust-laden procession.
  • 筑土为台，基高三十丈 - Refers to the construction of a high platform, emphasizing the grandeur and effort involved.
  • 远望如列星之坠地 - A metaphor comparing the appearance of the candles from a distance to falling stars.
  • 青槐夹道多尘埃 - Describes the scene of green willows lining the road with dust, setting a vivid backdrop.
  • 龙楼凤阙望崔嵬 - Refers to the grand and imposing architecture, possibly the imperial palace.
  • 清风细雨杂香来 - Describes a pleasant atmosphere with a mix of fragrances carried by the wind and rain.
  • 土上出金火照台 - A metaphorical phrase suggesting the rise of a new dynasty, with '土上出金' (gold emerging from the earth) symbolizing the transition from the Han to the Wei dynasty.
  • 朝为行云，暮为行雨 - A poetic expression often used to describe a woman's beauty and charm, implying her ability to captivate and influence others.
  • 非云非雨，非朝非暮 - A play on the previous phrase, suggesting that the woman's presence is even more extraordinary and beyond the ordinary.
  • 夜来 - A new name given to Ling Yun, possibly symbolizing her arrival or transformation.
  • 针神 - A title given to Ling Yun, highlighting her exceptional skill in needlework.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 灵芸 - A name, possibly referring to a historical figure or a fictional character in the context of the story.
  • 京师 - Refers to the capital city, likely the imperial capital during the Han dynasty.
  • 雕玉之辇 - A luxurious carriage, symbolizing the emperor's status and wealth.
  • 龙鸾之钗 - A type of ornate hairpin, often associated with high-ranking officials or nobility.
  • 明珠翡翠 - Precious gems, often used as symbols of wealth and status.
  • 针工 - Refers to the art of needlework, a highly valued skill in ancient China.

Proper Nouns

  • 灵芸 - A name, possibly referring to a historical figure or a fictional character.
  • 京师 - The capital city, likely the imperial capital during the Han dynasty.
  • 雕玉之辇 - A luxurious carriage, symbolizing the emperor's status and wealth.
  • 龙鸾之钗 - An ornate hairpin, often associated with high-ranking officials or nobility.
  • 明珠翡翠 - Precious gems, symbols of wealth and status.
  • 针工 - The art of needlework, a highly valued skill in ancient China.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 五帝之運，迭相生死 - Cyclical rise and fall of the Five Emperors, a concept rooted in Chinese cosmology and history.
  • 起伏因循 - Describes the ebb and flow of events, often implying a pattern of recurrence or continuity.
  • 顯于言端 - Means that these patterns are evident in speech or discourse.
  • 童谣信于春秋 - Refers to the belief in the predictive power of children's rhymes during the Spring and Autumn period.
  • 谶辞烦于汉末 - Indicates the prevalence of prophetic sayings or predictions at the end of the Han dynasty.
  • 或着明先典 - Means that some revelations are made clear through ancient precedents or texts.
  • 或托见图记 - Suggests that others are revealed through symbols or inscriptions.
  • 佥详《河》、《洛》 - Refers to the collective examination of the *Book of the Yellow River* and the *Book of the Luo River*, which are legendary texts associated with divination and prophecy.
  • 应运不同 - Implies that the timing or context of these revelations varies.
  • 唐尧以炎正禅虞 - Refers to the legendary transition of power from Emperor Yao to Emperor Shun, symbolizing a peaceful succession.
  • 大汉以火德受魏 - Refers to the Han dynasty's claim to the 'fire' element in the Five Elements theory, justifying its succession to the Wei dynasty.
  • 世历沿袭 - Means that historical events and dynastic changes follow a pattern of continuity.
  • 得其宜矣 - Suggests that such patterns are appropriate or fitting.
  • 升名藉璧 - Refers to the elevation of status through the use of jade, often associated with rituals or ceremonies.
  • 因事而来 - Means that such elevation is contingent on specific events or circumstances.
  • 柔曼之质见进 - Refers to the promotion of those with gentle or delicate qualities.
  • 裁缝之妙要宠 - Implies that those skilled in subtle or refined arts are favored.
  • 媚斯婉约 - Describes a pleasing or charming quality, often associated with elegance or grace.
  • 荣非世载 - Suggests that honor or glory is not permanent or enduring.
  • 取或一朝 - Means that such glory may last only for a single generation.
  • 去彼疑贱 - Refers to the removal of doubt or suspicion, often associated with social status.
  • 延此华轩 - Suggests the extension or prolongation of luxury or nobility.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 五帝 - Refers to the legendary Five Emperors of ancient China, often associated with the mythical era of the Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors.
  • 春秋 - Refers to the Spring and Autumn period, a time of significant historical and cultural development in China.
  • 汉末 - Refers to the end of the Han dynasty, a period marked by political instability and the rise of prophetic sayings.
  • 唐尧 - Refers to Emperor Yao, a legendary ruler of ancient China.
  • 虞 - Refers to Emperor Shun, the successor to Emperor Yao.
  • 大汉 - Refers to the Han dynasty, one of the most significant dynasties in Chinese history.
  • 魏 - Refers to the Wei dynasty, which succeeded the Han dynasty.
  • 世历 - Refers to the historical record or the sequence of dynastic changes.
  • 沿袭 - Refers to the continuity or inheritance of traditions and practices.
  • 升名 - Refers to the elevation of status or reputation.
  • 藉璧 - Refers to the use of jade in rituals or ceremonies, often associated with nobility or divinity.
  • 柔曼之质 - Refers to gentle or delicate qualities, often associated with refinement or elegance.
  • 裁缝之妙 - Refers to the art of subtle or refined craftsmanship.
  • 媚斯 - Refers to charm or attractiveness, often associated with elegance or grace.
  • 婉约 - Refers to a style of elegance or refinement, often associated with poetry or literature.
  • 荣 - Refers to honor or glory, often associated with social status or achievement.
  • 世载 - Refers to the enduring nature of something, often associated with history or tradition.
  • 华轩 - Refers to luxury or nobility, often associated with high social status.

Proper Nouns

  • 五帝 - The Five Emperors of ancient China.
  • 春秋 - The Spring and Autumn period.
  • 汉末 - The end of the Han dynasty.
  • 唐尧 - Emperor Yao.
  • 虞 - Emperor Shun.
  • 大汉 - The Han dynasty.
  • 魏 - The Wei dynasty.
  • 世历 - The historical record or the sequence of dynastic changes.
  • 沿袭 - The continuity or inheritance of traditions and practices.
  • 升名 - The elevation of status or reputation.
  • 藉璧 - The use of jade in rituals or ceremonies.
  • 柔曼之质 - Gentle or delicate qualities.
  • 裁缝之妙 - The art of subtle or refined craftsmanship.
  • 媚斯 - Charm or attractiveness.
  • 婉约 - A style of elegance or refinement.
  • 荣 - Honor or glory.
  • 世载 - The enduring nature of something.
  • 华轩 - Luxury or nobility.

[108]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 躬自掘土 - Emphasizes the emperor's personal involvement in digging the earth.
  • 群臣皆负畚锸 - Indicates the ministers' participation in the labor by carrying shovels.
  • 天阴冻寒 - Describes the weather conditions as overcast, freezing, and cold.
  • 死者相枕 - Vividly depicts the harsh conditions with the dead lying one upon another.
  • 洛、邺诸鼎，皆夜震自移 - Describes the supernatural movement of bronze tripods in Luoyang and Ye during the night.
  • 有怨叹之声 - Suggests the presence of supernatural or ghostly activity with sounds of lamentation.
  • 省薄奢费，以敦俭朴 - States the advisors' recommendation to reduce luxury and promote frugality.
  • 饬台榭累年而毕 - Describes the construction of pavilions and terraces over many years until completion.
  • 去烦归俭 - Means to eliminate extravagance and return to simplicity.
  • 死者收而葬之 - Describes the emperor's action to collect and bury the dead.
  • 人神致感，众祥皆应 - Describes the positive response from both people and spirits with all auspicious signs appearing.
  • 状如柏树，其文彪发，似人雕镂 - Means it looks like a pine tree, with patterns that stand out, as if carved by humans.
  • 自下及上皆合，而中开广六尺 - Means from bottom to top, it is connected, but in the middle, it opens up to six feet wide.
  • 谓之“神草” - Means called the 'divine grass'.
  • 沛国有黄麟见于戊己之地 - Describes a yellow dragon appearing in the region of Pei, which corresponds to the directions of west and south.
  • 修戊己之坛，黄星炳夜 - Means to build altars for the directions of west and south, with yellow stars shining at night.
  • 起昴毕之台，祭祀此星 - Means to build pavilions for the stars of Shen and Bi, and to worship these stars.
  • 魏之分野，岁时修祀焉 - Means in the Hui region, rituals were performed regularly according to the seasons.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 魏明帝 - Refers to Emperor Ming of Wei, the second ruler of the Cao Wei dynasty.
  • 凌云台 - A high platform or tower, symbolizing imperial power and ambition.
  • 洛、邺 - Refers to Luoyang and Ye, two important cities in the Cao Wei dynasty.
  • 高堂隆 - A prominent official and advisor during the Cao Wei dynasty.
  • 太山 - Refers to Mount Tai, one of the Five Great Mountains of China, often associated with imperial rituals and divine manifestations.
  • 连理文石 - A stone with intertwined patterns, symbolizing unity and harmony.
  • 合欢草 - A type of plant that symbolizes joy and harmony, often used in traditional Chinese medicine.
  • 黄麟 - A yellow dragon, a symbol of imperial power and good fortune.
  • 戊己之地 - Refers to the directions of west and south, which are associated with the earth element in Chinese cosmology.
  • 昴毕 - Stars in the constellation of Shen and Bi, often associated with good omens and imperial prosperity.
  • 魏之分野 - Refers to the astrological region of the Hui dynasty, which corresponds to the state of Hui.

Proper Nouns

  • 魏明帝 - Emperor Ming of Wei.
  • 凌云台 - A high platform or tower.
  • 高堂隆 - A prominent official and advisor during the Cao Wei dynasty.
  • 太山 - Mount Tai.
  • 连理文石 - A stone with intertwined patterns.
  • 合欢草 - A type of plant.
  • 黄麟 - A yellow dragon.
  • 戊己之地 - The directions of west and south.
  • 昴毕 - Stars in the constellation of Shen and Bi.
  • 魏之分野 - The astrological region of the Hui dynasty.

[109]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 百步中髭发 - Hyperbolic expression meaning he could hit a target from a hundred paces, even hitting the hair on the target's face.
  • 吞并巴蜀，如鸱衔腐鼠耳 - Metaphor comparing the conquest of Ba and Shu to a kite carrying away a dead mouse, suggesting it would be easy.
  • 空中闻数百人泣声 - Describes hearing the sounds of hundreds of people weeping in the air; could be interpreted as either a literal or supernatural event.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 任城王 - A title given to a prince of the Han dynasty, specifically referring to Prince Chang.
  • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han, a powerful and influential ruler.
  • 六经 - The Six Classics, a collection of ancient Chinese texts including the *Shijing* (Book of Poetry), *Shujing* (Book of Documents), *Yijing* (Book of Changes), *Liji* (Book of Rites), *Chunqiu* (Spring and Autumn Annals), and *Lunyu* (Analects of Confucius).
  • 洪范 - A chapter of the *Shujing* that outlines the principles of good governance.
  • 乐浪 - An ancient kingdom in present-day Korea, which was a vassal state of the Han dynasty.
  • 枭殷之徒 - A reference to the people of the Yin dynasty, often used to describe those who are cowardly or terrified.
  • 巴蜀 - Regions in southwestern China, known for their strategic importance.
  • 鸱衔腐鼠 - A metaphor from the *Zhuangzi* that compares something trivial to a kite carrying away a dead mouse.
  • 汉东平王 - Prince of Dongping, a title given to a prince of the Han dynasty.
  • 国史 - Official historians who recorded the deeds of important figures.
  • 秘阁 - The imperial library, where important documents were stored.

Proper Nouns

  • 任城王 - The title of Prince Chang.
  • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han.
  • 六经 - The Six Classics.
  • 洪范 - A chapter of the *Shujing*.
  • 乐浪 - An ancient kingdom in present-day Korea.
  • 巴蜀 - Regions in southwestern China.
  • 汉东平王 - Prince of Dongping.
  • 秘阁 - The imperial library.

[110]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 色如丹，大如燕 - Describes the appearance of the creature, comparing its color to cinnabar and its size to a swallow.
  • 翔飞颉颃 - Describes the creature's flight, suggesting it flies gracefully and in a balanced manner.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 建安三年 - The third year of the Jian'an era during the Eastern Han dynasty.
  • 胥徒国 - A kingdom or region, possibly fictional or mythical, known for its unique offerings.
  • 沉明石鸡 - A mythical creature, possibly symbolizing good fortune or divine favor.
  • 应时而鸣，声能远彻 - Highlights the creature's ability to call at the right time and its powerful voice.
  • 杀牲以祀之 - Reflects the cultural practice of sacrifice to honor or appease deities or spirits.
  • 嘉瑞 - A term for a good omen, often associated with political stability and prosperity.
  • 宝鸡 - A name given to the creature, possibly due to its association with peace and prosperity.
  • 道家云 - Indicates a statement attributed to Daoist teachings, which often emphasize natural harmony and longevity.
  • 昔仙人桐君采石 - Refers to an ancient legend about a legendary figure named Tong Jun who collected stones, possibly for medicinal or spiritual purposes.
  • 入穴数里 - Describes the depth of the cave or tunnel, suggesting a journey into the unknown.
  • 得丹石鸡 - Indicates the discovery of the red stone chicken, a rare and valuable find.
  • 舂碎为药 - Describes the process of grinding the stone into a medicinal form, reflecting traditional Chinese medicine practices.
  • 服之者令人有声气，后天而死 - Suggests that consuming the medicine would enhance one's vitality and longevity, a common theme in Daoist and traditional Chinese medicine.
  • 汉武帝宝鼎元年 - The year 113 AD, marking the reign of Emperor Wu of Han, a significant period in Chinese history.
  • 西方贡珍怪 - Refers to rare and exotic gifts presented by the Western regions, often associated with trade and diplomacy.
  • 虎魄燕 - A mythical or rare creature, possibly a bird made of amber, symbolizing rarity and value.
  • 置之静室 - Indicates placing the creature in a quiet room, suggesting a sacred or ceremonial setting.
  • 自于室中鸣翔 - Describes the creature's behavior in the room, calling and flying, symbolizing its divine or supernatural nature.
  • 《洛书》云 - Indicates a statement attributed to the *Luo Shu* (River Map), a classical text associated with cosmology and divination.
  • 皇图之宝，土德之征，大魏之嘉瑞 - Refers to the imperial emblem, the virtue of earth, and the good omen for the Wei dynasty, reflecting the political and cultural context of the time.

Proper Nouns

  • 建安三年 - The year 208 AD.
  • 胥徒国 - A kingdom or region.
  • 沉明石鸡 - A mythical creature.
  • 汉武帝宝鼎元年 - The year 113 AD.
  • 虎魄燕 - A mythical creature.
  • 《洛书》 - The *Luo Shu* (River Map).

[111]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 明帝即位二年 - In the second year of Emperor Ming's reign, a standard way to mark the time frame in historical narratives.
  • 起靈禽之園 - He established the Spirit Bird Garden, where '靈禽' refers to birds with supernatural or auspicious qualities.
  • 異鳥殊獸 - Strange birds and unusual beasts, a common phrase to describe exotic animals.
  • 畜此園也 - Housed in this garden, where '畜' means to keep or house.
  • 昆明國貢嗽金鳥 - The Kingdom of Kunming offered the Sputtering Gold Bird, where '嗽金' is a metaphorical description of the bird's behavior.
  • 形如雀而色黃 - Its shape is like that of a sparrow, but it is yellow in color, a descriptive phrase for the bird's appearance.
  • 羽毛柔密 - Its feathers are soft and dense, emphasizing the bird's physical characteristics.
  • 常翱翔海上 - It often soars over the sea, describing the bird's natural habitat and behavior.
  • ?者得之 - Whoever captures it, where the '?' indicates a missing character or unclear text.
  • 以為至祥 - Takes it as an extremely auspicious omen, expressing the significance of the bird.
  • 聞大魏之德 - Hearing of the virtue of the Great Wei, referring to the moral and political influence of the Wei dynasty.
  • 被於荒遠 - Reaching the distant and remote, indicating the extent of the Wei dynasty's influence.
  • 越山航海 - Crossing mountains and seas, describing the arduous journey to offer the bird.
  • 帝得此鳥 - The emperor obtained this bird, marking a significant event.
  • 畜於靈禽之園 - Kept in the Spirit Bird Garden, emphasizing the bird's special status.
  • 飲以龜腦 - Given turtle brain to drink, another example of the bird's special care.
  • 鳥常吐金屑如粟 - The bird often spits out gold dust like millet, describing a miraculous ability.
  • 鑄之可以為器 - Can be cast into vessels, indicating the practical use of the gold.
  • 昔漢武帝時 - In the time of Emperor Wu of Han, referring to a historical precedent.
  • 有人獻神雀 - Someone offered a divine sparrow, another example of a miraculous bird.
  • 畏霜雪 - Afraid of frost and snow, indicating the bird's sensitivity to cold.
  • 乃起小屋處之 - Built a small house to house it, showing the emperor's care for the bird.
  • 名曰“辟寒台” - Called it the 'Frost-Repelling Pavilion,' giving the structure a symbolic name.
  • 皆用水精為戶牖 - All the windows and doors were made of water crystal, indicating the luxurious materials used.
  • 使內外通光 - To allow light to pass through inside and out, emphasizing the transparency of the structure.
  • 宮人爭以鳥吐之金用飾钗佩 - The palace ladies competed to use the gold the bird spat out to adorn their hairpins and ornaments, showing the bird's value.
  • 謂之“辟寒金” - Called it 'Frost-Repelling Gold,' giving the gold a symbolic name.
  • 故宮人相嘲曰 - So the palace ladies would mock each other, indicating a social custom.
  • 不服辟寒金，那得帝王心 - If you don't wear Frost-Repelling Gold, how can you win the emperor's favor, expressing a social expectation.
  • 於是媚惑者 - Thus, those who flatter and seduce, referring to palace ladies who seek favor.
  • 亂爭此寶金為身飾 - Competed chaotically to use this precious gold for their adornment, showing the intensity of the competition.
  • 及行臥皆懷挾以要寵幸也 - And carried it with them in their travels and sleep to seek favor, indicating the bird's gold's value.
  • 魏氏喪滅 - The Wei dynasty perished, marking a significant historical event.
  • 池台鞠為煨烬 - The ponds and terraces were reduced to ashes, indicating the destruction of the garden.
  • 嗽金之鳥，亦自翱翔矣 - The Sputtering Gold Bird also flew away on its own, showing the end of the bird's special status.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 明帝 - Refers to Emperor Ming of Wei, a ruler of the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 靈禽 - Refers to birds with supernatural or auspicious qualities, often associated with divine or imperial favor.
  • 昆明國 - Refers to the Kingdom of Kunming, a historical region in what is now Yunnan, China.
  • 嗽金鳥 - A mythical bird with the ability to spit out gold, symbolizing wealth and divine favor.
  • 漢武帝 - Refers to Emperor Wu of Han, a powerful ruler of the Han dynasty known for his military campaigns and cultural achievements.
  • 神雀 - Refers to a divine sparrow, another example of a miraculous bird in Chinese mythology.
  • 辟寒金 - Refers to the gold spit out by the Sputtering Gold Bird, which was believed to have the power to repel cold and bring favor.

Proper Nouns

  • 明帝 - Emperor Ming of Wei.
  • 靈禽 - Spirit Bird.
  • 昆明國 - Kingdom of Kunming.
  • 嗽金鳥 - Sputtering Gold Bird.
  • 漢武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han.
  • 神雀 - Divine Sparrow.
  • 辟寒金 - Frost-Repelling Gold.

[112]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 咸熙二年 - Specific year in the Chinese calendar, corresponding to AD 265-266 during the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 宫中夜有异兽 - Describes an unusual animal appearing in the palace at night.
  • 白色光洁 - Describes the animal as white and clean.
  • 绕宫而行 - Indicates the animal moving around the palace.
  • 阉宦见之，以闻于帝 - Means eunuchs saw it and reported to the emperor.
  • 宫闱幽密 - Refers to the secluded and secret nature of the palace.
  • 若有异兽，皆非祥也 - Suggests that if there are unusual animals, they are not auspicious.
  • 使宦者伺之 - Means the emperor ordered eunuchs to watch for the animal.
  • 果见一白虎子 - Confirms that a white tiger cub was indeed seen.
  • 遍房而走 - Describes the tiger cub running through the rooms.
  • 候者以戈投之 - Means the guards threw a spear at the tiger cub.
  • 即中左目 - Indicates that the spear hit the tiger cub's left eye.
  • 比往取视 - Means when they went to check, only blood was found.
  • 不复见虎 - Indicates that the tiger was no longer visible.
  • 搜检宫内及诸池井 - Means they searched the entire palace and all pools and wells.
  • 不见有物 - Indicates that nothing was found.
  • 次检宝库中 - Means they then checked the treasure house.
  • 得一玉虎头枕 - Indicates finding a jade tiger-head pillow.
  • 眼有伤，血痕尚湿 - Describes the pillow with a wounded eye and wet blood stains.
  • 帝该古博闻 - Means the emperor is knowledgeable and well-read in ancient matters.
  • 汉诛梁冀，得一玉虎头枕 - Refers to a historical event where a jade tiger-head pillow was found after the execution of Liang Ji.
  • 云单池国所献 - Means it was said to be a gift from the Danchi Kingdom.
  • 检其颔下，有篆书字 - Indicates checking under the chin, where there were seal script characters.
  • 云是帝辛之枕，尝与妲己同枕之 - Means it was said to be Emperor Xin's pillow, which he used with Lady Daji.
  • 是殷时遗宝也 - Indicates it is a relic from the Shang Dynasty.
  • 又按《五帝本纪》云 - Means according to the *Five Emperors' Annals*.
  • 帝辛殷代之末 - Indicates that Emperor Xin was the last ruler of the Shang Dynasty.
  • 至咸熙多历年所 - Means up to the time of Xianxi, many years have passed.
  • 代代相传 - Means passed down through generations.
  • 凡珍宝久则生精灵 - Means that valuable treasures, if kept for a long time, can develop spirits.
  • 必神物凭之也 - Indicates that it must be a divine entity possessing it.

  • 汉诛梁冀，得一玉虎头枕 - Refers to a historical event where a jade tiger-head pillow was found after the execution of Liang Ji.
  • 云单池国所献 - Means it was said to be a gift from the Danchi Kingdom.
  • 检其颔下，有篆书字 - Indicates checking under the chin, where there were seal script characters.
  • 云是帝辛之枕，尝与妲己同枕之 - Means it was said to be Emperor Xin's pillow, which he used with Lady Daji.
  • 是殷时遗宝也 - Indicates it is a relic from the Shang Dynasty.
  • 又按《五帝本纪》云 - Means according to the *Five Emperors' Annals*.
  • 帝辛殷代之末 - Indicates that Emperor Xin was the last ruler of the Shang Dynasty.
  • 至咸熙多历年所 - Means up to the time of Xianxi, many years have passed.
  • 代代相传 - Means passed down through generations.
  • 凡珍宝久则生精灵 - Means that valuable treasures, if kept for a long time, can develop spirits.
  • 必神物凭之也 - Indicates that it must be a divine entity possessing it.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 魏禅晋之岁 - The year when the state of Wei transferred power to the state of Jin, a significant event in the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 北阙下有白光如鸟雀之状 - Describes a white light appearing near the northern gate, resembling birds.
  • 时飞翔来去 - Indicates the light moving about, suggesting an animate quality.
  • 有司闻奏帝所 - Refers to officials reporting the phenomenon to the emperor.
  • 得一白燕 - Means they found a white swallow.
  • 以为神物 - Means it was considered a divine or sacred object.
  • 于是以金为樊 - Means they made a golden cage for it.
  • 置于宫中 - Means they placed it in the palace.
  • 旬日不知所在 - Means after ten days, it was no longer found.
  • 金德之瑞 - The auspicious sign of the metal element, associated with the state of Jin.
  • 昔师旷时，有白燕来巢 - Refers to an ancient story about a white swallow nesting during the time of Shikuang, a famous musician and seer.
  • 检《瑞应图》，果如所论 - Means checking the *Book of Auspicious Signs* confirmed the interpretation.
  • 白色叶于金德 - Means the white color is associated with the metal element.
  • 师旷晋时人也 - Confirms that Shikuang was a person from the state of Jin.
  • 古今之义相符焉 - Means the ancient and modern interpretations align.
  • 薛夏，天水人也 - Introduces Xue Xia, a person from Tianshui.
  • 博学绝伦 - Means he was exceptionally learned.
  • 母孕夏时，梦人遗之一箧衣云 - Describes his mother's dream of receiving a box of clothes.
  • 夫人必产贤明之子也，为帝王之所崇 - The dreamer says the child will be a wise and virtuous person, honored by emperors.
  • 母记所梦之日 - Means the mother remembered the date of the dream.
  • 及生夏，年及弱冠 - Means when Xue Xia was born, he reached adulthood.
  • 才辩过人 - Means he had extraordinary talent and eloquence.
  • 魏文帝与之讲论，终日不息 - Describes Huiwen Emperor discussing with him all day long.
  • 应对如流，无有疑滞 - Means his responses were fluent and without hesitation.
  • 帝曰 - The emperor says.
  • 昔公孙龙称为辩捷 - Refers to Gong Sun Long, a famous logician known for his sharp debating skills.
  • 而迂诞诬妄 - He was often considered eccentric and unreliable.
  • 今子所说，非圣人之言不谈 - What Xue Xia says is only about the words of the sages.
  • 子游、子夏之俦，不能过也 - He is as good as Ziyu and Zixia, two famous disciples of Confucius.
  • 若仲尼在魏，复为入室焉 - If Confucius were in Wei, he would be considered a top student again.
  • 帝手制书与夏，题云“入室生” - The emperor wrote a letter to Xue Xia, calling him 'the top student.'
  • 位至秘书丞 - He held the position of Secretary of the Secretariat.
  • 居生甚贫，帝解御衣以赐之 - Xue Xia lived in poverty, so the emperor gave him a robe.
  • 果符元所梦 - It fulfilled the original dream.
  • 名冠当时，为一代高士 - He was the most famous of his time and a great scholar.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 魏禅晋之岁 - The year when the state of Wei transferred power to the state of Jin, a significant event in the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 北阙 - Refers to the northern gate of the imperial city, symbolizing the heart of the empire.
  • 白光如鸟雀之状 - A supernatural phenomenon, often interpreted as an omen.
  • 有司 - Refers to officials responsible for reporting such phenomena to the emperor.
  • 白燕 - A white swallow, often considered a symbol of good fortune or a divine sign.
  • 金德之瑞 - The auspicious sign of the metal element, associated with the state of Jin.
  • 师旷 - A famous musician and seer from the state of Jin, known for his wisdom and musical talent.
  • 《瑞应图》 - An ancient text recording auspicious signs and omens.
  • 薛夏 - A historical figure known for his exceptional learning and eloquence.
  • 天水 - A place name, likely referring to a region in present-day Gansu Province.
  • 魏文帝 - Emperor Wen of Wei, a ruler of the state of Wei during the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 公孙龙 - A famous logician and philosopher known for his sharp debating skills.
  • 子游、子夏 - Two famous disciples of Confucius, known for their wisdom and virtue.
  • 仲尼 - A respectful term for Confucius.
  • 秘书丞 - A high-ranking official position in the imperial bureaucracy.
  • 御衣 - A robe worn by the emperor, symbolizing imperial favor.

Proper Nouns

  • 北阙 - The northern gate of the imperial city.
  • 白燕 - A white swallow.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 鸡酒之礼 - A simple offering of chicken and wine, symbolizing respect and mourning.
  • 动于林野 - To move or echo through the forest and fields, suggesting the intensity of the emotions.
  • 神悟远识 - Divine insight and far-sighted wisdom, suggesting a supernatural connection.
  • 同归九地 - To return together to the underworld, implying a desire to share the same fate.
  • 安可逃乎 - How can one escape, indicating a sense of inevitability and acceptance.
  • 奄然不见 - Suddenly disappearing, suggesting a sudden and mysterious departure.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 田畴 - A historical figure known for his loyalty and devotion.
  • 北平 - A region in northern China.
  • 刘虞 - A Han dynasty official who was betrayed and killed by his subordinate, Gongsun Zan.
  • 公孙瓒 - A military general who betrayed and killed Liu Yu.
  • 幽州 - An ancient Chinese province, roughly corresponding to modern-day Beijing and surrounding areas.

  • 子万古之贞士也 - A title of honor, meaning 'a gentleman of unwavering integrity throughout the ages.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 自以業固河山，名超萬世 - Classical expression meaning 'believing that one's legacy is as solid as rivers and mountains and surpasses all generations in fame.'
  • 覆滅宗祀，由斯哀哀 - Phrase indicating the destruction of ancestral worship and the resulting profound sorrow.
  • 踐中區之沃盛 - Means 'stepping into the fertile and prosperous center of the land.'
  • 威靈所慑，比强列代 - Indicates the power and influence that intimidates even the strongest of past dynasties.
  • 祯祥神宝，史不绝书 - Means 'auspicious signs and divine treasures are frequently recorded in history.'
  • 殊方珍贡，府无虚月 - Refers to exotic and precious tributes that arrive regularly without fail.
  • 鼎据三方，称雄四海 - Describes holding power over three regions and being dominant throughout the world.
  • 圣教微于尧、禹，历代劣于姬、汉 - Suggests that the moral teachings have diminished compared to those of Yao and Yu, and the current dynasty is inferior to the Zhou and Han dynasties.
  • 东鲠闽、吴，西病邛蜀 - Indicates eastern troubles with Min and Wu, and western problems with Qiong and Shu.
  • 师旅岁兴，财力日费 - Means that military campaigns occur annually, and resources are spent daily.
  • 不能遵养黎元，远瞻前朴 - Suggests an inability to nurture the common people and look ahead to simpler times.
  • 宫室穷丽，池榭肆其宏广 - Describes palaces and gardens as being extremely luxurious and expansive.
  • 终取夷灭，数其然哉 - Concludes with the idea that this ultimately leads to destruction, and it is indeed so.
  • 任城渊谋神勇，智周祥艺 - Refers to Ren Cheng's deep strategy and divine courage, as well as his comprehensive wisdom and skills.
  • 田畴事死如生，守以直节 - Means that Tian Chou treats the dead as if they were alive, maintaining his integrity.
  • 精诚之至，通于神明 - Suggests that his sincerity and dedication reach the level of divinity.
  • 曹洪忠烈为心，爱亲忧国 - Describes Cao Hong as having a heart of loyalty and bravery, caring for his family and the country.
  • 此穆满之骏，方之“白鹄”，可谓齐足者也 - Compares these individuals to the swift horses of Mu Man, likening them to the 'White Swan,' suggesting they are equally capable.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 尧、禹 - Refers to Yao and Yu, legendary sage rulers of ancient China.
  • 姬、汉 - Refers to the Zhou and Han dynasties, known for their moral and cultural achievements.
  • 闽、吴 - Refers to the regions of Min and Wu, historically significant areas in southeastern China.

  • 任城 - A place name, possibly referring to a specific region or city.

  • 曹洪 - A person, likely a historical figure known for his loyalty and bravery.
  • 穆满之骏 - Refers to the swift horses of Mu Man, a legendary ruler of the Zhou dynasty.
  • 白鹄 - A mythical or symbolic bird, often associated with purity and nobility.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 錄曰 - Formal introductory phrase meaning 'The record says' or 'It is recorded that.'
  • 按《吳書》云 - Means 'According to Wu Shu it is said,' introducing a reference to the Wu Shu (History of Wu).
  • 與王之說為異 - Means 'differs from Wang's account,' indicating a contrast with another source.
  • 夫西方金位，以葉晉德 - Classical expression meaning 'The West is the position of metal, which aligns with the virtue of Jin,' referring to the Five Elements theory.
  • 興亡之兆，後而效焉 - Means 'the signs of rise and fall, which manifest later,' suggesting a delayed effect.
  • 蓋表吳亡而授晉也 - Means 'This indicates the fall of Wu and the rise of Jin,' summarizing the implication of the earlier statement.
  • 授蘭懷日，事類而非 - Means 'Granting a lily and holding a sun, these are similar but not identical,' referring to a specific divination practice.
  • 及吳氏之興年，嘉禾之號，芳茅之征信矣 - Means 'And the year of Wu's rise, the title 'Jiacheng,' and the verified auspicious reeds,' referring to specific historical and symbolic elements.
  • 至晉太康元年，孫皓送六金璽云 - Means 'By the first year of Tai Kang in Jin, Sun Hao sent six golden seals, saying,' introducing a historical event.
  • 時無玉工，故以金為印璽 - Means 'There were no jade craftsmen, so gold was used for the seals,' explaining a practical reason.
  • 夫孫氏擅割江東，包卷百越 - Means 'The Sun family ruled Jiangdong, encompassing the Baiyue,' describing the territorial control of the Sun family.
  • 吞席漢陽，威惕中夏 - Means 'Swallowing Hanyang, instilling fear in the Central Plains,' describing the military and political power of the Sun family.
  • 富強之業，三雄比盛 - Means 'The industry of wealth and strength, comparable to the three great powers,' highlighting the Sun family's prosperity.
  • 時有未賓而兵戈歲起 - Means 'At times there were those not yet subjugated, and wars broke out annually,' describing ongoing conflicts.
  • 每梗心於邛蜀，愤慨於燕魏 - Means 'Frequently troubled by Qiong and Shu, and filled with resentment towards Yan and Wei,' indicating regional tensions.
  • 四方未夷，有事征伐 - Means 'The four directions were not pacified, and there were frequent military campaigns,' describing the state of affairs.
  • 因之以師旅，遵之以儉素 - Means 'Following this with military campaigns, adhering to frugality and simplicity,' describing the approach to governance.
  • 去其游侈之費，塞兹雕靡之涂 - Means 'Eliminating extravagant spending, blocking the path of luxury and decay,' describing economic policies.
  • 不欲使四方民勞 - Means 'Not wanting to burden the people of the four directions,' expressing a concern for the populace.
  • 非無玉工也 - Means 'It was not that there were no jade craftsmen,' clarifying a previous statement.
  • 固能輕彼池山，賤斯棘實 - Means 'Indeed, they could consider the hills and pools as light, and the thorns as worthless,' describing a dismissive attitude.
  • 漢鄙盈車之屑，燕棄璞于衡庑 - Means 'Han discarded the dust that filled the carriage, and Yan abandoned the unpolished jade on the hall,' using metaphors to describe wastefulness.
  • 沉河底谷，義昭攸古 - Means 'Sinking to the bottom of the river, the meaning is clear from ancient times,' suggesting a historical precedent.
  • 務崇儉約，豈非高欤 - Means 'Striving for frugality and simplicity, is this not noble?' expressing a moral judgment.
  • 及乎吳亡時，以六代金璽歸晉 - Means 'When Wu fell, the six generations of golden seals were returned to Jin,' describing the final act.
  • 堅母之夢驗矣 - Means 'The dream of Sun Jian's mother was verified,' concluding the narrative.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 《吳書》 - The History of Wu, a historical text detailing the state of Wu during the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 王之說 - Refers to another historical or literary source, likely a different account of the same events.
  • 晉德 - Refers to the virtue of Jin, one of the Five Elements.
  • 六夢八征 - Refers to specific divinations and omens mentioned in the Zhouyi (Book of Changes).
  • 授蘭懷日 - A specific divination practice involving lilies and the sun.
  • 吳氏之興年，嘉禾之號 - Refers to the year of Wu's rise and the title 'Jiacheng,' a symbol of good fortune.
  • 芳茅之征信 - Refers to the verified auspicious reeds, a symbol of good fortune.
  • 晉太康元年 - The first year of Tai Kang in the Jin dynasty, a specific historical period.
  • 孫皓 - The last ruler of the state of Wu, known for his submission to Jin.
  • 六金璽 - Six golden seals, a significant historical artifact.
  • 孫氏 - The Sun family, rulers of the state of Wu.
  • 江東 - The eastern part of the Yangtze River region, a key area of control for the Sun family.

  • 游侈之費 - Extravagant spending, often criticized in classical texts.
  • 雕靡之涂 - The path of luxury and decay, a negative concept.
  • 池山 - Hills and pools, often used metaphorically in classical texts.
  • 棘實 - Thorns, often used metaphorically to represent something worthless.
  • 漢鄙盈車之屑 - A metaphor for wastefulness, using the image of dust filling a carriage.
  • 燕棄璞于衡庑 - A metaphor for wastefulness, using the image of unpolished jade being discarded.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 善画，巧妙无双 - Skilled in painting, unparalleled in her artistry.
  • 能于指间以彩丝织云霞龙蛇之锦 - Able to weave clouds, dragons, and serpents with colored silk between her fingers.
  • 大则盈尺，小则方寸 - Large ones fill a foot, small ones a square inch.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 權使夫人指其意思焉 - Sun Quan, the ruler of Wu, asked his wife to express her intentions.
  • 妾欲窮慮盡思 - The wife wishes to exhaust her thoughts and consider everything thoroughly.
  • 能使下绡帷而清风自入 - The fabric allows cool air to enter naturally.
  • 視外無有蔽礙 - Looking outside, there is no obstruction.
  • 列侍者飄然自涼 - Those in attendance feel cool and light.
  • 若驭風而行也 - As if riding the wind.
  • 權稱善 - The ruler praised it.
  • 夫人乃?片發，以神膠續之 - The wife then used a lock of hair and divine glue to join it.
  • 接弓弩之斷弦，百斷百續也 - Joining broken bowstrings, it can join a hundred times.
  • 乃織為?縠，累月而成 - Then she wove it into a fine fabric, taking many months to complete.
  • 裁為幔，內外視之，飄飄如煙氣輕動 - Cut it into a curtain, when viewed from inside and outside, it moves lightly like smoke.
  • 而房內自涼 - And the room inside becomes cool.
  • 時權常在軍旅，每以此幔自隨，以為征幕 - At that time, the ruler was often on military campaigns, always carrying this curtain with him, using it as a tent during campaigns.
  • 舒之則廣縱一丈，卷之則可納於枕中 - When unfolded, it is one zhang wide; when rolled up, it can fit in a pillow.
  • 時人謂之“絲絕” - People at the time called it 'silk thread.'
  • 故吳有“三絕”，四海無儔其妙 - Thus Wu had the 'Three Wonders,' no one in the world could match its excellence.
  • 後有貪寵求媚者，言夫人幻耀於人主 - Later, there were those who sought favor by flattering, saying the wife was using magic to dazzle the ruler.
  • 因而致退黜 - As a result, she was demoted.
  • 雖見疑墜，猶存錄其巧工 - Even though she was suspected and demoted, her skill was still recorded.
  • 吳亡，不知所在 - When Wu fell, her whereabouts are unknown.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 權 - Sun Quan, the ruler of Wu during the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 夫人 - The wife of Sun Quan, known for her exceptional weaving skills.
  • 神膠 - A mythical substance used for joining materials, originating from the country of Yuyi.
  • 郁夷國 - A mythical country mentioned in ancient Chinese texts.
  • 三絕 - The 'Three Wonders' of Wu, which include the weaving skill of the wife of Sun Quan.
  • 吳亡 - The fall of the state of Wu, marking the end of the Three Kingdoms period.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 權使夫人指其意思焉 - This phrase is somewhat elliptical, but it clearly indicates that Sun Quan asked his wife to express her intentions.
  • 妾欲窮慮盡思，能使下绡帷而清風自入 - This sentence combines the wife's desire to think thoroughly with the effect of her weaving, which allows cool air to enter naturally.
  • 乃織為?縠，累月而成 - This clause describes the process of weaving the fabric, emphasizing the time and effort involved.
  • 故吳有“三絕”，四海無儔其妙 - This sentence highlights the exceptional nature of the wife's skill, placing it among the 'Three Wonders' of Wu.
  • 後有貪寵求媚者，言夫人幻耀於人主 - This clause introduces a negative element, where flatterers accuse the wife of using magic, leading to her demotion.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 汉游洛妃之俦 - The legend of the Luo River Goddess, who was said to have accompanied the Han emperor on his travels.

  • 申、褒见列于前周 - Historical figures from the Zhou dynasty known for their virtuous conduct.
  • 班、赵载详于往汉 - Historical figures from the Han dynasty, possibly referring to Ban Gu and Zhao Feiyan.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '录曰：赵、潘二夫人，妍明伎艺，婉娈通神，抑亦汉游洛妃之俦，荆巫云雨之类' introduces two women, Zhao and Pan, and compares them to legendary figures from the Han and Chu dynasties.
  • The clause '而能避妖幸之嬖，睹进退之机' describes the women's ability to recognize and avoid danger and make wise decisions.
  • The philosophical statement '夫盈则有亏，道有崇替，居盛必衰，理固明矣' discusses the cyclical nature of life and the inevitability of decline after prosperity.
  • The metaphorical statement '语乎荣悴，譬诸草木，华落张弛，势之必然' compares the rise and fall of fortunes to the natural cycles of plants.
  • The clause '巧言萋斐，前王之所信惑' criticizes flattery and insincere praise, which even wise rulers can be deceived by.
  • The historical reference '是以申、褒见列于前周，班、赵载详于往汉' links the virtuous conduct of Shen and Bao in the Zhou dynasty to the notable figures Ban and Zhao in the Han dynasty.
  • The conclusion '异代同闻，可为叹也' emphasizes the universality of certain experiences across different generations.
  • The historical marker '黄龙元年，始都武昌' sets the context for the following narrative.
  • The detailed description '时越巂之南，献背明鸟，形如鹤，止不向明，巢常对北，多肉少毛，声音百变，闻钟磬笙竽之声，则奋翅摇头' describes the mythical bird and its behavior.
  • The initial perception '时人以为吉祥' indicates that the bird was initially seen as a good omen.
  • The relocation '是岁迁都建业，殊方多贡珍奇' describes the move of the capital to Jianye and the arrival of exotic gifts.
  • The mispronunciation '吴人语讹，呼背明为背亡鸟' explains the local dialect's mispronunciation of the bird's name.
  • The prediction '国中以为大妖，不及百年，当有丧乱背叛灭亡之事，散逸奔逃，墟无烟火' foretells future turmoil and destruction based on the bird's symbolism.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 國之將亡，其兆先見 - Classical expression meaning 'when a state is about to fall, its signs are seen beforehand.'
  • 明神見之，觀其德也 - Means 'the divine sees it, observing its virtue,' referring to the idea that the gods judge a state's fate based on its moral conduct.
  • 歸命面缚来降 - Literally 'surrendering with bound hands,' a gesture of complete submission, often used in historical contexts of defeat or capitulation.
  • 蛇、鹄者，虫禽之最灵 - Refers to snakes and cranes as the most intelligent creatures among animals, often used in Chinese culture to symbolize wisdom and longevity.
  • 嘉瑞 - Refers to auspicious signs or omens, often seen as indicators of good fortune or divine favor.
  • 吳王阖闾，崇饰厚葬 - Refers to King Helu of Wu, known for his elaborate and extravagant burials.
  • 生埋美人，多藏宝物 - Describes the practice of burying live concubines and hoarding treasures with the deceased, a common but controversial practice in ancient China.
  • 灵鹄翔于林壑，神虎啸于山丘 - Metaphorical expressions describing the spirits of a deceased person manifesting in nature, often seen as a sign of the person's continued influence or presence.
  • 湛卢之剑，飞入于楚 - Refers to the famous sword Zhanlu, which is said to have flown to the state of Chu, symbolizing the transfer of power or the loss of a great artifact.
  • 收魂聚怪，富丽以极 - Describes the accumulation of spirits and strange phenomena, often associated with the opulence and excess of a ruler's burial.
  • 诡异失中 - Means 'strange and out of balance,' suggesting that the practices described are excessive and inappropriate.
  • 速朽 - Means 'to decay quickly,' implying that such practices should be avoided to prevent the rapid deterioration of the deceased's legacy.
  • 宋桓、盛姬，前史讥其骄惑 - Refers to Duke Huan of Song and Lady Sheng, who were criticized in history for their arrogance and confusion.
  • 嬴博杨孙，君子贵其合礼 - Refers to the descendants of Ying Bo and Yang Sun, who were praised for adhering to proper rites and customs.
  • 远古，指详中代 - Means 'looking back to ancient times, pointing to detailed accounts of the middle ages,' suggesting a contrast between past and present practices.
  • 求诸事迹，俭泰相悬 - Means 'seeking through historical records, the difference between frugality and extravagance is vast,' highlighting the disparity between different eras.
  • 末世，渐相夸矫 - Refers to the late period of a dynasty, where practices become increasingly showy and artificial.
  • 生滋淫湎，死则同殉 - Describes the excessive indulgence in life and the practice of human sacrifice in death, both of which are condemned.
  • 委积珍宝，埃尘灭身 - Means 'accumulating precious treasures, only to be buried in dust and forgotten,' criticizing the futility of such practices.
  • 乖于同穴，可谓叹欤 - Means 'to be separated in death, how sad it is,' expressing the sorrow of not being buried together.
  • 吕蒙入吴，吴主劝其学业 - Refers to the historical event where Lu Meng, a general of the Wu Kingdom, was encouraged by the ruler to pursue education.
  • 蒙乃博览群籍，以《易》为宗 - Means 'Lu Meng then extensively read many books, taking the *I Ching* (Book of Changes) as his main focus.'
  • 常在孙策座上酣醉，忽卧，于梦中诵《周易》一部 - Describes a scene where Lu Meng, while drunk, falls asleep and dreams of reciting the entire *I Ching*.
  • 俄而惊起 - Means 'suddenly wakes up,' indicating a sudden awakening from the dream.
  • 吕蒙呓语通《周易》 - Means 'Lu Meng's mutterings were understood as the *I Ching*,' suggesting that his dream recitation was so profound that it was perceived as a deep understanding of the *I Ching*.

  • 若鄭君之感先聖，周盤之夢東里 - Refers to historical anecdotes where individuals were inspired by sages or had prophetic dreams.
  • 跡同事異，光被遐策 - Means 'the actions are the same but the circumstances differ, the glory extends far and wide,' suggesting that similar actions can have different outcomes.
  • 索隱钩深，妙于玄旨 - Means 'to explore the obscure and delve into the profound, to grasp the subtle meanings,' indicating a deep and nuanced understanding.
  • 孔门群说，未若吕生之学焉 - Means 'the various teachings of Confucius' school are not as profound as the learning of Master Lu,' suggesting that Master Lu's teachings surpass those of Confucius.
  • 和于月下舞水精如意 - Means 'Sun He dances with a jade如意 (a ceremonial object) under the moonlight,' suggesting a graceful and elegant action.
  • 误伤夫人颊，血流污裤，娇奼弥苦 - Means 'he accidentally injures her cheek, causing blood to flow and stain her clothes, making her more distressed,' describing a tragic and emotional scene.
  • 自舐其疮，命太医合药 - Means 'she licks her wound herself and orders the imperial physician to prepare medicine,' indicating her resilience and the high regard for medical treatment.
  • 得白獭髓，杂玉与琥珀屑，当灭此痕 - Means 'if you can get the marrow of a white otter, mixed with jade and amber powder, it will remove the mark,' suggesting a magical or rare remedy.
  • 即购致百金，能得白獭髓者，厚赏之 - Means 'he immediately offers a hundred gold pieces as a reward for anyone who can obtain the otter's marrow,' indicating a significant reward.
  • 有富春渔人云 - Means 'a fisherman from Fuchun says,' introducing a witness or informant.
  • 此物知人欲取，则逃入石穴 - Means 'this creature knows when someone wants to take it and hides in a stone cave,' suggesting the creature's intelligence.
  • 伺其祭鱼之时，獭有斗死者，穴中应有枯骨，虽无髓，其骨可合玉舂为粉，喷于疮上，其痕则灭 - Means 'wait until the time when the otter is sacrificed, and if one dies in a fight, there should be dry bones in the cave. Even without marrow, the bones can be ground into powder with jade and sprinkled on the wound, which will remove the mark,' suggesting a detailed and practical method.
  • 和乃命合此膏，琥珀太多，及差而有赤点如朱，逼而视之，更益其妍 - Means 'Sun He then ordered the preparation of this ointment, but there was too much amber, and when it was applied, it left red spots like cinnabar. When viewed closely, it became even more beautiful,' suggesting an unexpected but positive outcome.
  • 诸嬖人欲要宠，皆以丹脂点颊而后进幸 - Means 'various concubines wanted to gain favor and all used cinnabar to paint their cheeks before gaining his attention,' indicating a trend or custom.
  • 妖惑相动，遂成淫俗 - Means 'they influenced each other in a seductive way, leading to a decadent custom,' suggesting a moral decline.

  • 笁貨財如山，不可算計 - Means 'his wealth is like a mountain, impossible to count,' emphasizing the vastness of his fortune.
  • 內以方諸盆瓶，設大珠如卵，散滿於庭，謂之“寶庭” - Means 'inside, he uses square vessels and bottles to display large pearls as big as eggs, scattered throughout the courtyard, calling it the 'Treasure Courtyard,' describing a luxurious display of wealth.

  • 糜竺家當有火厄，萬不遺一 - Means 'there will be a fire disaster at Mi Zhu's house, not leaving a single thing intact,' predicting a catastrophic event.
  • 賴君能恤斂枯骨，天道不辜君德，故來禳卻此火 - Means 'thanks to your compassion and care for the dead, the heavenly way does not wrong your virtue, so we come to ward off this fire,' explaining the reason for the appearance of the supernatural beings.
  • 當使財物不盡 - Means 'to ensure your wealth is not entirely lost,' indicating the purpose of the intervention.
  • 自今以後，亦宜防衛 - Means 'from now on, you should also be vigilant,' advising Mi Zhu to take precautions.
  • 旬日，火從庫內起，燒其珠玉十分之一 - Means 'ten days later, a fire broke out in the warehouse, burning a tenth of the pearls and jade,' describing the actual disaster.
  • 皆是陽燧旱燥自能燒物 - Means 'all of it was caused by the sun's reflection and dryness, which naturally burns things,' explaining the cause of the fire.
  • 火盛之時，見數十青衣童子來撲火 - Means 'when the fire was at its peak, dozens of young boys dressed in green came to put it out,' describing the supernatural intervention.
  • 有青氣如雲，覆於火上，即滅 - Means 'a green mist like a cloud covered the fire, and it immediately went out,' describing the effect of the intervention.
  • 家人乃收鵁鶄數千頭養於池渠中，以厭火 - Means 'the family then collected thousands of cranes and kept them in ponds and channels to ward off fires,' describing the family's response to the advice.
  • 時三國交鋒，軍用萬倍，乃輸其寶物車服，以助先主 - Means 'at the time when the three kingdoms were in conflict, military expenses were ten times higher, so he donated his treasures and carriages to assist Lord Liu Bei,' describing his contribution to the war effort.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 不得中行，狂狷可也 - Classical saying meaning 'If one cannot be a person of the middle way, being a wild or strict person is acceptable.' Refers to Confucian ideals of moderation.
  • 淳于、优孟之俦 - Refers to historical figures like Chunyu and You Meng, who were known for using humor and performance to convey serious messages.
  • 因俳说以进谏 - Means 'to use jest and play to offer advice,' referring to a method of indirect persuasion.
  • 片言窃讽 - Means 'to subtly criticize with a few words,' indicating indirect criticism.
  • 媚足规范 - Means 'to be charming enough to set a standard,' suggesting that even playful speech can have a normative effect.
  • 因物而刺 - Means 'to criticize through objects,' referring to indirect criticism via metaphors or allusions.
  • 言之者无罪 - Classical saying meaning 'The speaker is blameless,' emphasizing the freedom of speech.
  • 五味令人口爽 - Classical saying from the *Dao De Jing* meaning 'The five flavors dull the taste buds,' warning against excessive indulgence.
  • 甘并桂石，美斯松草 - Refers to the sweetness of pine and the beauty of grass, likely used metaphorically to contrast worldly pleasures with spiritual or intellectual pursuits.
  • 含吐烟霞，咀食沆瀣 - Means 'to breathe in mist and clouds, to chew on the dew of heaven,' suggesting a life of refinement and detachment.
  • 迅千灵于一朝，方尘劫于俄顷 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to swiftly transcend a thousand spirits in a moment, to compare dust to an instant,' suggesting the fleeting nature of worldly pleasures.
  • 乎可淫此酣乐，忘彼久视者乎 - Means 'How can one indulge in such pleasures and forget the long view?' suggesting a warning against short-sighted indulgence.
  • 物有事异而名同者 - Means 'There are things that differ in their nature but share the same name,' referring to the complexity of language and meaning.
  • 穷神达理 - Means 'to fathom the divine and understand the principles,' suggesting deep insight.
  • 假于诐辞，专求于邪说 - Means 'to rely on biased words and seek only heretical ideas,' warning against misguided thinking.
  • 天命有兆，历运攸归 - Means 'The mandate of heaven has signs, and the course of history is destined,' referring to the inevitability of fate.
  • 妄信于谣讹，指怪于纤草 - Means 'to blindly believe rumors and point to strange phenomena in small plants,' warning against superstition.
  • 将溺所闻，信诸厥术 - Means 'To be submerged by what one hears and believe in its methods,' suggesting a warning against credulity.
  • 可为嗟乎 - Means 'This is indeed a matter of regret,' expressing sorrow or lament.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 帝王之兴 - Refers to the rise of a ruler or dynasty.
  • 叶休祥之应 - Means 'the response of auspicious signs,' indicating divine approval or prophecy.
  • 天无隐祥，地无蓄宝 - A poetic expression meaning 'heaven does not hide auspicious signs, and earth does not hoard treasures,' emphasizing the idea that divine favor and natural resources are openly available.
  • 因神物以表运，见星云以观德 - A formal statement meaning 'by using divine objects to indicate the course of destiny, and observing stars and clouds to discern virtue,' highlighting the connection between celestial phenomena and moral qualities.
  • 按《周官》有冯相氏，以观祥录之数 - Refers to the *Zhouguan* (Rites of Zhou) and the office of Fengxiangshi, who observed auspicious signs and recorded their meanings.
  • 晋以金德，故白云起于灞水 - A historical reference to the Jin dynasty, which was associated with the metal element, and the appearance of white clouds over the Ba River as a sign of its legitimacy.
  • 《山海经》及《异物志》 - Refers to two classical texts: the *Shanhaijing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas) and the *Yiwuzhi* (Record of Strange Things).
  • 燃洲之兽，生于火中，以毛织为布，虽有垢腻，投火则洁净也 - Describes a mythical creature from the *Shanhaijing* and *Yiwuzhi* that lives in fire and whose fur can be woven into fabric that cleanses itself when burned.
  • 两说不同，故偕录焉 - A concluding remark meaning 'since the two accounts differ, both are recorded,' indicating the author's approach to handling conflicting information.
  • 因墀国献五足兽，状如狮子 - Refers to a five-legged beast from the country of Yincheng, described as lion-like.
  • 玉钱千缗，其形如环，环重十两，上有“天寿永吉”之字 - Describes a thousand strings of jade coins, each string containing ten coins, with the characters 'Tian Shou Yong Ji' (Long Life and Eternal Good Fortune) inscribed on them.
  • 问其使者五足兽是何变化 - Asks the envoy about the transformation of the five-legged beast.
  • 东方有解形之民，使头飞于南海，左手飞于东山，右手飞于西泽，自脐以下，两足孤立 - Describes a mythical people from the east who can detach parts of their bodies.
  • 至暮，头还肩上，两手遇疾风飘于海外，落玄洲之上，化为五足兽，则一指为一足也 - Explains the transformation process where the head returns to the shoulder, and the hands are carried away by the wind to become the legs of the five-legged beast.
  • 其人既失两手，使傍人割里肉以为两臂，宛然如旧也 - Describes how the people regrow their arms by cutting flesh from others.
  • 因墀国在西域之北，送使者以铁为车轮，十年方至晋 - Indicates the location of Yincheng and the long journey of the envoy.
  • 及还，轮皆绝锐，莫知其远近也 - Notes the wear and tear on the wheels and the difficulty in determining the distance traveled.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 傍有丹石井 - Beside is a red stone well, describing a well made of red stone.
  • 非人之所凿 - Not made by humans, indicating that the well is natural or supernatural.
  • 下及漏泉，水常沸涌 - Describes a spring or underground water source that constantly bubbles and surges.
  • 诸仙欲饮之时，以长绠引汲也 - When the immortals wish to drink, they use a long rope to draw the water, indicating a method used by immortals to access the water.
  • 其国人皆多力，不食五谷 - The people of that country are all strong, and they do not eat the five grains, suggesting a unique diet or lifestyle.
  • 日中无影 - There is no shadow at noon, a phenomenon that suggests unusual conditions or magical properties.
  • 饮桂浆云雾 - Drink mist and clouds flavored with osmanthus, indicating a mystical and fragrant beverage.
  • 羽毛为衣 - Clothing made of feathers, describing the attire of the people.
  • 伸之几至一丈，置之自缩如蠡 - When stretched, it can reach nearly a zhang (about 3.3 meters), and when placed, it retracts itself like a snail, describing the elasticity and retractability of their hair.
  • 续人发以为绳，汲丹井之水 - Use human hair to make ropes and draw water from the red well, indicating a method used to access the water.
  • 久久方得升之水 - It takes a long time to draw a single measure of water, emphasizing the difficulty of obtaining water.
  • 水中有白蛙，两翅，常来去井上 - Describes a white frog with wings that frequently flies around the well.
  • 仙者食之 - Immortals eat it, indicating the significance of the frog.
  • 至周，王子晋临井而窥 - During the Zhou dynasty, Prince Jin of Zhou approached the well and looked inside, introducing a historical figure.
  • 有青雀衔玉杓以授子晋 - A green bird carried a jade ladle and gave it to Prince Jin, describing a supernatural event.
  • 子晋取而食之，乃有云起雪飞 - Prince Jin took it and ate it, and then clouds and snow rose up, indicating a magical reaction.
  • 子晋以衣袖挥云，则云雪自止 - Prince Jin waved his sleeve, and the clouds and snow stopped, describing a magical action.
  • 白蛙化为双白鸠入云，望之遂灭 - The white frog transformed into two white doves and flew into the clouds, disappearing from sight, indicating a transformation.
  • 皆频斯国之所记，盖其人年不可测也 - All these are recorded by the people of the country, and their age is immeasurable, suggesting the longevity and mystery of the people.
  • 使图其国山川地势瑰异之属，以示张华 - He asked to draw a map of the country's mountains, rivers, and strange landscapes to show Zhang Hua, indicating a request to document the place.
  • 华云：“此神异之国，难可验信。” - Zhang Hua said, 'This is a divine and extraordinary country, difficult to verify,' expressing skepticism.
  • 以车马珍服送之出关 - He sent him out of the pass with carriages, horses, and fine clothing, indicating a respectful farewell.

  • 捃采 - Means 'to gather' or 'to collect,' often used in the context of compiling knowledge or materials.
  • 书契之始 - Refers to the beginning of written records or the origins of writing.
  • 考验 - Means 'to test' or 'to verify,' often used in the context of examining or validating knowledge.
  • 闾里所说 - Refers to stories or tales circulating among the common people in the streets and neighborhoods.
  • 造《博物志》 - Means 'to compile the *Bamboo Annals of Natural History*,' a reference to the author's work.
  • 博识无伦 - Means 'extremely knowledgeable' or 'unparalleled in learning.'

  • 惑乱于后生 - Means 'to confuse or mislead future generations.'
  • 繁芜于耳目 - Means 'to overwhelm the senses with excessive or irrelevant information.'
  • 芟截浮疑 - Means 'to cut out vague or uncertain parts,' referring to the process of refining a work.
  • 置于函中 - Means 'to place in a box or case,' often used to describe the storage of important documents.
